chapters.name: Tracing Changes Through a Thousand Years

tests.name: Tracing Changes Through a Thousand YearsTest 01
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 07 Social Science Tracing Changes Through a Thousand Years CBSE Online Test 01 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: ________ was a fourteenth-century chronicler.
questions.explanation: The fourteenth-century chronicler Ziyauddin Barani wrote his chronicle first in 1356 and another version two years later.

questions.question: Which of the languages was used in Andhra Pradesh according to Amir Khusrau?
questions.explanation: In 1318 the poet Amir Khusrau noted that there was a different language in every region of the land. According to him, the language used in Andhra Pradesh was Telangani. This refers to the Telugu language, which is the official language of the state of Andhra Pradesh.

questions.question: A person who makes maps is called ________.
questions.explanation: Cartography is the study and practice of making maps. In this context Cartographer is a person who makes maps.

questions.question: Name the groups who asserted their political importance during the period between 700 to 1750?

options.html: Marathas, Sikhs, Jats, Muslims and Punjabi
Marathas, Sikhs, Jats, Kashmiri, and Kshatriya
Marathas, Sikhs, Jats, Ahoms, and Kayastha
Marathas, Sikhs, Jats, Muslims, and Shudras

questions.explanation: Groups of people such as the Marathas, Sikhs, Jats, Ahoms, and Kayasthas (a caste of scribes and secretaries) used the opportunities of the age to become politically important.

questions.question: The Shia Muslims who believed that the Prophet's son-in-law ________ was the legitimate leader of Muslim community.
questions.explanation: There were the Shia Muslims who believed that the Prophet’s son-in-law, Ali, was the legitimate leader of the Muslim community.

questions.question: Sunni Muslims accepted the authority of the early leaders ________.
questions.explanation: The Sunni Muslims accepted the authority of the early leaders (khalifas) of the community, and the succeeding Khalifas.

questions.question: Who brought the teachings of the Holy Quran to India?
questions.explanation: In the seventh century, Merchants and migrants first brought the teachings of the Holy Quran to India.

questions.question: Which of the following is the holy book of Muslims?
questions.explanation: Muslims regard the Quran as their holy book and accept the sovereignty of the one God, Allah.

questions.question: The periods in which British historians divided Indian history.
questions.explanation: In the middle of the nineteenth century British historians divided the history of India into three periods: “Hindu”, “Muslim” and “British”.

questions.question: ________, a name derived from "Rajaputra" - the son of a Ruler, a body of warriors who claimed Kshatriya caste status.
questions.explanation: One group of people who became important in the medieval period were the Rajputs, a name derived from “Rajaputra”, the son of a ruler. Between the eighth and fourteenth centuries, the term was applied more generally to a body of warriors who claimed Kshatriya caste status.

questions.question: An influential, wealthy individual who supports another person - an artist, a craftsperson, a learned man, or a noble is called _______.
questions.explanation: A person who gives financial or other support to a person, organization, or cause. Hence, with respect to the medieval period, Patron is an influential, wealthy individual who supports another person an artist, a craftsperson, a learned man, or a noble.

questions.question: The Mughal Empire declined in the ________ Century.
questions.explanation: The decline of the Mughal Empire in the eighteenth century led to the re-emergence of regional states.

questions.question: Which of the following is not the literary source?
questions.explanation: Historians rely upon coins, inscriptions, architecture, and textual records for information.

questions.question: A place where documents and manuscripts are stored as records for knowledge is called an ________.
questions.explanation: An archive is an accumulation of historical records, documents providing information about a place, institution, or group of people. It is a place where documents and manuscripts are stored as records for knowledge.

questions.question: Who made Maps in 1154 CE?
questions.explanation: A section of the world map was drawn by the Arab geographer Al-Idrisi in the twelfth century (1154 CE) showing the Indian subcontinent.

tests.name: Tracing Changes Through a Thousand YearsTest 02
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 07 Social Science Tracing Changes Through a Thousand Years CBSE Online Test 02 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: There was no printing press earlier so the writers copied manuscripts by ________.
questions.explanation: There was no printing press in earlier days so scribes copied manuscripts by hand.

questions.question: Who said - Common people do not know Sanskrit, only Brahmans do?
questions.explanation: Amir Khusrau went on to explain that in contrast to the languages there was Sanskrit which did not belong to any region. It was an old language and “common people do not know it, only the Brahmanas do”.

questions.question: Who coined the term Hindustan in the thirteenth century?
questions.explanation: When the term was used in the thirteenth century by Minhaj-i Siraj, a chronicler who wrote in Persian, he meant the areas of Punjab, Haryana and the lands between the Ganga and Yamuna.

questions.question: Minhaj-i-Siraj is a chronicler who wrote in ________.
questions.explanation: The term "Hindustan" was used in the thirteenth century by Minhaj-i Siraj. He was a chronicler who wrote in Persian.

questions.question: What led to the migration of forest dwellers in the 8th century?

options.html: Gradual clearing of forests, Extension of agriculture

questions.explanation: There was a gradual clearing of forests and the extension of agriculture, a change faster and more complete in some areas than in others. Changes in their habitat forced many forest-dwellers to migrate.

questions.question: The term Hindustan used in the 13th century by Minhaj-i-Siraj comprised of which areas?

options.html: Areas of Punjab, Haryana and the lands between the Ganga and Yamuna
Areas of Madhya Pradesh, Haryana and the lands between the Ganga and Yamuna
Areas of Uttar Pradesh, Haryana and the lands between the Ganga and Yamuna
Areas of Punjab, Himachal Pradesh and the lands between the Ganga and Yamuna

questions.explanation: When the term "Hindustan" was used in the thirteenth century by Minhaj-i Siraj, he meant the areas of Punjab, Haryana, and the lands between the Ganga and Yamuna.

questions.question: Today in simple terms for whom is the term foreigner used?

options.html: Who lives in rural areas of India

questions.explanation: A simple term like “foreigner” is used today to mean someone who is not an Indian.

questions.question: The one who copied manuscripts by hand known as ________.
questions.explanation: There was no printing press in earlies days so scribes were appointed. Scribes copied manuscripts by hand.

questions.question: ________ were collected by wealthy people, rulers, monasteries, and temples.
questions.explanation: Manuscripts were placed in libraries and archives and were collected by wealthy people, rulers, monasteries, and temples.

questions.question: The fourteenth-century chronicler Ziyauddin Barani wrote his chronicle first in ________.
questions.explanation: The fourteenth-century chronicler Ziyauddin Barani wrote his chronicle first in 1356 another version two years later.

questions.question: Refers to the environment of a region and the social and economic lifestyle of its residents.
questions.explanation: The area or natural environment in which an organism or population normally lives. It refers to the environment of a region and the social and economic lifestyle of its residents.

questions.question: New foods and beverages arrived in the subcontinent were:

options.html: potatoes, corn, chillies, tea, and coffee
potatoes, maize, chillies, maize, and wheat
potatoes, pulses, chillies, cabbage, and coffee
potatoes, corn, chillies, pulses and coffee

questions.explanation: At different moments in the period between 700 to 1750 new foods and beverages made their appearance. For eg. : Arrival of New foods and beverages in the subcontinent: potatoes, corn, chillies, tea, and coffee.

questions.question: Kanauj name is spelled in the Arabic language as:
questions.explanation: In the Arabic language, many words were spelled differently. For Eg: Kanauj's name is a spell as Qanauj.

questions.question: ________ framed their own rules and regulations to manage the conduct of their members.
questions.explanation: As society became more differentiated, people were grouped into jatis or sub-castes and ranked on the basis of their backgrounds and their occupations. Jatis framed their own rules and regulations to manage the conduct of their members.

questions.question: People of different regions - Gauda, Andhra, Kerala, Karnataka, Maharashtra, and Gujarat - apparently fled before Ghiyasuddin Balban's ________.
questions.explanation: People of different regions - Gauda, Andhra, Kerala, Karnataka, Maharashtra, and Gujarat - apparently fled before his armies. Historians regard these as - exaggerated claims of conquests.

tests.name: Tracing Changes Through a Thousand YearsTest 03
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 07 Social Science Tracing Changes Through a Thousand Years CBSE Online Test 03 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: One of the major developments was the emergence of the idea of ________ of a loving, personal deity that devotees could reach without the aid of priests or elaborate rituals.
questions.explanation: The thousand years of history that we are exploring witnessed major developments in religious traditions. One of the major developments was the emergence of the idea of bhakti - of a loving, personal deity that devotees could reach without the aid of priests or elaborate rituals.

questions.question: Two styles of writing in Persian and Arabic are:
questions.explanation: Different kinds of handwriting like the Nastaliq style and the Shikaste style could make the reading of Persian and Arabic difficult.

questions.question: By ________ many regions already possessed distinct geographical dimensions and their own language and cultural characteristics.
questions.explanation: Many regions already possessed distinct geographical dimensions and their own language and cultural characteristics by 700.

questions.question: Two schools of Islam are:
questions.explanation: There were other important differences between the various schools of law (Hanafi and Shafi’i mainly in India), and in theology and mystic traditions.

questions.question: The duration of Khalji dynasty is:
questions.explanation: The Khalji or Khilji dynasty was a Muslim dynasty which ruled large parts of the Indian subcontinent between 1290 and 1320.

questions.question: Who was the famous invader of Mughals?
questions.explanation: Genghis Khan or Chinggis Khaan was the founder and first Great Khan of the Mongol Empire and was the famous invader of Mughals.

questions.question: ________ means the empire crossing its region and spreading over several other regions.
questions.explanation: Pan region empire is a nation which influences other nation's actions completely or partially, generally, a powerful country which has a sphere of influence, might be political or economical or cultural or ideological. Hence, it means the empire crossing its region and spreading over several other regions.

questions.question: Muslims regard the ________ as their holy book and accept the sovereignty of the one God, Allah.
questions.explanation: Muslims regard the Quran as their holy book and accept the sovereignty of the one God, Allah, whose love, mercy, and beauty embrace all those who believe in Him, without regard to social background.

questions.question: Harshavardhana was from:
questions.explanation: Harshavardhana was an Indian emperor who ruled North India from 606 to 647 CE. He was a member of the Gupta dynasty.

questions.question: Siraj used the term Hindustan in:
questions.explanation: Minhaj-i-Siraj used the term "Hindustan" for the first time. He used the term in a political sense for lands that were a part of the dominions of the Delhi Sultan.

questions.question: In the middle of the ________ century, British historians divided the history of India into three periods: Hindu, Muslim, and British.
questions.explanation: In the middle of the nineteenth century, British historians divided the history of India on the idea that the religion of rulers was the only important historical change, and that there were no other significant developments in the economy, accordingly they divided Indian history into three periods: “Hindu”, “Muslim” and “British”.

questions.question: The worship of new deities, the construction of temples by royalty, and the growing importance of Brahmanas, the priests, as dominant groups in society were the important changes that occur that we call today.
questions.explanation: The thousand years of history that we are exploring witnessed major developments in religious traditions. It was during this period that important changes occurred in what we call Hinduism today. These included the worship of new deities, the construction of temples by royalty, and the growing importance of Brahmanas, the priests, as dominant groups in society.

questions.question: Learned theologians and jurists were:
questions.explanation: Many rulers were patrons of Islam and the ulama who were learned, theologians and jurists.

questions.question: Prashasti means:
questions.explanation: Prashasti is a Sanskrit word meaning in praise of King.

questions.question: The dynasties like ________ extended their empires pan-regional.
questions.explanation: Dynasties like the Cholas, Khaljis, Tughluqs, and Mughals were able to build an empire that was pan-regional - spanning diverse regions.

tests.name: Tracing Changes Through a Thousand YearsTest 04
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 07 Social Science Tracing Changes Through a Thousand Years CBSE Online Test 04 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Who was al-aldrisi?
questions.explanation: Muhammad-Al-Idrisi was a Muslim and Arab cartographer, geographer, traveler.

questions.question: ________ carries with it a sense of material progress and intellectual advancement.
questions.explanation: The “medieval” period is often contrasted with the “modern” period. “Modernity” carries with it a sense of material progress and intellectual advancement.

questions.question: What is the Nastaliq style of writing?
questions.explanation: The Nastaliq style is cursive and easy to read.

questions.question: ________ had knowledge of Sanskrit texts, which made them respectable in the society.
questions.explanation: Brahmanas earned a lot of respect in society because of their knowledge of Sanskrit texts.

questions.question: Who was the ruler of a vast empire that stretched from Bengal (Gauda) in the east to Ghazni (Gajjana) in Afghanistan in the west and included all of south India (Dravida)?
questions.explanation: A Sanskrit prashasti, praising the Delhi Sultan Ghiyasuddin Balban (1266-87) explained that he was the ruler of a vast empire that stretched from Bengal (Gauda) in the east to Ghazni (Gajjana) in Afghanistan in the west and included all of south India (Dravida).

questions.question: Delhi Sultan Ghiyasuddin Balban birth and death year is:
questions.explanation: A Sanskrit prashasti praising the Delhi Sultan Ghiyasuddin Balban birth and death year is 1266-87.

questions.question: Which Mughal ruler used Hindustan to describe the geography, the fauna, and the culture of the inhabitants of the subcontinent?
questions.explanation: In the early sixteenth century Babur used Hindustan to describe the geography, the fauna and the culture of the inhabitants of the subcontinent.

questions.question: A chivalric code of conduct - extreme valour and a great sense of loyalty - were the qualities attributed to Rajputs by their ________.
questions.explanation: One group of people who became important in this period were the Rajputs. The qualities attributed to Rajputs by their poets and bards: A chivalric code of conduct - extreme valour and a great sense of loyalty.

questions.question: ________ had the support of their patrons, who were new rulers and searching for prestige.
questions.explanation: Brahmanas a lot of respect in society because of knowledge of Sanskrit texts earned. Their dominant position was consolidated by the support of their patrons - new patrons rulers searching for prestige.

questions.question: Peoples belief in the divine was sometimes ________ and at other times collective.
questions.explanation: The thousand years of history that we are exploring witnessed major developments in religious traditions. People’s belief in the divine was sometimes deeply personal, but more usually it was collective.

questions.question: Which of the following is false?

options.html: Quran is the holy book of Muslims
Mughal empire flourished in the 18th century
Muslims accepted the sovereignty of one Allah

questions.explanation: The Mughal Empire declined in the eighteenth century.

questions.question: Why scribes copied manuscripts by hand?

options.html: King banned the use of printing press
It was costly to print the documents
It was in fashion to write with hands

questions.explanation: There was no printing press in those days so scribes copied manuscripts by hand.

questions.question:
Match the following:

(a) Gauda (i) Bengal
(b) Gajjana (ii) Afghanistan
(c) Kanauj (iii) Uttar Pradesh
questions.explanation:
1. Gauda in Bangal
  2. Gajjana in Afghanistan
  3. Kanauj in Uttar Pradesh

questions.question: Identify the following map what is shown
questions.explanation: The given picture is of the Provinces of the Delhi Sultanate during Muhammad Tughluq’s reign according to the Egyptian source Masalik al-Absar fi Mamalik al-Amsar of Shihabuddin Umari.

questions.question: During the period 700 to 1750 new technologies made their appearance:
questions.explanation: At different moments in the period between - 700 to 1750 new technologies made their appearances - like the Persian wheel in irrigation, the spinning wheel in weaving, and firearms in combat.

questions.question: Worship of new deities and construction of ________ by Kings began.
questions.explanation: Important changes occurred in what we call Hinduism today. These included the worship of new deities, the construction of temples by royalty, and the growing importance of Brahmanas, the priests, as dominant groups in society.

questions.question:
Match the following:

(a) Shia (i) Learned theologians and jurists
(b) Sunni (ii) Believe in Prophets son- in- law
(c) Ulama (iii) Believe in Khalifas
questions.explanation:
1. There were the Shia Muslims who believed that the Prophet’s son-in-law, Ali, was the legitimate leader of the Muslim community.
  2. The Sunni Muslims who accepted the authority of the early leaders (khalifas) of the community, and the succeeding Khalifas.
  3. Many rulers were the ulama –learned theologians and jurists.

questions.question: People started using ________ to write holy texts, chronicles of rulers, letters, and teachings of saints, petitions and judicial records and for registers of accounts and taxes.
questions.explanation: Paper gradually became cheaper and more widely available. People used it to write holy texts, chronicles of rulers, letters, and teachings of saints, petitions and judicial records, and for registers of accounts and taxes.

questions.question: Identify the figure shown below
questions.explanation: The given figure is of the Persian wheel used in irrigation.

questions.question:
Read the lines given below :
“a different language in every region of this land: Sindhi, Lahori, Kashmiri, Dvarsamudri (in southern Karnataka), Telangani (in Andhra Pradesh), Gujari (in Gujarat), Ma‘bari(in Tamil Nadu), Gauri, (in Bengal) … Awadhi (in eastern Uttar Pradesh) and Hindawi (in the area around Delhi)”.
Who said these lines?
questions.explanation: In 1318 the poet Amir Khusrau noted that there were different languages in different regions and said the above-mentioned line.

questions.question:
Match the following language in which it was used according to Amir Khusrau:

Language State
(a) Sindhi (i) Karnataka
(b) Telangani (ii) Andhra Pradesh
(c) Awadhi (iii) U.P
questions.explanation:
In 1318 the poet Amir Khusrau noted that there was a different language in every region of this land:

  1. Sindhi - (in southern Karnataka).
  2. Telangani (in Andhra Pradesh).
  3.  Awadhi (in eastern Uttar Pradesh)

questions.question: Which of the incorrect?

options.html: Historical records exist in a variety of languages which changed considerably
In the map by al-idrisi place, names are marked in English

questions.explanation: In al-Idrisi’s map place-names are marked in Arabic.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Term Meaning
(a) Patron (i) Place where documents are stored
(b) Chronicles (ii) Accounts of historical events
(c) Archives (iii) An influential and wealthy individual who support another person
questions.explanation:
1. Patron: Person who gives financial or other support to a person. Hence patron is an influential and healthy individual who support another person
  2. Chronicles: A factual written account of important or historical events in the order of their occurrence.
  3. Archives: Place where a collection of historical documents or records are kept or stored.

questions.question: Which of the following is false regarding Jati?

options.html: The status of jati was the same from area to area.
Jatis framed their own rules and regulations to manage the conduct of their members.
Regulations were enforced by an assembly of elders, described in some areas as the jati panchayat.
As society became more differentiated, people were grouped into jatis.

questions.explanation: The status of the same jati could vary from area to area.

questions.question: Map shown below was made in
questions.explanation: The given map was made in the 1720s by a French cartographer.

questions.question: The below mention is the map made by
questions.explanation: The given map is the subcontinent, from the early-eighteenth century Atlas Nouveau of Guillaume de l’Isle made in the 1720s by a French cartographer.

questions.question: Identify the figure shown below:

options.html: This is a painting of a scribe making a copy of a novel.
This is a painting of a scribe making a copy of clothes printing.
This is a painting of a painter making a painting of book
This is a painting of a scribe making a copy of a manuscript.

questions.explanation: The given figure is A painting of a scribe making a copy of a manuscript. This painting is only 10.5 cm by 7.1 cm in size. Because of its size, it is called a miniature.

questions.question: Why the below painting is called a miniature painting?

options.html: Because it is 10.5 cm by 7.1 cm in size
Because it is 10.5 m by 7.1 m in size
Because it is 10.5 m by 7 m in size
Because it is 10 cm by 70 cm in size

questions.explanation: The given image is a painting of a scribe making a copy of a manuscript. This painting is only 10.5 cm by 7.1 cm in size. Because of its size it is called a miniature.

questions.question: Identify the below mention figure in which language it is written
questions.explanation: There were different kinds of handwriting li ke Persian and Arabic. The given figure is of the Persian Nastaliq style.

questions.question: Which style of writing is used in the below figure
questions.explanation: The given style of handwriting is the nastaliq style which was written cursive and easy to read.

questions.question: Hiranyagarbha rituals were performed with the help of
questions.explanation: When Hiranya-garbha (literally, the golden womb) ritual was performed with the help of Brahmanas, it was thought to lead to the “rebirth” of the sacrificer as a Kshatriya, even if he was not one by birth.

questions.question: Kadamba Mayurasharman established their kingdom in
questions.explanation: The Kadamba Mayurasharman was a Brahmana who gave up his traditional profession and took to arms and successfully established his kingdom in Karnataka.

questions.question: Tribhuvana-chakravartin means
questions.explanation: Many of the new kings adopted high-sounding titles like Tribhuvana-chakravartin which means lord of the three worlds.

questions.question: Vetti is a ________.
questions.explanation: The inscriptions of the Cholas who ruled in Tamil Nadu refer to more than 400 terms for different kinds of taxes. The most frequently mentioned tax is vetti, taken not in cash but in the form of forced labour.

questions.question: Kadamai means ________.
questions.explanation: The inscriptions of the Cholas who ruled in Tamil Nadu refer to more than 400 terms for different kinds of taxes. The most frequently mentioned tax is kadamai

questions.question: The functionaries for collecting revenue were generally recruited from:
questions.explanation: The functionaries for collecting revenue were generally recruited from influential families, and positions were often hereditary.

questions.question: Gwalior is in:
questions.explanation: Gwalior is a city in the central Indian state of Madhya Pradesh.

questions.question: Kings often rewarded Brahmanas by grants of ________.
questions.explanation: Kings often rewarded Brahmanas by grants of land. These were recorded on copper plates, which were given to those who received the land.

questions.question: The specialists who worked at the temple and lived near it were:
questions.explanation: All the specialists who worked at the temple and very often lived near it - priests, garland makers, cooks, sweepers, musicians, dancers, etc.

questions.question: Rajatarangi is a metrical historical chronicle of the north-western Indian subcontinent, particularly the kings of ________.
questions.explanation: Rajatarangini is a metrical legendary and historical chronicle of the north-western Indian subcontinent, particularly the kings of Kashmir. It was written in Sanskrit by Kashmiri historian Kalhana in the 12th century CE.

questions.question: Following sources used by the Kalhana except:
questions.explanation: Kalhana used a variety of sources, including inscriptions, documents, eyewitness accounts, and earlier histories, to write his account.

questions.question: The city of Kanauj is in the
questions.explanation: One of the particularly prized areas was the city of Kanauj in the Ganga valley.

questions.question: Which of the following rulers involved in the tripartite struggles?
questions.explanation: For centuries, rulers belonging to the Gurjara-Pratihara, Rashtrakuta, and Pala dynasties fought for control over Kanauj. Because there were three “parties” in this long-drawn conflict, historians often describe it as the “tripartite struggle”.

questions.question: Mahmud of Ghazni was the ruler of
questions.explanation: In the history, one of the best-known rulers was Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni, Afghanistan.

questions.question: Somnath temple is in:
questions.explanation: The Somnath temple located in Prabhas Patan near Veraval in Saurashtra on the western coast of Gujarat.

questions.question: Sultan is an:
questions.explanation: Sultan is a Sultan Arabic term meaning ruler.

questions.question: Kitab-al Hind written by:
questions.explanation: Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni entrusted a scholar named Al-Biruni to write an account of the subcontinent. This Arabic work was known as the Kitab-al Hind.

questions.question: Chahamanas were known as ________.
questions.explanation: Kings who engaged in warfare include the Chahamanas who was later known as the Chauhans.

questions.question: Chahamanas ruled over the:
questions.explanation: The kings who engaged in warfare include the Chahamanas, later known as the Chauhans, who ruled over the region around Delhi and Ajmer.

questions.question: The best Chauhans ruler was ________.
questions.explanation: The best-known Chahamana ruler was Prithviraja III (1168-1192), who defeated an Afghan ruler named Sultan Muhammad Ghori in 1191.

questions.question: Who were subordinate to the Pallava kings of Kanchipuram?
questions.explanation: A minor chiefly family from Cholas known as the Muttaraiyar held power in the Kaveri delta. They were subordinate to the Pallava kings of Kanchipuram.

questions.question: Muvendavelan means:
questions.explanation: The Chola kings gave some rich landowners title like muvendavelan which means a velan or peasant serving three kings.

questions.question: A minor chiefly family known as the Muttaraiyar held power in the:
questions.explanation: Subordinate to the Pallava kings, a minor chiefly family known as the Muttaraiyar held power in the Kaveri delta.

questions.question: Rajaraja I, considered the most powerful
questions.explanation: Rajaraja I, considered the most powerful Chola ruler, became king in 985.

questions.question: Rajaraja's son ________.
questions.explanation: Rajaraja’s son Rajendra I continued hispolicies.

questions.question: Chola bronze images are considered amongst the finest in the ________.
questions.explanation: Amongst the crafts associated with temples, the making of bronze images was the most distinctive. Chola bronze images are considered amongst the finest in the world.

questions.question: Many of the achievements of the Cholas were made possible through new developments in which field?
questions.explanation: Water from the channels provides the necessary moisture for agriculture, particularly the cultivation of rice.Hence many of the achievements of the Cholas were made possible through new developments in agriculture.

questions.question: The river Kaveri branches off into several small channels before emptying into the:
questions.explanation: The river Kaveri branches off into several small channels before emptying into the Bay of Bengal. These channels overflow frequently, depositing fertile soil on their banks.

questions.question: Agriculture had developed earlier in other parts of ________, it was only from the fifth or sixth century that this area was opened up for large-scale cultivation.
questions.explanation: Many of the achievements of the Cholas were made possible through new developments in agriculture. Agriculture had developed earlier in other parts of Tamil Nadu, it was only from the fifth or sixth century that this area was opened up for large-scale cultivation.

questions.question: Settlements of peasants, known as ________, became prosperous with the spread of irrigation agriculture.
questions.explanation: The administration of the empire was organised in the following way: Settlements of peasants, known as ur, became prosperous with the spread of irrigation agriculture.

questions.question: Nagarams was ________.
questions.explanation: Associations of traders known as nagarams occasionally performed administrative functions in towns.

questions.question: In ________, an empire was established under the Tang dynasty.
questions.explanation: In China, an empire was established under the Tang dynasty, which remained in power for about 300 years.

questions.question: Chingleput district was in ________.
questions.explanation: Inscriptions from Uttaramerur in Chingleput district, Tamil Nadu, provide details of the way in which the sabha was organised.

questions.question: Many new dynasties emerged after the ________.
questions.explanation: The major ruling dynasties in different parts of the subcontinent between the seventh and twelfth centuries. Many new dynasties emerged after the seventh century.

questions.question: Mahamandaleshvara means ________.

options.html: the great lord of a society
the great lord of a caste
the great lord of a king
the great lord of a region

questions.explanation: As Samantas gained power and wealth, they declared themselves to be maha-Samanta, maha- mandaleshvara which means the great lord of a “circle” or region.

questions.question: Who were subordinate to the Chalukyas of Karnataka?
questions.explanation: Initially, Rashtrakutas in the Deccan, were subordinate to the Chalukyas of Karnataka.

questions.question: Hiranya-garbha means ________.
questions.explanation: In the mid-eighth century, Dantidurga, a Rashtrakuta chief, overthrew his Chalukya overlord and performed a ritual called hiranya-garbha which is literally called the golden womb.

questions.question: Hiranya-garbha was thought to lead to the "rebirth" of the sacrifice as:
questions.explanation: When a ritual called hiranya-garbha was performed with the help of Brahmanas, it was thought to lead to the “rebirth” of the sacrificer as a Kshatriya, even if he was not one by birth.

questions.question: Gurjara-Pratihara Harichandra established their kingdom in
questions.explanation: The Gurjara-Pratihara Harichandra were Brahmanas who gave up his traditional professions and took to arms and successfully established kingdom Rajasthan.

questions.question: The inscriptions of the Cholas who ruled in Tamil Nadu refer to more than ________ terms for different kinds of taxes.
questions.explanation: The inscriptions of the Cholas who ruled in Tamil Nadu refer to more than 400 terms for different kinds of taxes. The most frequently mentioned tax is vetti and kadamai. There were also taxes on thatching the house, the use of a ladder to climb palm trees, a cess on succession to the family property, etc.

questions.question: Nagabhata was a ________.
questions.explanation: One prashasti, written in Sanskrit and found in Gwalior, Madhya Pradesh, describes the exploits of Nagabhata who is a Pratihara king.

questions.question: Kings rewarded Brahmanas by grants of land and were recorded on
questions.explanation: Kings often rewarded Brahmanas by grants of land. These were recorded on copper plates, which were given to those who received the land.

questions.question: Kalhana was written in which language?
questions.explanation: Kalhana, a Kashmiri, was the author of Rajatarangini, an account of the history of Kashmir. He wrote his work in Sanskrit.

questions.question: Kalhana wrote his work in ________ CE.
questions.explanation: Rajatarangini was written in Sanskrit by Kashmiri historian Kalhana in the 12th century CE.

questions.question: Tripartite struggle fought for control over
questions.explanation: For centuries, rulers belonging to the Gurjara-Pratihara, Rashtrakuta, and Pala dynasties fought for control over Kanauj.

questions.explanation: Extending control over parts of Central Asia, Iran, and the north-western part of the subcontinents Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni ruled from 997 to 1030.

questions.question: Ghazni raided the subcontinent almost every year - his targets were wealthy temples, including that of:
questions.explanation: Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni extended control over parts of Central Asia, Iran, and the north-western part of the subcontinent. He raided the subcontinent almost every year - his targets were wealthy temples, including that of Somnath, Gujarat.

questions.question: Chahamanas attempted to expand their control to the west and the east, where they were opposed by the Chalukyas of:
questions.explanation: Chahamanas, later known as the Chauhans, who ruled over the region around Delhi and Ajmer. They attempted to expand their control to the west and the east, where they were opposed by the Chalukyas of Gujarat.

questions.question: Prithviraj birth and death year was-
questions.explanation: The best-known Chahamana ruler Prithviraja III was born in 1168 and died in 1192.

questions.question: Prithviraj defeated an Afghan ruler named Sultan _________ in 1191.
questions.explanation: The best-known Chahamana ruler was Prithviraja III (1168-1192), who defeated an Afghan ruler named Sultan Muhammad Ghori in 1191.

questions.question: Cholas built the town of Thanjavur and a temple for goddess:
questions.explanation: Vijayalaya, who belonged to the ancient chiefly family of the Cholas from Uraiyur, captured the delta from the Muttaraiyar in the middle of the ninth century. He built the town of Thanjavur and a temple for goddess Nishumbhasudini there.

Kaveri valley, Sri Lanka and countries of Southwest Asia

questions.explanation: Rajaraja I, considered the most powerful Chola ruler. Rajaraja’s son Rajendra I continued his policies and even raided the Ganga valley, Sri Lanka and countries of Southeast Asia, developing a navy for these expeditions.

options.html: political, social and cultural life as well
economic, social and cultural life as well
economic, social and political life as well

questions.explanation: The specialists who worked at the temple and very often lived near it - priests, garland makers, cooks, sweepers, musicians, dancers, etc. In other words, temples were not only places of worship; they were the hub of economic, social and cultural life as well.

questions.explanation: One prashasti, written in Sanskrit and found in Gwalior, Madhya Pradesh, describes the exploits of Nagabhata, a Pratihara king.

questions.question: What is the literal meaning of Gangaikonda?
questions.explanation: Gangaikonda means the town of the Chola who took over(conquered)Ganga (water from Ganga).

Kadamba Mayurasharman and Mayura - Pratihara Harichandra

questions.question:
Read the below mention lines:
We have demarcated the boundaries of the land by making earthen embankments, as well as by planting thorny bushes. This is what the land contains: fruit-bearing trees, water, land, gardens and orchards, trees, wells, open spaces, pastureland, a village, anthills, platforms, canals, ditches, rivers, silt-laden land, tanks, granaries, fish ponds, beehives, and deep lakes.
He who receives the land can collect taxes from it. He can collect the taxes imposed by judicial officers as fines, the tax on betel-leaves, that on woven cloth, as well as on vehicles. He can build large rooms, with upper stories made of baked bricks, he can get large and small wells dug, he can plant trees and thorny bushes, if necessary, he can get canals constructed for irrigation. He should ensure that water is not wasted and that embankments are built.
This is part of the Tamil section of a land grant given by the ________.
questions.explanation: Kings often rewarded Brahmanas by grants of land. These were recorded on copper plates, which were given to those who received the land. The given paragraph is part of the Tamil section of a land grant given by the Cholas.

questions.question: A mosque is called a masjid in Arabic, literally a place where a ________ prostrates in reverence to Allah.
questions.explanation: A mosque is a place of worship for followers of Islam (Muslims). Mosques originated on the Arabian Peninsula, but are now found in all inhabited continents. The mosque serves as a place where Muslims can come together for salat ("prayer") as well as a center for information, education, social welfare, and dispute settlement. The imam leads the congregation in prayer.

questions.question: The duty of _______ was to lead military campaigns and maintain laws and orders in their iqtas.
questions.explanation: The earlier Sultans, the Khalji, and Tughluq monarchs appointed military commanders as governors of territories of varying sizes. These lands were called iqta and their holder was called iqtadar or muqti. The duty of the muqtis was to lead military campaigns and maintain law and order in their iqtas. They collected the assigned revenues from their iqtas, paid the soldiers their salaries, and maintained the required army.

questions.question: Ibn Batuta was a fourteenth Century traveller from
questions.explanation: Ibn Batutah(1304-1369), was a Moroccan(Northern Africa)- a scholar who widely travelled the medieval world. Ibn Battuta visited most of the Islamic world and many non-Muslim lands, including North Africa, the Horn of Africa, West Africa, the Middle East, India, Central Asia, South east Asia, and China. He dictated an account of his journeys, titled-A Gift to Those Who Contemplate the Wonders of Cities and the Marvels of Travelling. This account of his journeys provides a picture of medieval civilization that is still widely consulted today.

questions.question: The Khalji and Tughluq Monarchs appointed military commanders as governors of territories of varying sizes. These lands were called ________.
questions.explanation: Sultans like Khalji and Tughluq monarchs appointed military commanders as governors of territories of varying sizes. These lands were called iqta and their holder was called iqtadar or muqti. The duty of the muqtis was to lead military campaigns and maintain law and order in their iqtas.

questions.question: Muhammad Tughluq built the Begumpuri mosque in his capital ________.
questions.explanation: Begumpuri mosque was built in the reign of Muhammad bin Tughluq. It was the main mosque of Jahanpanah (Jahanpanah was the fourth medieval city of Delhi established in 1326-1327 by Muhammad bin Tughlaq(1321-51), of the Delhi Sultanate. Tughlaq built the fortified city of Jahanpanah “Sanctuary of the World”, his new capital in Delhi.

questions.question: Delhi became an important city only in the ________ century.
questions.explanation: Delhi is the capital of India and hence it is politically the most important city in India. But Delhi was like any other non-descript city till the twelfth century. It was only in the twelfth century, that Delhi became an important city. Right from the days of the Rajput rulers; Delhi gradually grew in political importance in India. During the Mughal rule, it reached its pinnacle of political importance.

questions.question: Babur the ruler of Kabul was invited by ________ to invade India.
questions.explanation: Daulat Khan Lodi was the governor of Lahore during the reign of Ibrahim Lodi, the last ruler of the Lodi dynasty. Due to disaffection with Ibrahim, Daulat invited Babur to invade the kingdom.

questions.question: Gender distinctions means ________ differences between women and men.
questions.explanation: Gender distinctions are the Social and biological differences between women and men. Usually, these differences are used to argue that men are superior to women.

questions.question: Warangal is in:
questions.explanation: Warangal is a city in the south Indian state of Telangana. It was the capital of the Kakatiya dynasty from the 12th to 14th centuries, and many intricately carved monuments from this period still stand. Among them are the ruins of 13th-century Warangal Fort and the Thousand Pillar Temple, built-in 1163.

questions.question: Delhi first became the capital of a kingdom under the ________.
questions.explanation: Delhi first became the capital of a kingdom under the Tomara Rajputs. Angapal of the Tomar Dynasty built a fort called Lal Kot (the present area around Suraj Kund, Delhi). Later on, Chauhans defeated Tomaras and established their rule.

questions.question: Tomara Rajputs were defeated in the middle of the twelfth century by the Chauhans of:
questions.explanation: Delhi first became the capital of a kingdom under the Tomara Rajputs, who were defeated in the middle of the twelfth century by the Chauhans (also referred to as Chahamanas) of Ajmer. It was under the Tomaras and Chauhans that Delhi became an important commercial centre. Many rich Jaina merchants lived in the city and constructed several temples. Coins minted here, called Dehliwal, had a wide circulation.

questions.question: Which of the following is not from the Rajput dynasty?
questions.explanation: Raziya Begum sultan name, Raziya al-Din (1205 - 1240), throne name Jalalat-ud-Din Raziya, usually referred to in history as Razia Sultan, was the Muslim Sultan of Delhi from 10 November 1236 - 14 October 1240. She was famously the only female ever to rule the Delhi Sultanate.

questions.question: What is the time of rule of Ananga Pala?
questions.explanation: Anangpal Tomar ruled over the areas of Delhi in India. In the 11th century, Anangapala Tomar ruled from 1130 to 1145. Little is known of Tomar, who was a Gurjar from the Tomara dynasty and whose ancestors had settled in the Aravalli Hills around the end of the first millennium CE.

questions.question: In ________ Sultan Iltutmish daughter, Raziyya became Sultan.
questions.explanation: Raziyya Sultana was the daughter of Shams ud din Iltutmish, who had begun life as a Turk slave and ended it as Sultan of Delhi. In 1236 Raziyya, became the Sultan of Delhi sultanate. She was removed from the throne in 1240.

questions.question: Raziyya was removed from the throne in ________.
questions.explanation: Raziyya Sultana was the daughter of Shams ud din Iltutmish, who had begun life as a Turk slave and ended it as Sultan of Delhi. In 1236 Sultan Iltutmish’s daughter Raziyya became Sultan. Minhaj-i Siraj recognised that she was more able and qualified than all her brothers. She was removed from the throne in 1240.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: ________ changed her name on her inscriptions and pretended she was a man.
questions.explanation: As women were supposed to be subordinate to men. Rudramadevi was influenced by the then Sultan of Delhi sultanate Raziya sultan. This was in contrast to the queen Rudramadevi (1262-1289), of the Kakatiya dynasty of Warangal, part of modern Andhra Pradesh, Rudramadevi changed her name to Rudradeva Maharaja, on her inscriptions and pretended she was a man.

questions.question: ________, the lands adjacent to a city or port that supply it with goods and services.
questions.explanation: In the early thirteenth century, the control of the Delhi Sultans rarely went beyond heavily fortified towns occupied by garrisons. The Sultans seldom controlled the hinterland of the hinterland cities and were therefore dependent upon trade, tribute, or plunder for supplies. The lands adjacent to a city or port that supply it with goods and services are known as Hinterland.

questions.question: ________ town a fortified settlement, with soldiers.
questions.explanation: Garrison town was a fortified settlement, with soldiers. In the early thirteenth century, the control of the Delhi Sultans rarely went beyond heavily fortified towns occupied by garrisons. Controlling garrison towns in distant Bengal and Sind from Delhi was extremely difficult. Rebellion, war, even bad weather could snap fragile communication.

questions.question: Many rich ________ merchants lived in the city and constructed several temples.
questions.explanation: It was under the Tomaras and Chauhans that Delhi became an important commercial centre. Many rich Jaina merchants lived in the city and constructed several temples. Coins minted here, called Dehliwal, had a wide circulation.

questions.question: The transformation of Delhi into a capital that controlled vast areas of the subcontinent started with the foundation of the Delhi Sultanate in the beginning of the:
questions.explanation: The transformation of Delhi into a capital that controlled vast areas of the subcontinent started with the foundation of the Delhi Sultanate at the beginning of the thirteenth century. Five dynasties together made the Delhi Sultanate. The Delhi Sultans built many cities in the area that we now know as Delhi. These were Dehli-i Kuhna, Siri, and Jahanpanah.

questions.question: Who ruled from 1351-1388?
questions.explanation: Sultan Firuz Shah Tughlaq was a Turkic Muslim ruler of the Tughlaq Dynasty, who reigned over the Sultanate of Delhi from 1351 to 1388. His father's name was Rajab who had the title Sipahsalar.

questions.question: Who was the daughter of Sultan Iltutmish?
questions.explanation: Raziya Begum sultan(1205 - 1240), throne name Jalalat-ud-Din Raziya, usually referred to in history as Razia Sultan, was the Muslim Sultan of Delhi from 10 November 1236 - 14 October 1240. She was famously the only female ever to rule the Delhi Sultanate. Razia Sultan was the daughter of Shams-ud-din Iltutmish, who had begun life as a Turk slave and ended it as Sultan of Delhi.

questions.question: Rudramadevi belongs to which dynasty?
questions.explanation: Rani Rudrama Devi or Rudramadevi, was a monarch of the Kakatiya dynasty in the Deccan Plateau from 1263 until her death. She was one of the very few women to rule as monarchs in India and promoted a male image in order to do so.

questions.question: Queen Didda ruled in:
questions.explanation: In 958AD, Queen Didda was the queen of Kashmir after the death of king Khemgupta. She ruled Kashmir as a queen, guardian and ruled between 950-1003 AD. Initially, Ksemagupta was succeeded by his son, Abhimanyu II. As Abhimanyu was still a child, Didda acted as Regent and effectively exercised sole power.

questions.question: What is the time of rule of Qutbuddin Aybak?
questions.explanation: Qutb-al-din Aibak also known as Qutub ud-Din Aybak (1150-1210), was the founder of the Mamluk dynasty and the first sultan of the Delhi Sultanate. He ruled the Delhi sultanate from 1206-1210.

questions.question: Begumpuri mosque was the main mosque of ________.
questions.explanation: Begumpuri mosque, built in the reign of Muhammad Tughluq, was the main mosque of Jahanpanah, the “Sanctuary of the World”, his new capital in Delhi. Jahanpanah was the fourth medieval city of Delhi established in 1326–1327 by Muhammad bin Tughlaq(1321–51), of the Delhi Sultanate. To address the constant threat of the Mongols, Tughlaq built the fortified city of Jahanpanah (meaning in Persian: “Refuge of the World”).

questions.question: Tax on cultivation was called ________.
questions.explanation:
There were three types of taxes that were collected during Delhi sultanate:

  1. tax on cattle.
  2. tax on houses.
  3. tax on cultivation called Kharaj and amounting to about 50 percent of the peasant’s produce.

questions.question: Which of the following city was not developed by Delhi Sultanate?
questions.explanation: The transformation of Delhi into a capital that controlled vast areas of the subcontinent started with the foundation of the Delhi Sultanate at the beginning of the thirteenth century. The Delhi Sultans built many cities in the area that we now know as Delhi. These were Dehli-i-Kuhna, Siri, and Jahanpanah.

questions.question: Which of the following is not the tax collected under Delhi Sultanate?
questions.explanation:
There were three types of tax that were imposed at the time of Delhi sultanate :

  1. on cultivation called kharaj and amounting to about 50 percent of the peasant’s produce
  2. on cattle and
  3. on houses.

questions.question: Sher Shah Suri defeated the Mughal emperor:
questions.explanation: Sher Shah Sur (1540-1545) started his career as the manager of a small territory for his uncle in Bihar and eventually challenged and defeated the Mughal emperor Humayun (1530-1540, 1555-1556). Sher Shah captured Delhi and established his own dynasty. Although the Suri dynasty ruled for only fifteen years (1540-1555), it introduced an administration that borrowed elements from Alauddin Khalji and made them more efficient.

questions.question: Muslims stand facing Mecca. In India this is to the ________.
questions.explanation: During prayer, Muslims stand facing Mecca. In India, this is to the west. This is called as qibla. Mecca is a desert valley in western Saudi Arabia Islam’s holiest city, as it’s the birthplace of the Prophet Muhammad and the faith itself. Dating from the 7th century, the central Masjid al-Haram surrounds the Kaaba(qibla).

questions.question: The language of administration under the Delhi Sultans was ________.
questions.explanation: Inscriptions, coins, and architecture provide a lot of information, especially valuable are “histories”, Tarikh (singular)/tawarikh (plural), written in Persian, the language of administration under the Delhi Sultans.

questions.question: Under ________ Delhi became an important commercial centre.
questions.explanation: Under the Tomaras and Chauhans Delhi became an important commercial centre. Many rich Jaina merchants lived in the city and constructed several temples. Coins minted here, called Dehliwal, had a wide circulation.

questions.question: Suri dynasty ruled for only ________.
questions.explanation: Sher Shah Suri (1540-1545) started his career as the manager of a small territory in Bihar and eventually challenged and defeated the Mughal emperor Humayun (1530-1540, 1555-1556). Sher Shah captured Delhi and established his own dynasty. Although the Suri dynasty ruled for only fifteen years (1540-1555), it introduced an administration that borrowed elements from Alauddin Khalji. Sher Shah’s administration became the model followed by the great emperor Akbar.

questions.question: The idea of the Three Orders was first formulated in-
questions.explanation: The “Three Orders” were the “Peace of God”, Knights and the Crusades. The idea of the “Three Orders” was first formulated in France in the early eleventh century. It divided society into three classes: those who prayed, those who fought, and those who tilled the land. This division of society into “Three Orders” was supported by the Church to consolidate its dominant role in society. This helped the emergence of a new warrior group called knights.

questions.question: Coins minted in delhi, called ________, had a wide circulation.
questions.explanation: It was under the Tomaras and Chauhans that Delhi became an important commercial centre. Many rich Jaina merchants lived in the city and constructed several temples. Coins minted here, called Dehliwal, had a wide circulation.

questions.question: The authors of tawarikh were learned men were:
questions.explanation: Tarikh (singular) / Tawarikh (plural), written in Persian, was the language of administration under the Delhi Sultans. The authors of tawarikh were learned men: secretaries, administrators, poets, and courtiers, who both recounted events and advised rulers on governance, emphasizing the importance of just rule.

questions.question: The first set of campaigns for expansion of Delhi Sultanate aimed at consolidating the hinterlands of the garrison towns. During these campaigns, forests were cleared in the ________ doab and hunter-gatherers.
questions.explanation: The first set of campaigns along the “internal frontier” of the Sultanate aimed at consolidating the hinterlands of the garrison towns. During these campaigns, forests were cleared in the Ganga-Yamuna doab and hunter-gatherers and pastoralists expelled from their habitat. These lands were given to peasants and agriculture was encouraged. New fortresses and towns were established to protect trade routes and to promote regional trade.

questions.question: Dehli-I kuhna is ________.
questions.explanation: Quwwat al-Islam mosque and minaret was the congregational mosque of the first city built by the Delhi Sultans, described in the chronicles as Dehli-i kuhna (the old city).

questions.question: Qutub Minar was built by three sultans:

options.html: Qutbuddin Aybak, Iltutmish, and Firuz Shah Tughluq
Qutbuddin Aybak, Raziyya, and Firuz Shah Tughluq
Qutbuddin Aybak, Raziyya, and Muhammad Tughluq
Qutbuddin Akbar, Iltutmish, and Muhammad Tughluq

questions.explanation: The Qutb complex is an array of monuments and buildings at Mehrauli in Delhi, India. The best-known structure in the complex is the Qutub Minar, built to honor the Sufi saint Qutbuddin Bakhtiar Kaki. Its foundation was laid by Qutb-ud-din Aibak, who later became the first Sultan of Delhi of the Mamluk dynasty. The Minar was added upon by his successor Iltutmish, and much later by Firoz Shah Tughlaq, a Sultan of Delhi from the Tughlaq dynasty in 1368 AD.

questions.question: Qibla means:

options.html: A direction that should be faced when a Muslim prays

questions.explanation: During prayer, Muslims stand facing Mecca. In India, this is to the west. This is called as the qibla or Kaba.

questions.question: The chronicler of the age, ________, recognised that Raziyya was more able and qualified than all her brothers.
questions.explanation: In 1236 Sultan Iltutmish’s daughter, Raziyya became Sultan. The chronicler of the age, Minhaj-i-Siraj, recognised that she was more able and qualified than all her brothers. But he was not comfortable at having a queen as a ruler. Nor were the nobles happy at her attempts to rule independently. She was removed from the throne in 1240.

questions.question: The Mongols under Genghis Khan invaded Transoxiana in ________ in 1219.
questions.explanation: The Mongols under Genghis Khan invaded Transoxiana in north-east Iran in 1219 and the Delhi Sultanate faced their onslaught soon after. Mongol attacks on the Delhi Sultanate increased during the reign of Alauddin Khalji and in the early years of Muhammad Tughluq’s rule.

questions.question: What forced the two rulers Allauddin Khilji and Muhammed Tughluq to mobilise a large standing army in Delhi?
questions.explanation: The Mongols under Genghis Khan invaded Transoxiana in north-east Iran in 1219 and the Delhi Sultanate faced their onslaught soon after. Mongol attacks on the Delhi Sultanate increased during the reign of Alauddin Khalji and in the early years of Muhammad Tughluq’s rule. This forced the two rulers to mobilise a large standing army in Delhi which posed a huge administrative challenge.

questions.question: Sultan Muhammed Tughluq appointed ________, as wine distiller.
questions.explanation: Sultan Muhammad Tughluq appointed Aziz Khummar, a wine distiller, Firuz Hajjam, a barber, Mank a Tabbakh, a cook, and two gardeners, Ladha and Pira, to high administrative posts.

questions.question: Sultan Muhammed Tughluq appointed ________, a barber.
questions.explanation: Sultan Muhammad Tughluq appointed Firuz Hajjam, a barber, Aziz Khummar, a wine distiller Mank a Tabbakh, a cook, and two gardeners, Ladha and Pira, to high administrative posts.

questions.question: ________, a fourteenth-century chronicler reported the appointments as a sign of the sultan's loss of judgment and his incapacity to rule.
questions.explanation: Ziauddin Barani, a mid-fourteenth-century-chronicler, reported the appointments as a sign of the Sultan’s loss of political judgment and his incapacity to rule.

questions.question: Where were the residents of the city of Dehli-i-Kuhna sent?
questions.explanation: The Sultanate was attacked in the early years of Muhammad Tughluq’s reign. The Mongol army was defeated. Muhammad Tughluq was confident about the strength of his army and his resources to plan an attack on Transoxiana. He, therefore, raised a large standing army. Rather than constructing a new garrison town, the oldest of the four cities of Delhi (Dehli-i Kuhna) was emptied of its residents and the soldiers garrisoned there. The residents of the old city were sent to the new capital of Daulatabad in the south.

questions.question: The Tawarikh was written in ________, the language of the Delhi Sultans.
questions.explanation: Tarikh (singular) / Tawarikh (plural), written in Persian, was the language of administration under the Delhi Sultans. The authors of Tawarikh were learned men: secretaries, administrators, poets, and courtiers, who both recounted events and advised rulers on governance, emphasising the importance of just rule.

questions.question: The five dynasties that together made the Delhi Sultanate were:
questions.explanation: The Delhi Sultanate was a Muslim sultanate based mostly in Delhi that stretched over large parts of the Indian subcontinent for 320 years (1206-1526). Five dynasties ruled over the Delhi Sultanate sequentially: the Mamluk dynasty (1206-90), the Khalji dynasty (1290-1320), the Tughlaq dynasty (1320-1414), the Sayyid dynasty (1414-51) and the Lodi dynasty (1451-1526).

questions.question: ________ started the construction of Qutub Minar at Delhi.
questions.explanation: Qutub Minar is a minaret that forms part of the Qutb complex, in the Mehrauli area of Delhi, India. Qutub Minar is a 73-metre tall tapering tower of five storeys, It contains a spiral staircase of 379 steps. Qutb Ud-Din-Aibak, a founder of the Delhi Sultanate, started construction of the Qutub Minar's first storey around 1192. In 1220, Aibak's successor Iltutmish completed a further three storeys. In 1369, Firoz Shah Tughlaq replaced the damaged storey and added one more.

questions.question: The second expansion occurred along the external frontier of the Sultanate. Military expeditions into southern India started during the reign of:
questions.explanation: The first set of campaigns along the “internal frontier” of the Sultanate aimed at consolidating the hinterlands of the garrison towns. The second expansion occurred along the “external frontier” of the Sultanate. Military expeditions into southern India started during the reign of Alauddin Khalji and culminated with Muhammad Tughluq. In their campaigns, Sultanate armies captured elephants, horses, and slaves and carried away precious metals.

questions.question: Which Phrase from the following defined the relationship between the king and his subjects?
questions.explanation: The Circle of Justice: Fakhr-i Mudabbir wrote in the thirteenth century. A king cannot survive without soldiers. And soldiers cannot live without salaries. Salaries come from the revenue collected from peasants. But peasants can pay revenue only when they are prosperous and happy. This happens when the king promotes justice and honest governance.

questions.question: Quwwat al-Islam mosque and minaret built during the last decade of the ________ century.
questions.explanation: Quwwat al-Islam mosque and minaret built during the last decade of the twelfth century. This was the congregational mosque of the first city built by the Delhi Sultans, described in the chronicles as Dehli-i kuhna (the old city). The mosque was enlarged by Iltutmish and Alauddin Khalji. The minar was built by three Sultans-Qutbuddin Aybak, Iltutmish and Firuz Shah Tughluq.

questions.question: A mosque is called a masjid in ________.
questions.explanation: A mosque is called a masjid in Arabic, literally a place where a Muslim prostrates in reverence to Allah. In a “congregational mosque” Muslims read their prayers (namaz) together. Members of the congregation choose the most respected, learned male as their leader (imam) for the rituals of prayer.

questions.question: Which mosque, built in late 1520?
questions.explanation: Jamali Kamali Mosque and Tomb, located in Mehrauli, Delhi, is the mosque and the tomb of two persons with the names Jamali and Kamali. Jamali was greatly regarded in the Mughal dynasty rule of the Lodi's, a period from the rule of Sikander Lodi to the Mughal Dynasty. Kamali was an unknown person but associated with Jamali. The mosque and the tomb were constructed in 1528-1529, and Jamali was buried in the tomb after his death in 1535.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Dynasty Period
(a) Sayyid (i) 1290-1320
(b) Lodi (ii) 1414-1451
(c) Khalji (iii) 1451-1526
questions.explanation:
1. Sayyid Dynasty (1414 - 1451) Khizr Khan
  2. Lodi Dynasty (1451 - 1526) Bahlul Lodi
  3. Khalji Dynasty (1290 - 1320)

questions.question: Identify the figure shown below :-
questions.explanation: Firoz Shah's tomb is located at Hauz Khas complex in New Delhi, Attached to the tomb is a madrasa built by Firoz Shah in 1352-53. The entrance to the tomb is through a courtyard. The archway of the entrance gate has a date engraved on it- 1507-08, the reign of Sikandar Lodi, which suggests that he may have got the tomb repaired.

questions.question: Identify the figure shown below:-
questions.explanation: The Alai Darwaza that translates to 'Alai Gate' was named after the first Khalji Sultan named Ala-ud-din Khalji (Khilji) of the Khalji dynasty in 1311 AD. It lies towards the southern end of the ancient Quwwat-Ul-Islam Masjid within the Qutb Complex in Delhi.

questions.question: Identify the tomb shown below
questions.explanation: Shams ud-Din Iltutmish was the third ruler of the Delhi Sultanate, belonging to the Mamluk dynasty. Iltutmish consolidated the position of the sultanate in the Indian subcontinent. In 1236 Iltutmish died and was buried in the Qutb complex in Delhi.

questions.question: The authors of tawarikh were learned men and included ________.

questions.question: Identify whose campaign is shown in the below mention map

options.html: Alauddin Khalji's campaign into South India
Muhammad Tughluqs campaign into South India

questions.explanation: The given map shows Alauddin Khalji’s campaign into South India.

questions.question: Identify what process shown in the following pictures
questions.explanation:
Four stages in the preparation of a manuscript are:

  1. Preparing the paper.
  2. Writing the text.
  3. Melting gold to highlight important words and passages.
  4. Preparing the binding

questions.question:
Arrange the process of manuscript in order as shown below

  1. Writing the text
  2. Preparing the paper
  3. Preparing the binding
  4. Melting gold
questions.explanation:
Four stages in the preparation of a manuscript are as follows:

  1. Preparing the paper
  2. Writing the text
  3. Melting gold to highlight important words and passages
  4. Preparing the binding

questions.question: Identify the mosque shown below
questions.explanation: Begumpuri Masjid is one of the seven mosques said to have been built by Khan-i-Jahan Junan Shah, the Prime Minister of Firuz Shah Tughluq (A.D. 1351-88).

questions.question: The founder of the Sayyid Dynasty was ________.
questions.explanation: The Sayyid dynasty was established by Sayyid Khizr Khan, deputised by Timur to be the governor of Multan (Punjab). Khizr Khan captured Delhi from Daulat Khan Lodi on May 28, 1414, thereby establishing the Sayyid dynasty.

questions.question: The Sultans were dependent on ________ for supplies.
questions.explanation: In the early thirteenth century, the control of the Delhi Sultans rarely went beyond heavily fortified towns occupied by garrisons. The Sultans seldom controlled the hinterland of the hinterland cities and were therefore dependent upon trade, tribute, or plunder for supplies.

questions.question: The poet known as the Parrot of India was ________.
questions.explanation: Ab'ul Hasan Yamin ud-Din Khusrau (1253 - 1325) better known as Amir Khusrow, was a South Asian Sufi musician, poet, and scholar. He was an iconic figure in the cultural history of the Indian subcontinent. He was a mystic and a spiritual disciple of Nizamuddin Auliya of Delhi. He wrote poetry primarily in Persian, but also in Hindi. Khusrow is sometimes referred to as the "parrot of India".

questions.question: Identify the masjid below
questions.explanation: Moth Ki Masjid is a mosque located in Delhi, and was built in 1505 by Wazir Miya Bhoiya, Prime Minister during the reign of Sikander Lodi of the Lodi dynasty.

questions.question: This Masjid build during the reign of
questions.explanation: The above-shown photo is the Moth ki Masjid. It is a mosque located in Delhi and was built in 1505 by Wazir Miya Bhoiya, Prime Minister during the reign of Sikander Lodi of the Lodi dynasty.

questions.question: The first Turkish ruler to introduce Arabic coinage was ________.
questions.explanation: Iltutmish was the first to introduce a “Pure Arabic Coin” in India, Silver Tanka, and Copper Jital, the two coins of the Delhi Sultanate. The Silver Tanka issued by Iltutmish was weighing 175 grains. Balban later issued gold Tanka of the same weight. The coins prior to Iltutmish were introduced by the invaders, which bear the Sanskrit characters and even Hindu Gods, Bull and Shivaling.

questions.question: Identify the figure given below
questions.explanation: The Quwwat-Ul-Islam Mosque which means the 'Might of Islam' is known to be one of the oldest and ancient mosques that survived through the centuries and a definite sight to behold. It is called by many as the 'Great Mosque of India' that lies within the beautiful and ancient expanse of Qutb Complex in South Delhi and was built along with the Qutub Minar in 1193 AD and finished in 1197 AD by Sultan Qutb-ud-din Aibak of the Slave dynasty.

questions.question: Where the below monument was built in
questions.explanation: Quwwat al-Islam mosque and minaret built during the last decade of the twelfth century. This was the congregational mosque built in Delhi by the Delhi Sultans. The mosque was enlarged by Iltutmish and Alauddin Khalji. The minar was built by three Sultans – Qutbuddin Aybak, Iltutmish and Firuz Shah Tughluq. Combinedly it is known as the Qutb complex.

questions.question:
Match the following:

(a) Namaz (i) Leaders
(b) Imam (ii) Prayers
(c) Khutba (iii) Sermon
questions.explanation: A mosque is called a masjid in Arabic, literally a place where a Muslim prostrates in reverence to Allah. In a “congregational mosque” Muslims read their prayers (namaz) together. Members of the congregation choose the most respected, learned male as their leader (imam) for the rituals of prayer. He also delivers the sermon (khutba) during the Friday prayer. During prayer, Muslims stand facing Mecca. In India, this is to the west. This is called the qibla.

questions.question:
Match the following (official of Sultan Muhammad Tughluq)

Officials Designation
(a) Firuz Hajjam (i) Barber
(b) Mank a Tabbakh (ii) Cook
(c) Ladha and Pira (iii) Gardeners
questions.explanation: Sultan Muhammad Tughluq appointed Aziz Khummar, a wine distiller, Firuz Hajjam, a barber, Mank a Tabbakh, a cook, and two gardeners, Ladha and Pira, to high administrative posts. Ziauddin Barani, a mid-fourteenth-century-chronicler, reported their appointments as a sign of the Sultan’s loss of political judgment and his incapacity to rule.
