questions.question: Who was the first Mughal emperor?
questions.explanation: Babur was the first Mughal emperor (1526 - 1530), who succeeded the throne of Ferghana in 1494 when he was only 12 years old. He was forced to leave his ancestral throne due to the invasion of another Mongol group, the Uzbegs. After years of wandering, he seized Kabul in 1504. In 1526 he defeated the Sultan of Delhi, Ibrahim Lodi, at Panipat and captured Delhi and Agra.

questions.question: Amber is in ________.
questions.explanation: Jaipur State was a princely state of India from 1128 to 1948. It was centred on Jaipur town. It existed from the 12th century until Indian Independence in 1947. According to the different periods of history it was also known as Jaipur Kingdom, Amber Kingdom, Dhundhar Kingdom, and Kachwaha Kingdom.

questions.question: Name the new religion founded by Akbar.
questions.explanation: The Din-i Ilahi ("Religion of God") was a syncretic religion propounded by the Mughal emperor Akbar in 1582 AD, intending to merge the best elements of the religions of his empire, and thereby reconcile the differences that divided his subjects. The elements were primarily drawn from Islam and Hinduism, but some others were also taken from Christianity, Jainism, and Sikhism.

questions.question: When did Humayun re-establish Mughal empire in India?
questions.explanation: Sher Khan defeated Humayun at Chausa (1539) and Kanauj (1540), forcing him to flee to Iran. In Iran, Humayun received help from the Safavid Shah. He recaptured Delhi in 1555 but died in an accident in the following year.

questions.question: Sher-Shah Suri was killed in ________.
questions.explanation: Sher Shah Suri (1486-22 May 1545), born Farid Khan was the founder of the Sur Empire in North India, with its capital at Delhi. Sher Shah took control of the Mughal Empire in 1540. Sher Shah was killed on 22 May 1545 during the siege of the Kalinjar fort of Rajputs.

questions.question: Babur defeated the Sultan of delhi ________ in April 1526 AD.
questions.explanation: Babur, the first Mughal emperor (1526-1530), succeeded to the throne of Ferghana in 1494 when he was only 12 years old. He was forced to leave his ancestral throne due to the invasion of another Mongol group, the Uzbegs. After years of wandering, he seized Kabul in 1504. In April 1526 he defeated the Sultan of Delhi, Ibrahim Lodi, and his army at Panipat and captured Delhi and Agra.

questions.question: Babur defeated the Lodhi army in the battlefield of ________.
questions.explanation: In 1526 Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi and his Afghan supporters at Panipat. In 1527 he defeated Rana Sanga, Rajput rulers, and allies at Khanua. In 1528 he defeated the Rajputs at Chanderi. Established control over Agra and Delhi before his death.

questions.question: Akbar became an emperor at the age of ________ years.
questions.explanation: Abul-Fath Jalal-ud-din Muhammad Akbar (15 October 1542– 27 October 1605), popularly known as Akbar I and later Akbar the Great, was the third Mughal emperor, who reigned from 1556 to 1605. Akbar was 13 years old when he became emperor. Akbar succeeded his father, Humayun, under a regent, Bairam Khan, who helped the young emperor expand and consolidate Mughal domains in India.

questions.question: ________ refused to accept the Mughal authority for a long time.
questions.explanation: The Sisodiya Rajputs refused to accept Mughal authority for a long time. Once defeated, however, they were honourably treated by the Mughals, given their lands (Watan) back as assignments (watan jagir). The careful balance between defeating but not humiliating their opponents enabled the Mughals to extend their influence over many kings and chieftains.

questions.question: Who is intolerant of religious and cultural beliefs?
questions.explanation: A bigot is an individual who is intolerant of another person’s religious beliefs or culture. Religious scholars who emphasised ritual and dogma were often dogma bigots. Their bigot teachings create divisions and disharmony.

questions.question: Who was the financial officer of a province?
questions.explanation: The empire was divided into provinces called subas, that are governed by a subadar who carried out both political and military functions. Each province also had a financial officer known as diwan. For the maintenance of peace and order in his province, the subadar was supported by other officers such as the military paymaster (bakhshi), the minister in charge of religious and charitable patronage (sadr), military commanders (faujdars) and the town police commander (kotwal).

questions.question: Who wrote Ain-i-Akbari?
questions.explanation: The Ain-i-Akbari or the "Constitution of Akbar", is a 16th-century, detailed document recording the administration of emperor Akbar's empire, written by his vizier, Abu'l-Fazl ibn Mubarak. It makes the Volume III and the final part of the much larger document, the Akbarnama, the Book of Akbar, also by Abul Fazl, and it itself is in three volumes.

questions.question: Nur Jahan was the wife of__
questions.explanation: Nur Jahan born Mehr-un-Nissa (31 May 1577 – 17 December 1645) was Empress consort of the Mughal Empire from 25 May 1611 to 28 October 1627 as the eighteenth (and last) wife of the Mughal emperor Jahangir.She was also his most beloved and influential wife and acted as his chief consort and Padshah Begum, officially from 1620–1627, after the title's previous holder, Saliha Banu Begum (the Padshah Begum for most of Jahangir's reign), died in 1620.

questions.question: The mother of Shahjahan was the daughter of the Rajput ruler of ________.
questions.explanation: Jagat Gosaini was a wife of the Mughal emperor Jahangir and the mother of his successor, the fifth Mughal emperor Shah Jahan. She is also known as Jodh Bai, Manmati, and was given the posthumous title of Bilqis Makani. By birth, she was a Rajput princess of Marwar(jodhpur) and was the daughter of Raja Udai Singh, the Rathore ruler of Marwar.

questions.question: From their father's side, Mughals were the successors of:
questions.explanation: The Mughals were descendants of two great lineages of rulers. From their mother’s side, they were descendants of Genghis Khan, ruler of the Mongol tribes, China and Central Asia. From their father’s side, they were the successors of Timur (died 1404), the ruler of Iran, Iraq, and modern-day Turkey.

questions.question: Genghis khan was a ruler of ________.
questions.explanation: Genghis Khan, born Temujin, was the Great Khan and founder of the Mongol Empire, which became the largest contiguous empire in history after his death. He came to power by uniting many of the nomadic tribes of Northeast Asia.

questions.question: Who was the successor of Sher Shah Suri?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah Suri(1486-22 May 1545), born Farid Khan was the founder of the Sur Empire in North India, with its capital at Delhi. An ethnic Pashtun, Sher Shah took control of the Mughal Empire in 1540. After his accidental death in 1545, his son Islam Shah became his successor.

questions.question: The revenue system adopted by Todar Mal was known as?
questions.explanation: Akbar’s revenue minister, Todar Mal, carried out a careful survey of crop yields, prices, and areas cultivated for a ten-year period, 1570-1580. On the basis of this data, the tax was fixed on each crop in cash. Each province was divided into revenue circles with its own schedule of revenue rates for individual crops. This revenue system was known as Zabt.

questions.question: From their mother's side Mughals were descendants of ________.
questions.explanation: The Mughals were descendants of two great lineages of rulers. From their mother’s side, they were descendants of Genghis Khan (died 1227), ruler of the Mongol tribes, China and Central Asia. From their father’s side, they were the successors of Timur (died 1404), the ruler of Iran, Iraq and modern-day Turkey.

questions.question: Mughals were the descendants of ________ who was the ruler of Iran, Iraq.
questions.explanation: The Mughals were descendants of two great lineages of rulers. From their mother’s side, they were descendants of Genghis Khan (died 1227), ruler of the Mongol tribes, China and Central Asia. From their father’s side, they were the successors of Timur (died 1404), the ruler of Iran, Iraq, and modern-day Turkey.

questions.question: When did Shah Jahan die?
questions.explanation: Shah Jahan(5 January 1592 - 22 January 1666) was the fifth Mughal emperor, who reigned from 1628 to 1658. In September 1657, Shah Jahan fell seriously ill, which set off a war of succession among his four sons, in which his third son Aurangzeb, emerged victorious. Shah Jahan recovered from his illness, but Aurangzeb put his father under house arrest in Agra Fort from July 1658 until his death in January 1666. On 31 July 1658, Aurangzeb crowned himself emperor under the title "Alamgir".

questions.question: Which Mughal emperor was interested in the religion and social customs of different people?
questions.explanation: While Akbar was at Fatehpur Sikri during the 1570s he started discussions on religion with the ulama, Brahmanas, Jesuit priests who were Roman Catholics, and Zoroastrians. These discussions took place in the ibadat khana. He was interested in the religion and social customs of different people. It made him realise that religious scholar who emphasised ritual and dogma were often dogma bigots.

questions.question: A statement or an interpretation declared as authoritative with the expectation that it would be followed without question.
questions.explanation: Dogma is an official system of principles or tenets concerning faith, morals, behaviour, etc without raising a question. Religious scholars who emphasised ritual and dogma were often dogma bigots.

questions.question: Babur succeeded to the throne of Ferghana in ________.
questions.explanation: Babur, the first Mughal emperor (1526- 1530), succeeded to the throne of Ferghana in 1494 when he was only 12 years old. He was forced to leave his ancestral throne due to the invasion of another Mongol group, the Uzbegs. After years of wandering, he seized Kabul in 1504. In 1526 he defeated the Sultan of Delhi, Ibrahim Lodi, at Panipat and captured Delhi and Agra.

questions.question: Babur was forced to leave his ancestral throne due to the invasion of another Mongol group:
questions.explanation: Babur, the first Mughal emperor (1526- 1530), succeeded to the throne of Ferghana in 1494 when he was only 12 years old. He was forced to leave his ancestral throne due to the invasion of another Mongol group, the Uzbegs. After years of wandering, he seized Kabul in 1504. In 1526 he defeated the Sultan of Delhi, Ibrahim Lodi, at Panipat and captured Delhi and Agra.

questions.question: The rule of primogeniture is:

options.html: Where the eldest daughter inherited his father's estate
Where the youngest daughter inherited his father's estate
Where the eldest son inherited his father's estate
Where the youngest son inherited his father's estate

questions.explanation: Primogeniture is the right of succession belonging to the firstborn child, especially the feudal rule by which the whole real estate of an intestate passed to the eldest son. The Mughals did not believe in the rule of primogeniture. Instead, they followed the Mughal and Timurid custom of coparcenary inheritance or a division of the inheritance amongst all the sons.

questions.question: Who insulted Shivaji when he came to accept Mughal authority?
questions.explanation: Mughal intervention in the succession and internal politics of the Rathor Rajputs of Marwar led to their rebellion. Campaigns against the Maratha chieftain Shivaji were initially successful. But Aurangzeb insulted Shivaji who escaped from Agra, declared himself an independent king and resumed his campaigns against the Mughals.

questions.question: What does the term Mansab mean?
questions.explanation: Those who joined Mughal service were enrolled as mansabdars. It was a grading system used by the Mughals to fix rank, salary, and military responsibilities. The term mansab means a position or rank, and Mansabdar refers to an individual who holds a mansab.

questions.question: In Akbar's reign there were ________ mansabdars with a rank of 5,000 zat, by Aurangzeb's reign the number of mansabdars had increased to ________?
questions.explanation: Nobles with a zat of 5,000 were ranked higher than those of 1,000. In Akbar’s reign, there were 29 mansabdars with a rank of 5,000 zat; by Aurangzeb’s reign, the number of mansabdars had increased to 79.

questions.question: In Mansabdar system rank and salary were determined by a numerical value called?
questions.explanation: Those who joined Mughal service were enrolled as Mansabdars. The term Mansabdar refers to an individual who holds a Mansab, meaning a position or rank. It was a grading system used by the Mughals to fix: rank, salary and military responsibilities. Rank and salary were determined by a numerical value called Zat.

questions.question: Mansabdars received their salaries as revenue assignments known as-
questions.explanation: The term Mansabdar refers to an individual who holds a Mansab, meaning a position or rank. It was a grading system used by the Mughals to fix rank, salary, and military responsibilities. Mansabdars received their salaries as revenue assignments called jagirs which were somewhat like Iqtas.

questions.question: Todar Mal, carried out a careful survey of crop yields, prices and areas cultivated for a ten-year period of:
questions.explanation: The main source of income available to Mughal rulers was the tax on the produce of the peasantry. Each province was divided into revenue circles with its own schedule of revenue rates for individual crops. This revenue system was known as Zabt. Akbar’s revenue minister, Todar Mal, carried out a careful survey of crop yields, prices, and areas cultivated for a ten-year period, 1570-1580. On the basis of this data, the tax was fixed on each crop in cash.

questions.question: Who helped Babur against Lodhis?
questions.explanation: Rana Sanga invited Babur with a cunning plan of letting him destroy Lodhis of Delhi.

questions.question: Mehrunnisa, married the Emperor ________ in 1611?
questions.explanation: Nur Jahan born as Mehr-un-Nissa (31 May 1577 - 17 December 1645) was Empress consort of the Mughal Empire from 25 May 1611 to 28 October 1627 as the eighteenth (and last) wife of the Mughal emperor Jahangir. She was also his most beloved and influential wife and acted as his chief consort and Padshah Begum, officially from 1620-1627, after the title's previous holder, Saliha Banu Begum, died in 1620.

questions.question: Sulh-i kul means:
questions.explanation: Sulh-i kulis an Arabic term literally meaning “peace with all” or “universal peace” drawn from a Sufi mystic principle. As applied by the third Mughal Emperor of India, Akbar (who reigned 1556-1605), it described a peaceful and harmonious relationship among different religions.

questions.question: During Mughal Reign the empire was divided into provinces called ________, governed by a ________.
questions.explanation: The broad features of administration were laid down by Akbar and were elaborately discussed by Abul Fazl in his book the Akbar Nama, in particular in its last volume, the Ain-i Akbari. Abul Fazl explained that the empire was divided into provinces called Subas, governed by a subadar who carried out both political and military functions. Each province also had a financial officer or diwan.

questions.question: Who was the minister in charge of religious and charitable patronage during Mughal reign?
questions.explanation: Abul Fazl a historian in Akbar's court in his book Ain-i-Akbari explained that the empire was divided into provinces called Subas, governed by a subadar who carried out both political and military functions. Each province also had a financial officer or diwan. For the maintenance of peace and order in his province, the subadar was supported by other officers such as the military paymaster (Bakhshi), the minister in charge of religious and charitable patronage (Sadr), military commanders (faujdars) and the town police commander (kotwal).

questions.question: Who was a Bakhshi during the reign of Mughals?
questions.explanation: Abul Fazl in Ain-i-Akbari explained that the empire was divided into provinces called Subas, governed by a subadar who carried out both political and military functions. Each province also had a financial officer or diwan. For the maintenance of peace and order in his province, the subadar was supported by other officers such as the military paymaster (Bakhshi), the minister in charge of religious and charitable patronage (Sadr), military commanders (faujdars) and the town police commander (kotwal).

questions.question: Documents from the ________ year of Shah Jahan's reign inform us that the highest-ranking Mansabdars were only 445 in number out of a total of 8,000.
questions.explanation: The administrative and military efficiency of the Mughal Empire led to great economic and commercial prosperity. Documents from the twentieth year of Shah Jahan’s reign inform us that the highest-ranking Mansabdars were only 445 in number out of a total of 8,000. This small number a mere 5.6 percent of the total number of Mansabdars received 61.5 percent of the total estimated revenue of the empire as salaries for themselves and their troopers. The Mughal emperors and their Mansabdars spent a great deal of their income on salaries and goods.

questions.question: Elizabeth was the last ruler of a dynasty known as the ________.
questions.explanation: Queen Elizabeth I (1558-1603) of England was the last ruler of a dynasty known as the Tudors. Elizabeth’s rule was marked by several conflicts - foremost amongst these were conflicts between the Roman Catholic Church and the Protestants, who were attempting to reform the Church.

questions.question: When was Queen Elizabeth I born and died?
questions.explanation: Queen Elizabeth, I born on 7 September 1533  and died on 24 March 1603 was Queen of England and Ireland from 17 November 1558 until her death. Sometimes called The Good Queen Bess, Elizabeth was the last monarch of the House of Tudors. Elizabeth’s rule was marked by several conflicts - foremost amongst these were conflicts between the Roman Catholic Church and the Protestants, who were attempting to reform the Church.

questions.question: A poet named Edmund Spenser wrote a long epic poem called:
questions.explanation: A poet named Edmund Spenser wrote a long epic poem called The Faerie Queene in her praise.

questions.question: What was the official language of Mughal empire?
questions.explanation: Nobody was forced to learn any particular language at the time of Mughal sultanate but the official language was Persian. The language of the court was also Persian, the business of the court was conducted in Persian. Those documents are still intact. Lots of those documents are now translated into English. People who study Mughal history oftentimes learn Persian also to dig out information from original Persian documents.

questions.question: Who among the following is not the son of Shah Jahan?
questions.explanation: Shahab-ud-din Muhammad Khurram better known by his regnal name Shah Jahan was the fifth Mughal emperor, who reigned from 1628 to 1658. In 1657-1658, there was conflict over succession amongst Shah Jahan’s 7 sons: Aurangzeb Dara Shukoh, Shah Shauja, Murad Baksh Sultan Daulat Afza, Sultan Luftallah, Sultan Ummid Baksh, Aurangzeb was victorious and acceded the throne.

questions.question: Salim was the name of Mughal emperor-
questions.explanation: Mirza Nur-ud-din Beig Mohammad Khan Salim, or "Salim" known by his imperial name Jahangir (31 August 1569 - 28 October 1627), was the fourth Mughal Emperor who ruled from 1605 until his death in 1627.

questions.question: The capital of Mirza Hakim, Akbar's half-brother, was ________.
questions.explanation: Mirza Muhammad Hakim (29 April 1553 – 10 October 1585), sometimes known simply as Mirza Hakim, or Mirza, was the second son of Mughal emperor Humayun. He ruled Kabul in Afghanistan and often conflicted with his elder brother emperor Akbar. Mirza Hakim, later on, mended ways with Emperor Akbar. He is known for writing Tajdar-e-Haram.

questions.question: The five Deccan Sultanates were Berar, Khandesh, Ahmadnagar, ________ and ________.
questions.explanation: The Deccan sultanates were five dynasties that ruled late medieval Indian kingdoms, namely, Bijapur, Golkonda, Ahmadnagar, Bidar, and Berar in south-western India. The Deccan sultanates were located on the Deccan Plateau, between the Krishna River and the Vindhya Range. These kingdoms became independent during the break-up of the Bahmani Sultanate. In 1490, Ahmadnagar declared independence, followed by Bijapur and Berar in the same year. Golkonda became independent in 1518 and Bidar in 1528. In 1510, Bijapur repulsed an invasion by the Portuguese against the city of Goa but lost it later that year.

questions.question: If Zat determined a Mansabdar's rank and salary, then sawar indicated his ________.
questions.explanation: The term Mansabdar refers to an individual who holds a Mansab, meaning a position or rank. Rank and salary were determined by a numerical value called Zat. The Mansabdar’s military responsibilities required him to maintain a specified number of sawar or cavalrymen. The Mansabdar brought his cavalrymen for review, got them registered, their horses branded, and then received money to pay them as salary.

questions.question: Who lost the second battle of Panipat?
questions.explanation: The Second Battle of Panipat was fought on November 5, 1556, between the forces of Hemu, the Hindu general and Chief Minister of Adil Shah Suri, and the army of the Mughal emperor, Akbar. When Hemu Vikramaditya at the battle of Panipat attacked Akbar at the head of his army it was the artillery he had lost that was utilized by the enemy to hold on to its ranks.

questions.question: Who was the last ruler of Delhi Sultanate?
questions.explanation:
The Delhi Sultanate was a Muslim sultanate based mostly in Delhi that stretched over large parts of the Indian subcontinent for 320 years (1206-1526). Five dynasties ruled over the Delhi Sultanate sequentially:

  1. the Mamluk dynasty (1206-90),
  2. the Khalji dynasty (1290-1320),
  3. theTughlaq dynasty (1320-1414),
  4. the Sayyid dynasty (1414-51), and
  5. the Lodi dynasty (1451-1526): The last ruler of this dynasty, Ibrahim Lodi was defeated and killed by Babur in the First Battle of Panipat on April 20, 1526. After Defeated by Babur in the First battle of Panipat (in 1526)thus the Delhi Sultanate ended in 1526.

questions.question: Policy of Sulh-i-kul was applied by ________.
questions.explanation: Sulh-i kulis an Arabic term literally meaning “peace with all,” “universal peace,” drawn from a Sufi mystic principle. As applied by the third Mughal Emperor of India, Akbar (who reigned 1556-1605), it described a peaceful and harmonious relationship among different religions.

questions.question: Who lost the fierce battle of Khanwa?
questions.explanation: The Battle of Khanwa was fought near the village of Khanwa, in Bharatpur District of Rajasthan, on March 17, 1527. It was the second major battle fought in modern-day India, between the invading forces of the first Mughal Emperor Babur and the Rajput forces led by Rana Sanga of Mewar, after the Battle of Panipat. Rana Sanga lost the battle. The victory Of Babur's Army consolidated the new Mughal dynasty in India.

questions.question: What were the affects of teaching of bigots?
questions.explanation: The bigot is an individual who is intolerant of another person’s religious beliefs or culture. Religious scholars who emphasised ritual and dogma were often dogma bigots. Their bigots' teachings created divisions and disharmony amongst the subjects.

questions.question:
Match the following:

(a) Akbar (i) 1627-1658
(b) Jahangir (ii) 1605-1627
(c) Shah Jahan (iii) 1556-1605
questions.explanation:
1. Abu'l-Fath Jalal-ud-din Muhammad Akbar (15 October 1542-27 October 1605), popularly known as Akbar I and later Akbar the Great, was the third Mughal emperor, who reigned from 1556 to 1605
  2. Mirza Nur-ud-din Beig Mohammad Khan Salim, known by his imperial name Jahangir (31 August 1569 – 28 October 1627) was the fourth Mughal Emperor who ruled from 1605 until his death in late 1627.
  3. Shahab-ud-din Muhammad Khurram (5 January 1592-22 January 1666) better known by his regnal name Shah Jahan, was the fifth Mughal emperor, who reigned from January 1628 to 1658.

questions.question: Identify the figure shown below
questions.explanation: The Red Fort is a historical fort in the city of Delhi in India. It was the main residence of the emperors of the Mughal dynasty for nearly 200 years, until 1857. It is located in the center of Delhi and houses a number of museums. In addition to accommodating the emperors and their households, it was the ceremonial and political centre of the Mughal state and the setting for events critically impacting the region.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Officers Duties
(a) Faujdar (i) Town police commander
(b) Kotwal (ii) Military commanders
(c) Bakshi (iii) Military paymaster
questions.explanation:
Abul Fazl explained that the empire was divided into provinces called Subas, governed by a subadar who carried out both political and military functions. Each province also had a financial officer or diwan.

  1. For the maintenance of peace and order in his province, the subadar was supported by other officers such as themilitary paymaster (bakhshi).
  2. town police commander (kotwal).
  3. military commanders (faujdars).

questions.question: Who wrote the biography of Humayun?
questions.explanation: Gulbadan Begum (1523 - 7 February 1603) was a Mughal princess and the youngest daughter of Emperor Babur, the founder of the Mughal Empire and the first Mughal emperor. She is best known as the author of Humayun-Nama, the account of the life of her half-brother, Emperor Humayun, which she wrote on the request of her nephew, Emperor Akbar.

questions.question:
Match the following:

(a) Babur father (i) Umar Shaikh
(b) Babur grandfather (ii) Miran Shah
(c) Babur great-great-grandfather (iii) Abu Said
questions.explanation: One of the miniature paintings (dated 1702-1712) of Timur, his descendants, and the Mughal emperors depicted that Timur is in the centre and on his right is his son Miran Shah (the first Mughal emperor Babur’s great-great-grandfather) and then Abu Said (Babur’s grandfather). To the left of Timur are Sultan Muhammad Mirza (Babur’s great-grandfather) and Umar Shaikh (Babur’s father).

questions.question: Rana Sanga was the king of which place?
questions.explanation: Maharana Sangram Singh (12 April 1482 - 30 January 1528) commonly known as Rana Sanga, was Rana of Mewar and head of a powerful Hindu Rajput confederacy in Rajputana during the 16th century. He belonged to the Sisodiya clan of Rajput. Rana ruled between 1508 and 1528. Rana Sanga succeeded his father, Rana Raimal, as king of Mewar in 1508. He fought against the Afghan Lodhi dynasty of Delhi Sultanate, and later against the Mughals.

questions.question: Nur Jahan was the wife of ________.
questions.explanation: Mehrunnisa married the Emperor Jahangir in 1611 and received the title Nur Jahan. She remained extremely loyal and supportive to the monarch. As a mark of honour, Jahangir struck silver coins bearing his own titles on one side, and on the other, the inscription “struck in the name of Queen Begum, Nur Jahan”.

General Instructions:

questions.question: Who was the last ruler of Mughal Empire?
questions.explanation: Mirza Abu Zafar Sirajuddin Muhammad Bahadur Shah Zafar or Bahadur Shah Zafar II, (24 October 1775 - 7 November 1862) was the last Mughal emperor. He was the second son and became the successor to his father, Akbar II, upon his death on 28 September 1837. He was a nominal Emperor, as the Mughal Empire existed in name only, and his authority was limited only to the city of Delhi.

questions.question: When did Jahangir die?
questions.explanation: Mirza Nur-ud-din Beig Mohammad Khan Salim, known by his imperial name Jahangir (31 August 1569 - 28 October 1627) was the fourth Mughal Emperor who ruled from 1605 until his death in 1627. Jahangir died in 1627 due to excessive drinking.

questions.question: Cannons were first used in ________ century.
questions.explanation: A cannon is a type of gun classified as artillery that launches a projectile using propellant. Cannons were an important addition in sixteenth-century warfare. Babur used them effectively in the first battle of Panipat.

questions.question: Babur used Cannons effectively at the first battle of ________.
questions.explanation: Cannons were an important addition in sixteenth-century warfare. Babur used them effectively at the first battle of Panipat. Babur's artillery played a "key role" in the establishment of the Mughal Empire. In 1526, the First Battle of Panipat saw the introduction of massed artillery tactics to Indian warfare. Under the guidance of Ottoman gun master Ustad Ali Quli, Babur deployed cannons behind a screening row of carts.

questions.question:
Match the following:

(a) 1526 (i) Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi
(b) 1527 (ii) Babur defeated the Rajputs at Chanderi
(c) 1528 (iii) Babur defeated Rana Sanga
questions.explanation:
1. The First Battle of Panipat, on 21 April 1526, was fought between the invading forces of Babur and the Lodi Kingdom, Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi. It took place in north India and marked the beginning of the Mughal Empire.
  2. The Battle of Khanwa was fought near the village of Khanwa, in Bharatpur District of Rajasthan, on March 17, 1527. It was the second major battle fought in modern-day India, between the invading forces of the first Mughal Emperor Babur and the Rajput forces led by Rana Sanga of Mewar, after the Battle of Panipat. 
  3. The Battle of Chanderi took place in the aftermath of the Battle of Khanwa in January 1528 which the Mughal Emperor Babur had defeated a confederacy of Rajputs and Afghans which was headed by Rana Sanga of Mewar.

questions.question: What was the full name of Akbar?
questions.explanation: Abul-Fath Jalal-ud-din Muhammad Akbar (15 October 1542-27 October 1605]), popularly known as Akbar I and later Akbar the Great, was the third Mughal emperor, who reigned from 1556 to 1605. Akbar succeeded his father, Humayun, under a regent, Bairam Khan, who helped the young emperor expand and consolidate Mughal domains in India.

questions.question: Where did Akbar set up an Ibadatkhana?
questions.explanation: The Ibadat Khana was a meeting house built in 1575 CE by the Mughal Emperor Akbar at Fatehpur Sikri to gather spiritual leaders of different religious grounds so as to conduct a discussion on the teachings of the respective religious leaders.

questions.question: Aurangzeb was the son of Emperor ________.
questions.explanation: Muhi-ud-Din Muhammad commonly known by the sobriquet Aurangzeb was born on 3 November 1618, in Dahod, Gujarat. He was the third son and sixth child of Emperor Shah Jahan and Mumtaz Mahal. He was the sixth, and widely considered the last effective Mughal emperor. His reign lasted for 49 years from 1658 until his death in 1707.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Terms Meaning
(a) Jagir (i) Rank
(b) Mansab (ii) Revenue assignment
(c) Zat (iii) Numerical value
questions.explanation:
Mansabdari was a grading system used by the Mughals to fix rank, salary, and military responsibilities.

  1. The Salary was determined by a numerical value called Zat.
  2. Mansabdars received their salaries as revenue assignments called jagirs.
  3. The term Mansabdar refers to an individual who holds a Mansab, meaning a position or rank.

questions.question: Identify what is shown in the figure below
questions.explanation: Details from a miniature, A corrupt officer receives a bribe

questions.question: When was the first battle of Panipat fought?
questions.explanation: The First Battle of Panipat, on 21 April 1526, was fought between the invading forces of Babur and the Lodi Kingdom. It took place in north India and marked the beginning of the Mughal Empire. This was one of the earliest battles involving gunpowder firearms and field artillery in the Indian subcontinent which were introduced by Mughals in this battle.

questions.question: Who was the revenue minister in the court of Emperor Akbar?
questions.explanation: Todar Mal was the Revenue or Finance Minister of the Mughal empire during Akbar's reign. He was one of the Navaratnas in Akbar's durbar.

questions.question: Who was prince Khurram?
questions.explanation: Shahab-ud-din Muhammad Khurram widely famous as Shah Jahan was born on 5 January 1592 was the third son of Prince Jahangir. The name "Khurram" (joyous) was chosen for the young prince by his grandfather, Emperor Akbar, with whom the young prince shared a close relationship.

questions.question: The book which was written by Abul Fazl is divided into how many volumes?
questions.explanation:
The Akbarnama which translates to Book of Akbar is the official chronicle of the reign of Akbar, written by his court historian and biographer, Abul Fazl who was one of the nine jewels in Akbar's court. It has 3 volumes,

  1. The first volume of Akbarnama deals with the birth of Akbar, the history of Timur's family, and the reigns of Babur and Humayun and the Suri sultans of Delhi.
  2. The second volume describes the detailed history of the reign of Akbar till 1602 and records the events during Akbar's reign. It also deals with that how Bairam Khan and Akbar won the battle of Panipat against Hemu an Indian warrior.
  3. The third volume is named Ain-a-Akbari and details the administrative system of the Empire.

questions.question: The Third volume of book Akbarnama deals with ________.
questions.explanation: The Akbarnama which translates to Book of Akbar, is the official chronicle of the reign of Akbar, by Akbar himself and by his court historian and biographer, Abul Fazl who was one of the nine jewels in Akbar's court. The third volume is named Ain-a-Akbari and details the administrative system of the Empire as well as containing the famous "Account of the Hindu Sciences". It also deals with Akbar's household, army, the revenues and the geography of the empire.

questions.question: Identify the following figure:
questions.explanation:
Qutub Minar is a minaret that forms part of the Qutb complex,in Delhi,India.

It is made up of red sandstone and marble, Qutub Minar is a 73-metre tall tapering tower of five storeys, with a 14.3 metre base. Qutb Ud-Din-Aibak, founder of the Delhi Sultanate, started construction of the Qutub Minar,Iltutmish completed a further three storeys. In 1369, a lightning strike destroyed the top storey.Firoz Shah Tughlaq replaced the damaged storey, and added one more.

questions.question: Who started the construction of Qutb Minar?
questions.explanation: Qutub Minar is a minaret that forms part of the Qutb complex, located in Delhi, India. It is Made up of red sandstone and marble, Qutub Minar is a 73-metre tall tapering tower of five storeys. It contains a spiral staircase of 379 steps. Qutb Ud-Din-Aibak, a founder of the Delhi Sultanate, started construction of the Qutub Minar's first storey around 1192. In 1220, Aibak's successor Iltutmish completed a further three-storeys.

questions.question: Who built the Agra Fort?
questions.explanation: Agra Fort is a historical fort in the city of Agra in India. It was the main residence of the emperors of the Mughal Dynasty till 1638 when the capital was shifted from Agra to Delhi. It is about 2.5km northwest of the famous monument, the Taj Mahal. It was built by emperor Akbar, the Agra Fort required 2,000 stone-cutters, 2,000 cement, and lime-makers and 8,000 labourers.

questions.question: Which of the following construction activity was not carried out by the king?
questions.explanation: Construction activity like building temples, mosques, and wells was carried out by kings.

questions.question: ________ is the part of a building above the ground floor.
questions.explanation: To make a large room with an elaborate superstructure requires more sophisticated skills. The Superstructure is the part of a building above the ground floor.

questions.question: Monuments provide an insight into:
questions.explanation: Monuments provide an insight into the technologies used for construction.

questions.question: Roofs, doors, and windows were still made by placing a horizontal beam across two vertical columns, a style of architecture called:
questions.explanation: Between the seventh and tenth centuries, architects started adding more rooms, doors, and windows to buildings. Roofs, doors, and windows were still made by placing a horizontal beam across two vertical columns, a style of architecture called “trabeate” or “corbelled”.

questions.question: The structure constructed in front of the main gate is known as ________.
questions.explanation: An ornamented gateway led to an entrance and the main hall which is called Mahamandapa where dances were performed.

questions.question: This was the place for ritual worship where only the king, his immediate family, and priests gathered.
questions.explanation: The image of the chief deity was kept in the main shrine i.e Garbhagriha. This was the place for ritual worship where only the king, his immediate family, and priests gathered.

questions.question: Village near the Rajarajeshvara temple is called ________.
questions.explanation: A village near the temple is called Charupallam, the “Village of the Incline”.

questions.question: Quwwat al-Islam mosque is in ________.
questions.explanation: Quwwat-al-Islam Mosque is situated near the Qutub Minar in New Delhi.

questions.question: Temples and mosques were beautifully constructed:

options.html: because they were places of trade
because they were places of worship
because they were places of education
because they were places to live

questions.explanation: Temples meant to demonstrate the power, wealth, and devotion of the patron. Temples and mosques were beautifully constructed because they were places of worship.

questions.question: Rajarajeshvara temple built by the ________.
questions.explanation: An inscription mentions that the Rajarajeshvara temple was built by King Rajarajadeva for the worship of his god, Rajarajeshvaram.

questions.question: The name of the ruler and the god are very similar. The king took the god's name:

options.html: because local people of their village like those name
because it was told by their parents
because it was auspicious and he wanted to appear like a god
because he did not wants to appear like a god

questions.explanation: The name of the ruler and the god are very similar. The king took the god’s name because it was auspicious and he wanted to appear like a god.

questions.question: Identify the following figure :
questions.explanation: The given Figure is the  Plan of the Jami Masjid built by Shah Jahan in his new capital at Shahjahanabad,1650-1656.

questions.question: Limestone cement used when mixed with stone chips hardened into:
questions.explanation: Limestone cement was increasingly used in construction. This was very high-quality cement, which, when mixed with stone chips hardened into concrete.

questions.question: The greatest law giver and architect was ________ himself.
questions.explanation: God created the world out of chaos and introduced order and symmetry. Hence, the greatest law-giver and architect was God Himself.

questions.question: ________ are safe, protected, and grandiose places of rest in this world.
questions.explanation: Tomb is a large part, typically an underground one, for burying the dead. Tombs are safe, protected and grandiose places of rest in this world.

questions.question: Which style of the construction was used in the construction of temples, mosque, tombs attached to large stepped walls between the 8th and 13th centuries?
questions.explanation: Between the eighth and thirteenth centuries, the trabeate style was used in the construction of temples, mosques, tombs, and in buildings attached to large stepped-wells (Baolis).

questions.question: Ab is the Persian word which means ________.
questions.explanation: Derived from the Persian word ab means water.

questions.question: Sultan Iltutmish won universal respect for constructing a large reservoir just outside ________.
questions.explanation: Sultan Iltutmish won universal respect for constructing a large reservoir knows as Hauz-i-Sultani or the “King’s Reservoir” just outside Dehli-i-Kuhna.

questions.question: Golden Temple is in ________.
questions.explanation: Amritsar is a city in the northwestern Indian state of Punjab.

questions.question: Amritsar is in ________.
questions.explanation: Amritsar is a city in the northwestern Indian state of Punjab.

questions.question: Pandyan king Shrimara Shrivallabha invaded ________ and defeated the ________.
questions.explanation: In the early ninth century when the Pandyan king Shrimara Shrivallabha invaded Sri Lanka and defeated the king, Sena I (831-851).

questions.question: Which of the following is not the Mughal emperor?
questions.explanation: Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni was a contemporary of Rajendra I.

questions.question: Identify the following figure- whose paintings is shown below
questions.explanation: The given image is a 1590 painting of Babur supervising workers laying out a chahar bagh in Kabul.

questions.question: Among the following mughals especially ________ was personally interested in literature, art and architecture.
questions.explanation: Babur, Humayun, Akbar, Jahangir, and especially Shah Jahan were personally interested in literature, art, and architecture.

questions.question: Who described his interest in planning and laying out formal gardens, placed within rectangular walled enclosures, and divided into four quarters by artificial channels.
questions.explanation: In Babur's autobiography, there is a description of his interest in planning and laying out formal gardens, placed within rectangular walled enclosures, and divided into four quarters by artificial channels.

questions.question: The central towering dome and the tall gateway are known as ________.
questions.explanation: The central towering dome and the tall gateway known as Pishtaq became important aspects of Mughal architecture.

questions.question: Hasht Bihisht is a central hall surrounded by:
questions.explanation: A tall gateway was placed in the centre of a huge formal Chahar bagh and built in the tradition known as “eight paradises” or Hasht Bihisht - a central hall surrounded by eight rooms.

questions.question: Coloured hard stone placed in a depression carved into marble sandstone creating beautiful, ornate patterns is called ________.
questions.explanation: The emperor’s throne were a series of pietra dura inlays which is a Coloured hard stone placed in a depression carved into marble sandstone creating beautiful, ornate patterns.

questions.question: The pedestal on which Shah Jahan's throne was placed frequently described as the ________.
questions.explanation: The pedestal on which Shah Jahan's throne was placed was frequently described as the qibla, the direction faced by Muslims at prayer.

questions.question: Qutb Minar constructed around-
questions.explanation: Qutbuddin Aybak had Qutb Minar constructed around 1199.

questions.question: Look at the following figure. Find two bands of inscriptions under the balcony? These are in
questions.explanation: The pattern created under the balcony by the small arches and geometrical designs and two bands of inscriptions under the balcony are in Arabic.

questions.question: Between the eighth and the eighteenth centuries kings and their officers built two kinds of structures. Which of the following not covered under the first structure or differ from the other three?
questions.explanation: Between the eighth and the eighteenth centuries kings and their officers built two kinds of structures: The first were forts, palaces, garden residences, and tombs - safe, protected, and grandiose places of rest in this world. Mosques were included in the second kind of structure.

questions.question: Identify the following figure :-

options.html: Screen in the Red Fort, Agra
Screen in the Quwwat al-Islam mosque, Agra
Screen in the Quwwat al-Islam mosque, Delhi
Screen in the Taj Mahal, Agra

questions.explanation: The given figure is the Screen in the Quwwat al-Islam mosque, Delhi (late twelfth century).

questions.question: Shah Jahan’s audience halls were specially constructed to resemble a ________.
questions.explanation: It was during Shah Jahan’s reign that the different elements of Mughal architecture were fused together in a grand harmonious synthesis. Shah Jahan’s audience halls were specially constructed to resemble a mosque.

questions.question: Diwan-i-Khas or Diwan-i-Am courts were also described as ________.
questions.explanation: The ceremonial halls of the public and private audiences (Diwan-i-Khas or Diwan-i-Am) were carefully planned. Placed within a large courtyard, these courts were also described as Chihil Sutun or forty-pillared halls.

questions.question: Planning and laying out formal gardens, placed within rectangular walled enclosures, and divided into four quarters by artificial channels. These gardens were called:
questions.explanation: These gardens were called Chahar bagh, four gardens, because of their symmetrical division into quarters.

questions.question: King Dhangadeva belongs to which dynasty?
questions.explanation: The Kandariya Mahadeva temple dedicated to Shiva was constructed in 999 by the king Dhangadeva of the Chandela dynasty.

questions.question: To whom the Kandariya Mahadeva temple is dedicated?
questions.explanation: The Kandariya Mahadeva temple dedicated to Shiva was constructed in 999 by the king Dhangadeva of the Chandela dynasty.

questions.question: The image of the chief deity was kept in the main shrine called ________.
questions.explanation: The image of the chief deity was kept in the main shrine called Garbhagriha.

questions.question: The Rajarajeshvara temple at ________.
questions.explanation: Rajarajachola built Rajarajeshwara temple at thanjavur on 10th century A.D.

questions.question: Charupallam means:
questions.explanation: A village near the temple is called Charupallam, the “Village of the Incline”.

questions.question: Name the two technological and stylistic developments that are noticeable from the twelfth century.

options.html: Arcuate architectural form and use of limestone cement
Arcuate architectural form and use of marble
Trabeato architectural form and use of limestone cement
Corbelled architectural form and use of marble

questions.explanation:
Two technological and stylistic developments are noticeable from the twelfth century:

  1. The weight of the superstructure above the doors and windows was sometimes carried by arches. This architectural form was called “arcuate”
  2. Limestone cement which was increasingly used in construction.

questions.question: Identify the figure
questions.explanation: The image shown is a True arch; detail from the Alai Darwaza(early fourteenth century).

questions.question: The Qutb Minar is __________ storeys high.
questions.explanation: Qutb Minar, constructed by Qutbuddin Aybak had this constructed is 5 storeys high.

questions.question: Rajarajeshvara temple built by the King for the worship of ________.
questions.explanation: An inscription mentions that it was built by King Rajarajadeva for the worship of his god, Rajarajeshvaram.

questions.question: Persian court chronicles described the Sultan as the ________.
questions.explanation: Muslim Sultans and Padshahs did not claim to be incarnations of god but Persian court chronicles described the Sultan as the “Shadow of God”.

questions.question: An inscription in the Delhi mosque explained that God chose Alauddin as a king because he had the qualities of:
questions.explanation: An inscription in the Delhi mosque explained that God chose Alauddin as a king because he had the qualities of Moses and Solomon, the great law-givers of the past.

questions.question: Ab is the ________word.
questions.explanation: The word ab, meaning water has been derived from the Persian term.

questions.question: Who won universal respect for constructing a large reservoir?
questions.explanation: Sultan Iltutmish won universal respect for constructing a large reservoir just outside Dehli-i-Kuhna. It was called the Hauz-i Sultani or the “King’s Reservoir”.

questions.question: Hauz-i Sultani means:
questions.explanation: Sultan Iltutmish constructed a large reservoir outside Dehli-i-Kuhna. It was called the Hauz-i-Sultani or the “King’s Reservoir”.

questions.question: Shrimara Shrivallabha was a ________.
questions.explanation: In the early ninth century, Shrimara Shrivallabha who was the Pandyan king invaded Sri Lanka.

questions.question: Madurai was the capital of the ________.
questions.explanation: According to Sangam literature, Madurai was the capital of the Pandyan dynasty.

questions.question: Rajendra I was the ________.
questions.explanation: In the early eleventh century, the Chola king Rajendra I built a Shiva temple in his capital.

questions.question: Who was a contemporary of Rajendra I?
questions.explanation: Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni was a contemporary of Rajendra I.

questions.question: Sultan Mahmud of Ghazni looted which temple?
questions.explanation: Sultan Mahmud was not a very important ruler at that time. But by destroying and looting temples - especially the one at Somnath - he tried to win credit as a great hero of Islam.

questions.question: The Taj Mahal at Agra, completed in ________.
questions.explanation: An immense structure of white marble, built-in Agra between 1631 and 1643 by order of the Mughal emperor Shah Jahan.

questions.question: Akbar's architects turned to the tombs of his Central Asian ancestor:
questions.explanation: There were several important architectural innovations during Akbar’s reign. For inspiration, Akbar’s architects turned to the tombs of his Central Asian ancestor, Timur.

tests.name: Rulers and Buildings Test 05
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 07 Social Science Rulers and Buildings CBSE Online Test 05 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Built by Akbar, the Agra Fort required ________ stone-cutters, ________ cement and lime-makers and ________ labourers.
questions.explanation:
Labour required for the Agra:
Fort Built by Akbar, the Agra Fort required: 2,000 stone-cutters, 2,000 cement and lime-makers and 8,000 labourers.

questions.question: Between the ____ and the _____ centuries kings and their officers built two kinds of structures
questions.explanation:
Between the eighth and the eighteenth centurieskings and their officers built two kinds of structures:
1. Forts, palaces, garden residences etc.
2.Structures meant for public activity including temples, mosques, tanks, wells, etc

questions.question: Identify the temple
questions.explanation: The given picture is of the Kandariya Mahadeva temple dedicated to Shiva was constructed by the king Dhangadeva.

questions.question: Who constructed the Kandariya Mahadeva temple and when?
questions.explanation: The Kandariya Mahadeva temple dedicated to Shiva was constructed in 999 by the king Dhangadeva of the Chandela dynasty.

questions.question: What is special about the Shikhara of Rajarajeshwara temple?

options.html: Built in a very short time
Shortest among the temple of its time
Tallest among the temple of its time

questions.explanation: The Rajarajeshvara temple at Thanjavur had the tallest shikhara amongst temples of its time.

questions.question: Thanjavur is located in ________.
questions.explanation: Thanjavur is a city in the south Indian state of Tamil Nadu.

questions.question: Which of the following is false?

options.html: Rulers offered patronage to the learned and pious.
Constructing places of worship provided rulers with the chance to proclaim their close relationship with God, especially important in an age of rapid Economic change.
Tried to transform their capitals and cities into great cultural centres that brought fame to their rule and their realm.
As each new dynasty came to power, kings wanted to emphasise their moral right to be rulers.

questions.explanation: Constructing places of worship provided rulers with the chance to proclaim their close relationship with God, especially important in an age of rapid political change.

questions.question: Rajarajeshwara temple Construction was not easy because:

options.html: people can easily lift the heavy stone of 90 tonne.
modern technology were there to lift the heavy stone.
there were cranes in those days which cannot lift 90 tonne stone.
there were no cranes in those days and the 90 tonne stone for the top of the shikhara was too heavy to lift manually.

questions.explanation:
The Rajarajeshvara temple at Thanjavur had the tallest shikhara amongst temples of its time. Constructing it was not easy because:

  1. There were no cranes in those days.
  2. The 90 tonne stone for the top of the shikhara was too heavy to lift manually.

questions.question: Inclined path to the top of the Rajarajeshwara temple started more than ________ kilometers away so that it would not be too steep.
questions.explanation: The architects built an inclined path to the top of the temple, placed the boulder on rollers and rolled it all the way to the top. The path started more than 4 km away so that it would not be too steep.

questions.question: Identify the following figure:-
questions.explanation: The given figure is a painting from the Akbar Nama (dated 1590-1595), showing the construction of the water-gate at the Agra Fort.

questions.question: Temples were meant to demonstrate ________.
questions.explanation: Temples were meant to demonstrate the power, wealth and devotion of the patron.

questions.question:
Match the following:

(a) Shikhara (i) The gateway of the temple
(b) Mandapa (ii) The structure over the central shrine of a temple
(c) Gopuram (iii) Constructed in front of the main shrine
questions.explanation:
1. Shikhara-Superstructure set above the inner sanctum or shrine of a temple.
  2. Mandapa -  Pillared hall or porch fronting a Hindu temple.
  3. Gopuram-Gopuram is a monumental tower at the entrance of any temple.

questions.question: Tomb of Humayun, constructed between ________.
questions.explanation: The construction of Humayun's tomb was started in1562 and took nine years to complete i.e 1571.

questions.question: Which of the following is not true?

options.html: As king and his allies worshipped their deities together in the royal temples it seemed as if they brought the just rule of the gods on earth.
The other, lesser deities in the temple were gods and goddesses of the allies and subordinates of the ruler.
The temple was a miniature model of the Palace in which the king and his allies live.
The largest temples were all constructed by kings.

questions.explanation: The temple was a miniature model of the world ruled by the king and his allies.

questions.question:
Match the following:
When the Chola king Rajendra I built a Shiva temple in his capital he filled it with prized statues seized from defeated rulers. Now match the prized statue with the rulers from where it was seized.

Rulers Prized statues
(a) Chalukyas (i) Sun-pedestal
(b) Kalingas of Orissa (ii) a Kali statue
(c) Palas of Bengal (iii) an image of Bhairava (a form of Shiva) and Bhairavi
questions.explanation: In the early eleventh century, when the Chola king Rajendra I built a Shiva temple in his capital he filled it with prized statues seized from defeated rulers. The list included: a Sun-pedestal from the Chalukyas, a Ganesha statue and several statues of Durga a Nandi statue from the eastern Chalukyas an image of Bhairava (a form of Shiva) and Bhairavi from the Kalingas of Orissa; and a Kali statue from the Palas of Bengal.

tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 07 Social Science Towns Traders and Crafts persons CBSE Online Test 01 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Thanjavur is capital of the ________.
questions.explanation: Thanjavur, formerly Tanjore first rose to prominence during the reign of Chola when it served as the capital of the empire.

questions.question: Madras is present day ________.
questions.explanation: Chennai was formerly known as Madras.

questions.question: Sthapati were called ________.
questions.explanation: Some distance away at Svamimalai, the sthapatis or sculptors are making exquisite bronze idols and tall, ornamental bell metal lamps.

questions.question: Kabul is the capital of ________.
questions.explanation: Kabul is the capital of Afghanistan.

questions.question: ________ were often central to the economy and society.
questions.explanation: Temple towns represent a very important pattern of urbanisation, the process by which cities develop. Temples were often central to the economy and society.

questions.question: Which of the following work is not performed by the weavers?
questions.explanation: Pottery making is the work of Potter.

questions.question: Bronze is an alloy containing:
questions.explanation: Bronze is an alloy containing copper and tin. Bell metal contains a greater proportion of tin than other kinds of bronze.

questions.question: Whose bronze statues were made using the lost wax technique?
questions.explanation: Chola bronze statues were made using the “lost wax” technique.

questions.question: Pushkar lake is in ________.
questions.explanation: Pushkar Lake or Pushkar Sarovar is located in the town of Pushkar in Ajmer district of the Rajasthan.

questions.question: Whose dargah is in Ajmer?
questions.explanation: Ajmer Sharif Dargah or Dargah Sharif is a Sufi shrine (Dargah) of Sufi saint, Moinuddin Chishti located at Ajmer, Rajasthan, India.

questions.question: Vasco da Gama was a ________.
questions.explanation: Vasco da Gama, a Portuguese sailor, was one of those who sailed across the Atlantic to the African coast.

questions.question: People in a small town income from far of places to sell their distant product except:
questions.explanation: Many people came from far and near to these towns to buy local articles and sell products of distant places like horses, salt, camphor, saffron, betel nut, and spices like pepper.

questions.question: Identify the figure
questions.explanation: The given figure is of a Wood Carver

questions.question: Taxes were levied by the ________.
questions.explanation: Usually, a Samanta or, in later times, a zamindar built a fortified palace in or near these towns. They levied taxes on traders, artisans, and articles of trade.

questions.question: ________ traders, including the communities of Hindu ________ and Muslim Bohras, traded extensively with the ports of the Red Sea, Persian Gulf, East Africa, Southeast Asia, and China.
questions.explanation: Gujarati traders, including the communities of Hindu Baniyas and Muslim Bohras, traded extensively with the ports of the Red Sea, Persian Gulf, East Africa, Southeast Asia, and China.

tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 07 Social Science Towns Traders and Crafts persons CBSE Online Test 02 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: From Africa traders brought ________.
questions.explanation: Gujarati traders, including the communities of Hindu Baniyas and Muslim Bohras, traded extensively with the ports of the Red Sea, Persian Gulf, East Africa, Southeast Asia, and China. They sold textiles and spices in these ports and, in exchange, brought gold and ivory from Africa.

questions.question: Saliyar or Kaikkolars were ________.
questions.explanation: Weavers such as the Saliyar or Kaikkolars emerged as prosperous communities, making donations to temples.

questions.question: Hampi is located in the ________.
questions.explanation: Hampi is located in the Krishna-Tungabhadra basin, which formed the nucleus of the Vijayanagara Empire, founded in 1336.

questions.question: Devadasi were the ________.
questions.explanation: Devadasis who were temple dancers performed before the deity, royalty, and masses.

questions.question: The most important festivals celebrated at Hampi was ________.
questions.explanation: The Mahanavami festival, known today as Navaratri in the south, was one of the most important festivals celebrated at Hampi.

questions.question: Emporium means:

options.html: A place where goods from diverse production centres are bought and sold
A place where goods are stored
A place where goods are produced

questions.explanation: An emporium is a place where goods from diverse production centres are bought and sold. Surat in Gujarat was the emporium of western trade during the Mughal period along with Cambay.

questions.question: Which of the following is not the Deccani sultan?
questions.explanation: Deccani Sultans - the rulers of Golconda, Bijapur, Ahmadnagar, Berar and Bidar.

questions.question: What is Zari?
questions.explanation: The textiles of Surat were famous for their gold lace borders i.e zari.

questions.question: Vasco da Gama reached Calicut in ________.
questions.explanation: Vasco da Gama's first journey took more than a year; he reached Calicut in 1498.

questions.question: English East India Company shifted its headquarters in 1668 to ________.
questions.explanation: Surat began to decline towards the end of the seventeenth century. This was because of many factors. Competition from Bombay (present-day Mumbai) where the English East India Company shifted its headquarters in 1668.

questions.question: Fish port town is called ________.
questions.explanation: As per the description of Masulipatnam by William Methwold Masulipatnam was the first port fisher town.

questions.question: The fort at Masulipatnam was built by the ________.
questions.explanation: Dutch and English East India Companies attempted to control Masulipatnam as it became the most important port on the Andhra coast. The fort at Masulipatnam was built by the Dutch.

questions.question: Official merchant of the East India Company was called ________.
questions.explanation: The description of Masulipatnam by William Methwold states about a Factor of the English East India Company. The factor was an Official merchant of the East India Company.

questions.question: Qutb Shahi rulers of ________.
questions.explanation: The Qutb Shahi rulers of Golconda imposed royal monopolies on the sale of textiles, spices, and other items.

questions.question: Who formed East India Companies in order to expand their commercial activities in the east?
questions.explanation: In the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries, European countries were searching for spices and textiles, which had become popular both in Europe and West Asia. The English, Dutch, and French formed East India Companies in order to expand their commercial activities in the east.

tests.name: Towns Traders and Crafts persons Test 03
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 07 Social Science Towns Traders and Crafts persons CBSE Online Test 03 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Who emerged as the most successful commercial and political power in the subcontinent?
questions.explanation: The European Companies used their naval power to gain control of the sea trade and forced Indian traders to work as their agents. Ultimately, the English emerged as the most successful commercial and political power in the subcontinent.

questions.question: Fort St. George was in ________ and Fort St William was in ________.
questions.explanation: The “white” rulers occupied the superior residencies of Fort St. George in Madras or Fort St William in Calcutta.

questions.question: A piece of land beyond coast is known as ________.
questions.explanation: Hinterland is the remote areas of a country away from the coast or the banks of major rivers.

questions.question: That part of the town where Indian artisans and merchants were to live was called ________.
questions.explanation: Crafts and commerce underwent major changes as merchants and artisans (such as weavers) were moved into the Black Towns established by the European companies within these new cities.

questions.question: Identify the picture
questions.explanation: The given picture is of Vasco da Gama, a Portuguese sailor.

questions.question: Which of the following metal was used by the Bidar craftsmen?
questions.explanation: The craftspersons of Bidar were so famed for their inlay work in copper and silver that it came to be called Bidri.

questions.question: Which river flows near the Rajarajeshvara temple?
questions.explanation: The perennial river Kaveri flows near this beautiful town and the temple built by King Rajaraja Chola.

questions.question: The ________ weavers of Thanjavur and the nearby town of Uraiyur are busy producing cloth.
questions.explanation: The Saliya weavers of Thanjavur and the nearby town of Uraiyur are busy producing cloth for flags to be used in the temple festival, fine cotton for the king and nobility, and coarse cotton for the masses.

questions.question: Who were making exquisite bronze idols and tall, ornamental bell metal lamps?
questions.explanation: Some distance away at Svamimalai, the Sthapatis or sculptors are making exquisite bronze idols and tall, ornamental bell metal lamps.

questions.question: Figure given below is statue of Krishna subduing the serpent demon Kaliya It is made up of which metal-
questions.explanation: Bronze is an alloy containing copper and tin. The given image is relating to a bronze metal. It is bronze statue of Krishna subduing the serpent demon Kaliya.

questions.question: Bhillasvamin temple town is in ________.
questions.explanation: Towns emerged around temples such as those of Bhillasvamin (Bhilsa or Vidisha in Madhya Pradesh).

questions.question: Which of the Pilgrimage centre developed into township?
questions.explanation: Pilgrimage centres also slowly developed into townships. Vrindavan (Uttar Pradesh) and Tiruvannamalai (Tamil Nadu) are examples of two such towns.

questions.question: Which was the capital of the Chauhan kings in the twelfth century?
questions.explanation: Ajmer (Rajasthan) was the capital of the Chauhan kings in the twelfth century and later became the Suba headquarters under the Mughals.

questions.question: Taxes on the market was collected in:
questions.explanation: There were taxes in kind on: Sugar and jaggery, dyes, thread, and cotton, On coconuts, salt, areca nuts, butter, sesame oil, on cloth. Besides, there were taxes on traders, on those who sold metal goods, on distillers, on oil, on cattle fodder, and on loads of grain. Some of these taxes were collected in kind, while others were collected in cash.

questions.question: Gujarati traders sold:

options.html: Spices and Pulses in the ports
Textiles and Pottery in the ports
Textiles and Spices in the ports

questions.explanation: Gujarati traders traded extensively with the ports of the Red Sea, Persian Gulf, East Africa, Southeast Asia, and China. They sold textiles and spices in these ports and, in exchange, brought gold and ivory from Africa; and spices, tin, etc.

questions.question: Traders brought blue Pottery and Silver from ________.
questions.explanation: Traders bought Chinese blue pottery and silver from Southeast Asia and China.

questions.question: The towns on the west coast were home to the following except:
questions.explanation: The towns on the west coast were home to Arab, Persian, Chinese, Jewish and Syrian Christian traders.

questions.question: Jean Baptiste Tavernier was a ________.
questions.explanation: In the seventeenth century Jean Baptiste Tavernier, a diamond merchant, estimated that the horse trade at Kabul amounted to ₹ 30,000 annually.

questions.question: The Panchalas or Vishwakarma community, consisting of:

options.html: goldsmiths, bronzesmiths, blacksmiths, masons, and carpenters
goldsmiths, bronzesmiths, farmers, silversmiths, and carpenters
goldsmiths, silversmiths, blacksmiths, masons, and carpenters

questions.explanation: The Panchalas or Vishwakarma community, consisting of goldsmiths, bronzesmiths, blacksmiths, masons, and carpenters, were essential to the building of temples.

questions.question: Identify which type of work is done in the following candle stand figure
questions.explanation: The given image is of a seventeenth-century candlestand; brass with black overlay. The craftspersons of Bidar were so famed for their inlay work in copper and silver that it came to be called Bidri.

questions.question: Murshidabad is situated on the bank of ________.
questions.explanation: The city of Murshidabad is located on the eastern bank of the Bhagirathi, a distributary of the Ganges River.

questions.question: Hampi is located in the Krishna-Tungabhadra basin, which formed the nucleus of the Vijayanagara Empire, founded in ________.
questions.explanation: In 1336 CE, the Vijayanagara Empire arose from the ruins of the Kampili kingdom. The Vijayanagara Empire built its capital around Hampi, calling it Vijayanagara.

questions.question: Identify the temple
questions.explanation: The given figure is of the Stone chariot, Vitthala temple.

questions.question: Virupaksha was a form of ________.
questions.explanation: Virupaksha is the seventh name of Lord Shiva as the one who has three eyes or is diversely eyed.

questions.question: Hampi fell into ruin following the defeat of Vijayanagara in ________ by the Deccani Sultans.
questions.explanation: Hampi fell into ruin following the defeat of Vijayanagara in 1565 by the Deccani Sultans - the rulers of Golconda, Bijapur, Ahmadnagar, Berar, and Bidar.

questions.question: Surat has been called the gate to ________ because many pilgrim ships set sail from here.
questions.explanation: Surat has also been called the gate to Mecca because many pilgrim ships set sail from here.

questions.question: In the seventeenth century who had not established their factories and warehouses at Surat.
questions.explanation: In the seventeenth century the Portuguese, Dutch, and English had their factories and warehouses at Surat.

questions.question: ________ is a note recording a deposit made by a person. The amount deposited can be claimed in another place by presenting the record of the deposit.
questions.explanation: The Surat Hundis were honoured in the far-off markets. Hundi means a note recording a deposit made by a person. The amount deposited can be claimed is another place by presenting the record of the deposit.

questions.question: Who had built huge banking houses at Surat?
questions.explanation: The Kathiawad Seths or Mahajans (moneychangers) had huge banking houses at Surat.

questions.question: Surat began to decline towards the end of the seventeenth century. This was because:

options.html: English East India Company shifted its headquarters to Surat
control of the sea routes by the Portuguese and competition from Surat

questions.explanation: Surat began to decline towards the end of the seventeenth century. This was because of many factors: the loss of markets and productivity because of the decline of the Mughal Empire.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: The town of Masulipatnam or Machlipatnam (literally, fish port town) lay on the delta of the ________.
questions.explanation: In the seventeenth century the town of Masulipatnam or Machlipatnam (literally, fish port town). It was a centre of intense activity lay on the delta of the Krishna river.

questions.question: Which two Companies attempted to control Masulipatnam as it became the most important port on the Andhra coast?
questions.explanation: Both the Dutch and English East India Companies attempted to control Masulipatnam as it became the most important port on the Andhra coast.

questions.question: In 1686-1687 which Mughal Emperor annexed Golconda?
questions.explanation: In 1686-1687 Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb annexed Golconda.

questions.question: Mulla Abdul Ghafur and Virji Vora were great traders who owned ________.
questions.explanation: Initially, great Indian traders like Mulla Abdul Ghafur and Virji Vora who owned a large number of ships competed with them. However, the European Companies used their naval power to gain control of the sea trade.

questions.question: The capital of Portugal in 1498:
questions.explanation: Lisbon is the stunning capital city of Portugal.

questions.question: From which trade the Italian traders purchased Indian spices and cloth?
questions.explanation: Indian spices and cloth sold in the Red Sea ports were purchased by Italian traders and eventually reached the European market.

questions.question:
Match the following:

(a) Name used collectively for Muslim merchants (i) Cosmopolitans
(b) Famous guild in 18th century (ii) Moors
(c) All caste and creed live in the city (iii) Domingo
(d) Portuguese traveller (iv) Manigramam
questions.explanation:
1. Moors was a name used collectively for Muslim merchants
  2. Domingo Paes was a Portuguese traveller
  3. Surat city was cosmopolitan and people of all castes and creeds lived there
  4. There were several guilds in south India from the eighth century onwards - the most famous being the Manigramam

questions.question:
Arrange the following steps for making the bronze statue:

  1. An image was made of wax
  2. This was covered with clay and allowed to dry
  3. Once the metal cooled and solidified
  4. Next, it was heated, and a tiny hole was made in the clay cover
  5. The molten wax was drained out through this hole
  6. Then molten metal was poured into the clay mould through the hole
  7. The clay cover was carefully removed, and the image was cleaned and polished
questions.explanation:
Chola bronze statues were made using the “lost wax” technique.
First, an image was made of wax. This was covered with clay and allowed to dry. Next, it was heated, and a tiny hole was made in the clay cover. The molten wax was drained out through this hole. Then the molten metal was poured into the clay mould through the hole. Once the metal cooled and solidified, the clay cover was carefully removed, and the image was cleaned and polished.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Towns Temples
(a) Somnath (i) Andhra Pradesh
(b) Kanchipuram (ii) Gujarat
(c) Tirupati (iii) Tamil Nadu
questions.explanation: Towns emerged around temples such as Somnath in Gujarat, Kanchipuram in Tamil Nadu, and Tirupati in Andhra Pradesh.

questions.question: Which of the following statement is true?

options.html: Merchants preferred to travel individually rather than in caravans
Name of the architect of the Rajarajeshvara temple from an inscription
Kabul was the major centre for trade in elephants
Surat was an important trading port in the Bay of Bengal

questions.explanation: We know the name of the architect of the Rajarajeshvara temple from an inscription.

questions.question: Murshidabad became the capital of West Bengal in 1704 as it was a centre for ________.
questions.explanation: Murshidabad (West Bengal) on the banks of the Bhagirathi, which rose to prominence as a centre for silks and became the capital of Bengal in 1704.

questions.question: Which of the following is false regarding the networks of the small towns?

options.html: Many came from far and near to these towns to buy local articles and sell products of distant places
From the Fifth century onwards the subcontinent was dotted with several small towns
These probably emerged from large villages. They usually had a Mandapika (or mandi of later times) to which nearby villagers brought their produce to sell
They also had market streets called hatta (haat of later times) lined with shops

questions.explanation: From the eighth century onwards the subcontinent was dotted with several small towns.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a temple town?
questions.explanation: Thanjavur is also an example of a temple town. Temple towns represent a very important pattern of urbanisation, the process by which cities develop.

questions.question:
Match the following:

(a) Cairo (i) Iraq
(b) Basra (ii) Egypt
(c) Antwerp (iii) Belgium
questions.explanation: Surat Hundis were honoured in the far-off markets of Cairo in Egypt, Basra in Iraq, and Antwerp in Belgium.

questions.question: What was the part of the town where artisans and merchants of Indian origin used to live?
questions.explanation: Crafts and commerce underwent major changes as merchants and artisans (such as weavers) were moved into the Black Towns established by the European companies within these new cities.
