questions.question: Under the rule of ________, the hierarchy between social classes grew further in Delhi.
questions.explanation: Under the Delhi Sultans and the Mughals, this hierarchy between social classes grew further.

questions.question: Tribes obtained their livelihood in various ways except which of the following?
questions.explanation: Many tribes obtained their livelihood from agriculture. Others were hunter-gatherers or herders.

questions.question: The ________ and ________ depended on each other for their diverse needs. This relationship, of conflict and dependence, gradually caused both societies to change.
questions.explanation: The caste-based and tribal societies also depended on each other for their diverse needs. This relationship, of conflict and dependence, gradually caused both societies to change.

questions.question: Khokhar tribes were in ________.
questions.explanation: In Punjab, the Khokhar tribe was very influential during the thirteenth and fourteenth centuries.

questions.question: ________ refers to a group of families or households claiming descent from a common ancestor.
questions.explanation: A clan is a group of families or households claiming descent from a common ancestor. The tribal organization is often based on kinship or clan loyalties.

questions.question: In which part, the shepherd tribe of Gaddis lived?
questions.explanation: Shepherd tribe of Gaddis lived in the Western Himalaya.

questions.question: Raja Mansingh was the famous general of ________.
questions.explanation: Raja Man Singh, Akbar’s famous general, attacked and defeated the Cheros in 1591.

questions.question: The emergence of states is closely related to ________ change amongst tribal people.
questions.explanation: The emergence of states is closely related to social change amongst tribal people.

questions.question: Mundas and Santals lived in the region of:
questions.explanation: The Mundas and Santals were among the other important tribes that lived in this region and also in Orissa and Bengal.

questions.question: The large tribe of Bhils was spread across:
questions.explanation: The large tribe of Bhils was spread across western and central India. By the late sixteenth century, many of them had become settled agriculturists and some even zamindars. Many Bhil clans, nevertheless, remained hunter-gatherers.

questions.question: Identify the following figure :-
questions.explanation: It shows - Bhils hunting deer by night.

questions.question: Nomads are ________ people.
questions.explanation: Nomads are wandering people. Many of them are pastoralists who roam from one pasture to another with their flocks and herds.

questions.question: Following are the itinerant groups except:
questions.explanation: Nomads are wandering people. Many of them are pastoralists who roam from one pasture to another with their flocks and herds. Whereas, itinerant groups, such as craftspersons, pedlars and entertainers travel from place to place practising their different occupations.

questions.question: Identify the metal of which this crocodile statue is made up of
questions.explanation: It is Bronze crocodile by Kutiya Kond tribe, Orissa.

questions.question: The Bronze crocodile is made by ____________ tribe.
questions.explanation: It is made by Bronze crocodile, Kutiya Kond tribe, Orissa

questions.question: Specialised artisans, smiths, carpenters and masons were recognised as separate jati by the ________.
questions.explanation: Specialised artisans - smiths, carpenters, and masons - were also recognised as separate jatis by the Brahmanas.

questions.question: The Gonds lived in a vast forested region called ________.
questions.explanation: The Gonds lived in a vast forested region called Gondwana - or “country inhabited by Gonds”.

questions.question: Cultivation in which trees and bushes in a forest area are first cut and burnt. The crop is sown in the ashes. When this land loses its fertility, another plot of land is cleared and planted in the same way. It is called-
questions.explanation: Shifting cultivation Trees and bushes in a forest area are first cut and burnt. The crop is sown in the ashes. When this land loses its fertility, another plot of land is cleared and planted in the same way.

questions.question: The Gond kingdom was divided into ________.
questions.explanation: The Gond kingdom was divided into Garhs.

questions.question: Which Gond raja of Garha Katanga, assumed the title of Sangram Shah?
questions.explanation: Aman Das, the Gond raja of Garha Katanga, assumed the title of Sangram Shah.

questions.question: Durgawati was the daughter of:

options.html: Ram Das, the Chandel Rajput raja
Aman Das, the Chandel Rajput raja

questions.explanation: Sangram Shah's son, Dalpat, married princess Durgawati, the daughter of Salbahan, the Chandel Rajput raja of Mahoba.

questions.question: Bastar is in which of the following state?
questions.explanation: Bastar is in Madhya Pradesh.

questions.question: Emperor ____ wrote in his memoirs that the Banjaras carried grain on their bullocks from different areas and sold it in towns
questions.explanation: Emperor Jahangir wrote in his memoirs that the Banjaras carried grain on their bullocks from different areas and sold it in towns. They transported food grain for the Mughal army during military campaigns. With a large army there could be 100,000 bullocks carrying grain.

questions.question: The rise of which clans to the position of rulers set an example for the tribal people to follow many dominant tribes of Punjab, Sind and the North-West Frontier had adopted Islam quite early?
questions.explanation: The rise of Rajput clans to the position of rulers set an example for the tribal people to follow.

questions.question: To whom the Gonds kingdom was granted by the Mughals?
questions.explanation: When the Mughals defeated the Gonds, they captured a huge booty of precious coins and elephants. They annexed part of the kingdom and granted the rest to Chandra Shah, an uncle of Bir Narain.

questions.question: Bhuiyans means:
questions.explanation: Bhuiyans means landlords.

questions.question: The Ahom state depended upon forced labour. Those forced to work for the state were called ________.
questions.explanation: The Ahom state depended upon forced labour. Those forced to work for the state were called Paiks.

questions.question: Kabui tribe live in which of the following state?
questions.explanation: The 33 scheduled tribes of Manipur are Aimol, Anal, Angami, Chiru, Chothe, Gangte, Hmar, Kabui, Kacha Naga, Koirao, Koireng, Kom, Lamkang, Mao, Maram, Maring, Any Mizo tribe (Lushei), Monsang, Moyon, Paite, Purum, Ralte, Sema, Simte, Sahlte (Sukte), Tangkhul, Thadou, Vaiphei, Zou, Kharam, Tarao, Poumai Naga, and Any Kuki tribe.

questions.question: Ahoms believe in ________.
questions.explanation: Hinduism became the predominant religion among the Ahoms.

questions.question: Many societies did not follow the social rules and rituals prescribed by the Brahmanas. Nor were they divided into numerous unequal classes. Such societies are often called ________.
questions.explanation: Many societies in the subcontinent did not follow the social rules and rituals prescribed by the Brahmanas. Nor were they divided into numerous unequal classes. Such societies are often called tribes.

questions.question: Choose the false statement with regard to the tribal people.

options.html: Customs were not passed down to each new generation
They did not keep written records

questions.explanation: A few exceptions apart, tribal people did not keep written records. But they preserved rich customs and oral traditions. These were passed down to each new generation.

questions.question: Chief of the Gakkhars tribe was ________.
questions.explanation: Gakkhars's chief, Kamal Khan Gakkhar, was made a noble (mansabdar) by Emperor Akbar.

questions.question: Following are the tribes of east India except:
questions.explanation: The Langah is a Muslim tribe and is settled Punjab, Sindh, Balochistan, Lasbela in Pakistan, and in the states of Gujarat and Rajasthan in India.

questions.question: In many areas of present-day Bihar and Jharkhand, Chero chiefdoms had emerged by the ________.
questions.explanation: In many areas of present-day Bihar and Jharkhand, Chero chiefdoms had emerged by the twelfth century.

questions.question: Raja Man Singh, Akbar's famous general, attacked and defeated the Cheros in the year ________.
questions.explanation: Raja Man Singh, Akbar’s famous general, attacked and defeated the Cheros in 1591.

questions.question: Under Aurangzeb, Mughals forces captured many ___ fortresses and subjugated the tribe.
questions.explanation: Under Aurangzeb, Mughal forces captured many Chero fortresses and subjugated the tribe.

questions.question: The Maharashtra highlands and Karnataka were home to:
questions.explanation: The Maharashtra highlands and Karnataka were home to Kolis, Berads and numerous others.

questions.question: Which of the following is tribe of the South region?
questions.explanation: Further south there were large tribal populations of Koragas, Vetars, Maravars, and many others.

questions.question: The large tribe of Bhils was spread across western and central India. By the late sixteenth century, many of them had become:

options.html: Settled agriculturists and some even zamindars
Started making pottery and some even craftsman
Started making pottery and some even zamindars
Settled agriculturists and some even craftsmen

questions.explanation: By the late sixteenth century, many of them had become settled agriculturists and some even zamindars.

questions.question: Identify the picture and what does it depict about the tribes?

options.html: A chain of mobile traders connected India to the outside world

questions.explanation: A chain of mobile traders connected India to the outside world. Here you see nuts being gathered and loaded on the backs of camels. Central Asian traders brought such goods to India and the Banjaras and other traders carried these to local markets.

questions.question: The Banjaras were the most important trader nomads. Their caravan was called ________.
questions.explanation: The Banjaras were the most important trader nomads. Their caravan was called tanda.

questions.question: Who used the Banjaras to transport grain to the city markets?
questions.explanation: Sultan Alauddin Khalji used the Banjaras to transport grain to the city markets.

questions.question: Who transported food grain for the Mughals army during military campaigns?
questions.explanation: Banjaras transported food grain for the Mughal army during military campaigns. With a large army, there could be 100,000 bullocks carrying grain.

questions.question: ________ rather than varnas became the basis for organising society.
questions.explanation: Jatis, rather than varna, became the basis for organising society.

questions.question: Tiruchirapalli taluka is in present-day ________.
questions.explanation: A twelfth-century inscription from Uyyakondan Udaiyar, in Tiruchirapalli taluka (in present-day Tamil Nadu), describes the deliberations in a sabha of Brahmanas.

questions.question: Among the ________, new Rajput clans became powerful by the eleventh and twelfth centuries.
questions.explanation: Among the Kshatriyas, new Rajput clans became powerful by the eleventh and twelfth centuries.

questions.question: Rajput belongs to different lineages except which one of them?
questions.explanation: They belonged to different lineages, such as Hunas, Chandelas, Chalukyas and others.

questions.question: The Akbar Nama, a history of Akbar's reign, mentions the Gond kingdom of Garha Katanga that had ________.
questions.explanation: The Akbar Nama, a history of Akbar’s reign, mentions the Gond kingdom of Garha Katanga that had 70,000 villages.

questions.question: Each Garh was controlled a particular Gond clan. This was further divided into units of 84 villages called ________ which was further subdivided into the unit of 12 villages each called ________.
questions.explanation: Each Garh was controlled by a particular Gond clan. This was further divided into units of 84 villages called Chaurasi. The Chaurasi was subdivided into Barhots which were made up of 12 villages each.

questions.question: Salbahan was the:
questions.explanation: Salbahan was the Chandel Rajput raja of Mahoba.

questions.question: Identify the following carved door made by which tribe-
questions.explanation: A carved door, made by Gond tribe, Bastar area, Madhya Pradesh.

questions.question: Who was the son of Durgawati and Dalpat?
questions.explanation: Dalpat, however, died early. Rani Durgawati was very capable and started ruling on behalf of her five-year-old son, Bir Narain.

questions.question: In which year the Mughals forces under Asaf Khan attacked Garha Katanga?
questions.explanation: In 1565, the Mughal forces under Asaf Khan attacked Garha Katanga. Strong resistance was put up by Rani Durgawati. She was defeated and preferred to die rather than surrender.

questions.question: Ahoms migrated to the Brahmaputra valley from present-day ________ in the thirteenth century.
questions.explanation: The Ahoms migrated to the Brahmaputra valley from present-day Myanmar in the thirteenth century.

questions.question: In 1662 who defeated the Ahom kingdom?
questions.explanation: In 1662, the Mughals under Mir Jumla attacked the Ahom kingdom. Despite their brave defence, the Ahoms were defeated. But direct Mughal control over the region could not last long.

questions.question: Identify the following figure:-
questions.explanation: Ear ornaments,by Koboi Naga tribe, Manipur

questions.question: The ear ornament figure has been taken from which tribe?
questions.explanation: Ear ornaments, made by Koboi Naga tribe, Manipur.

questions.question: Historical works, known as Buranjis, were also written first in the Ahom language and then in ________.
questions.explanation: Historical works, known as Buranjis, were also written - first in the Ahom language and then in Assamese.

questions.question: The best-known pastoral and hunter-gatherer tribe in history were the ________.
questions.explanation: The best-known pastoral and hunter-gatherer tribe in history were the Mongols. They inhabited the grasslands (steppes) of Central Asia and the forested areas further north.

questions.question: In Punjab, the Khokhar tribe was very influential during the ________.
questions.explanation: In Punjab, the Khokhar tribe was very influential during the thirteenth and fourteenth centuries.

questions.question: Kolis live in following regions except ________.
questions.explanation: The Maharashtra highlands and Karnataka were home to Kolis, Berads and numerous others. Kolis also lived in many areas of Gujarat.

questions.question: Kamal Khan Gakkhar was made a noble (Mansabdar) by Emperor ________.
questions.explanation: Gakkhars chief, Kamal Khan Gakkhar, was made a noble (mansabdar) by Emperor Akbar.

questions.question: Raja Man Singh defeated the Cheros in 1591 and a large amount of ________ was taken from them.
questions.explanation: Raja Man Singh, Akbar’s famous general, attacked and defeated the Cheros in 1591. A large amount of booty was taken from them, but they were not entirely subdued.

questions.question: In Multan and Sind, the ________ dominated extensive regions before they were subdued by the Mughals.
questions.explanation: In Multan and Sind, the Langahs and Arghuns dominated extensive regions before they were subdued by the Mughals.

questions.question: The Gonds were founding great numbers across the present-day states of:

options.html: Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, and Andhra Pradesh
Chhattisgarh, Assam, Gujarat and Andhra Pradesh
Chhattisgarh, Assam, Gujarat and Uttar Pradesh
Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, and Uttar Pradesh

questions.explanation: The Gonds were found in great numbers across the present-day states of Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, and Andhra Pradesh.

questions.question: Which of the following statement is false?

options.html: Aman Das was the Gond Raja of Garha Katanga
Many dominated tribes of Punjab, Sind, etc adopted Hinduism
Gond kingdoms were later defeated by Banjaras

questions.explanation: Hinduism became the predominant religion for Ahoms.

questions.question: Who replaced the older rulers, especially in agricultural areas?
questions.explanation: Among the Kshatriyas, new Rajput clans became powerful by the eleventh and twelfth centuries. They belonged to different lineages, such as Hunas, Chandelas, Chalukyas, and others. Some of these, too, had been tribes earlier. Many of these clans came to be regarded as Rajputs. They gradually replaced the older rulers, especially in agricultural areas.

questions.question:
Match the following:

State Tribes
(a) Punjab (i) Mundas and Santals
(b) Orissa and Bengal (ii) Gonds
(c) Chhattisgarh and Madhya Pradesh (iii) Khokhars
questions.explanation:
1. In Punjab, the Khokhar tribe was very influential during the thirteenth and fourteenth centuries
  2. The Mundas and Santals were among the other important tribes that lived in this region and also in Orissa and Bengal.
  3. The Gonds were found in great numbers across the present-day states of Chhattisgarh, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, and Andhra Pradesh

questions.question: Akbar Nama was written by ________.
questions.explanation: The Akbar Nama, a history of Akbar’s reign, was written by Abul Fazl.

questions.question: Banjaras transported food grain for the Mughals army during military campaigns. With a large army, there could be:
questions.explanation: They transported food grain for the Mughal army during military campaigns. With a large army, there could be 100,000 bullocks carrying grain.

questions.question: Who described about the Banjaras?
questions.explanation: Peter Mundy, an English trader who came to India during the early seventeenth century, has described the Banjaras: In the morning we met a tanda of Banjaras with14,000 oxen. They were all laden with grains such as wheat and rice. These Banjaras carry their household - wives, and children – along with them. One tanda consists of many families. Their way of life is similar to that of carriers who continuously travel from place to place. They own their oxen. They are sometimes hired by merchants, but most commonly they are themselves, merchants. They buy grain where it is cheaply available and carry it to places where it is dearer. From there, they again reload their oxen with anything that can be profitably sold in other places. In a tanda, there may be as many as 6 or 7 hundred persons. They do not travel more than 6 or 7 miles a day - that, too, in the cool weather. After unloading their oxen, they turn them free to graze as there is enough land here, and no one there to forbid them.

questions.question: Which of the following is true regarding Ahoms?

options.html: They did not use fire arms
By the first-half of 17th century, the administration becomes quite centralised

questions.explanation: By the first half of the seventeenth century, the administration became quite centralised.

questions.question: Ahoms annexed the kingdoms of the ________ and the ________.
questions.explanation: During the sixteenth century, they annexed the kingdoms of the Chhutiyas (1523) and of Koch-Hajo (1581) and subjugated many other tribes.

questions.question: By 1206 who had united the Mongol and Turkish tribes into a powerful military force?
questions.explanation: By 1206 Genghis Khan had united the Mongol and Turkish tribes into a powerful military force.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Tribal Region Tribes
(a) South Tribes (i) Gakkhars, Janjuhas, Langahs
(b) Eastern Tribes (ii) Ahoms, Nagas, Khasis
(c) North Tribes (iii) Bhils, Koyas, Katkaris
questions.explanation:
1. In Multan and Sind, the Langahs and Arghuns dominated extensive regions and Ghakkars in Punjab.
  2. The distant north-eastern part of the subcontinent too was entirely dominated by tribes – the Nagas, Ahoms, and many others.
  3. The Maharashtra highlands and Karnataka were home to Kolis, Berads, and numerous others. Kolis also lived in many areas of Gujarat. Further south there were large tribal populations of Koragas, Vetars, Maravars, and many others.

questions.question: Shankra was an advocate of-
questions.explanation: Shankara one of the most influential philosophers of India, was born in Kerala in the eighth century. He was an advocate of Advaita or the doctrine of the oneness of the individual soul and the Supreme God which is the Ultimate Reality.

questions.question: Identify from which manuscript the below figure is taken
questions.explanation: A page from a south Indian manuscript of the Bhagavadgita.

questions.question: Which deities were came to be worshipped through elaborate rituals?
questions.explanation: Shiva, Vishnu, and Durga as supreme deities came to be worshipped through elaborate rituals.

questions.question: Nayanars were:
questions.explanation: Nayanars were (saints devoted to Shiva)

questions.question: Sangam literature is the ________ literature.
questions.explanation: Sangam literature is the earliest example of Tamil literature, composed during the early centuries of the Common Era.

questions.question: There were ________ Nayanars and ________ Alvars.
questions.explanation: There were 63 Nayanars, who belonged to different caste backgrounds such as potters, “untouchable” workers, peasants, hunters, soldiers, Brahmanas, and chiefs. There were 12 Alvars, who came from equally divergent backgrounds.

questions.question: Hagiography means:
questions.explanation: Hagiography means writing of saint's lives.

questions.question: Who was born in Tamil Nadu in the eleventh century and was deeply influenced by the Alvars?
questions.explanation: Ramanuja, born in Tamil Nadu in the eleventh century, was deeply influenced by the Alvars. According to him the best means of attaining salvation was through intense devotion to Vishnu.

questions.question: Virashaiva movement began in ________.
questions.explanation: Virashaiva movement initiated by Basavanna and his companions like Allama Prabhu and Akkamahadevi. This movement began in Karnataka in the mid-twelfth century. The Virashaivas argued strongly for the equality of all human beings and against Brahmanical ideas about caste and the treatment of women. They were also against all forms of ritual and idol worship.

questions.question:
The following are Vachanas or sayings attributed to ________
"The rich,
Will make temples for Shiva.,
What shall I,
A poor man,
Do? ,
My legs are pillars,
The body the shrine,
The head a cupola,
Of gold. ,
Listen, O Lord of the meeting rivers,
Things standing shall fall,
But the moving ever shall stay,"
questions.explanation: These are Vachanas or sayings attributed to Basvanna.

questions.question: Which amongst the following are the saints of Maharashtra?
questions.explanation: From the thirteenth to the seventeenth centuries Maharashtra saw a great number of saint-poets, whose songs in simple Marathi continue to inspire people. The most important among them were Janeshwar, Namdev, Eknath and Tukaram as well as women like Sakkubai and the family of Chokhamela, who belonged to the “untouchable” Mahar caste.

questions.question: Which temple is located in Pandharpur?
questions.explanation: From the thirteenth to the seventeenth centuries Maharashtra saw a great number of saint-poets, whose songs in simple Marathi continue to inspire people. The most important among them were Janeshwar, Namdev, Eknath and Tukaram as well as women like Sakkubai and the family of Chokhamela, who belonged to the “untouchable” Mahar caste. This regional tradition of bhakti focused on the Vitthala (a form of Vishnu) temple in Pandharpur.

questions.question: They are Vaishnavas who understand the pain of others. Who said this line?
questions.explanation: A new humanist idea emerged as they insisted that bhakti lay in sharing others’ pain. As the famous Gujarati saint Narsi Mehta said, “They are Vaishnavas who understand the pain of others.”

questions.question: Sufis were ________ mystics.
questions.explanation: The saints had much in common with the Sufis, so much so that it is believed that they adopted many ideas of each other. Sufis were Muslim mystics. They rejected outward religiosity and emphasised love and devotion to God and compassion towards all fellow human beings.

questions.question: Muslim scholars developed a holy law called ________.
questions.explanation: Islam propagated strict monotheism or submission to one God. It also rejected idol worship and considerably simplified rituals of worship into prayers. At the same time, Muslim scholars developed a holy law called Shariat.

questions.question:
Match the following:

(a) Zikr (i) chanting of a name or sacred formula
(b) Sama (ii) dancing
(c) Raqs (iii) singing
questions.explanation: Like the Nathpanthis, Siddhas, and Yogis, the Sufis too believed that the heart can be trained to look at the world in a different way. They developed elaborate methods of training using zikr (chanting of a name or sacred formula), contemplation, Sama(singing), Raqs (dancing), discussion of parables, breath control, etc. under the guidance of a master or Pir.

questions.question: From where the below mention page is taken?
questions.explanation: It is a page from a manuscript of the Quran, Deccan, late fifteenth century.

questions.question: House of rest for travellers, especially one kept by a religious order.
questions.explanation: Hospice: House of rest for travellers, especially one kept by a religious order. The Sufi masters held their assemblies in their Khanqahs or hospices.

questions.question: The Sufi masters held their assemblies in their ________.
questions.explanation: House of rest for travellers, especially one kept by a religious order. The Sufi masters held their assemblies in their Khanqahs or hospices.

questions.question: Jalaluddin Rumi was a great thirteenth-century Sufi poet from ________.
questions.explanation: Jalaluddin Rumi was a great thirteenth-century Sufi poet from Iran who wrote in Persian.

questions.question: Who wrote Ramcharitmanas?
questions.explanation: Tulsidas conceived of God in the form of Rama. Tulsidas’s composition, the Ramcharitmanas, written in Awadhi (a language used in eastern Uttar Pradesh), is important both as an expression of his devotion and as a literary work.

questions.question: Sangam literature composed:

options.html: During the centuries of the after Common Era
During the late centuries of the Common Era
During the early centuries of the Common Era
During the Middle centuries of the Common Era

questions.explanation: The Sangam literature (the earliest example of Tamil literature, composed during the early centuries of the Common Era).

questions.question: Mirabai was a ________ princess.
questions.explanation: Mirabai was a Rajput princess married into the royal family of Mewar in the sixteenth century. Mirabai became a disciple of Ravidas, a saint from a caste considered “untouchable”. She was devoted to Krishna and composed innumerable bhajans expressing her intense devotion.

questions.question: Who amongst the following was not the Nayanar saint?
questions.explanation:
There were 63 Nayanars, who belonged to different caste backgrounds such as potters, “untouchable” workers, peasants, hunters, soldiers, Brahmanas, and chiefs. The best known among them were Appar, Sambandar, Sundarar, and Manikkavasagar. There are two sets of compilations of their songs - Tevaram and Tiruvacakam.
There were 12 Alvars, who came from equally divergent backgrounds, the best known being Periyalvar, his daughter Andal, Tondaradippodi Alvar, and Nammalvar. Their songs were compiled in the Divya Prabandham.

questions.question: The sacred space created by Guru Nanak was known as Dharmsala, which is now known as:
questions.explanation: The sacred space thus created by Guru Nanak was known as dharmsala. It is now known as Gurdwara.

questions.question: Who amongst the following was not the Sikh Guru?
questions.explanation: Before his death in 1539, Guru Nanak appointed one of his followers as his successor. His name was Lehna but he came to be known as Guru Angad, signifying that he was a part of Guru Nanak himself. Guru Angad compiled the compositions of Guru Nanak, to which he added his own in a new script known as Gurmukhi. The three successors of Guru Angad also wrote under the name of “Nanak” and all of their compositions were compiled by Guru Arjan in 1604. To this compilation were added the writings of other figures like Shaikh Farid, Sant Kabir, Bhagat Namdev, and Guru Tegh Bahadur. In 1706 this compilation was authenticated by his son and successor, Guru Gobind Singh. It is now known as Guru Granth Sahib, the holy scripture of the Sikhs.

questions.question: Surdas was an ardent devotee of ________.
questions.explanation: Surdas was an ardent devotee of Krishna.

questions.question: Shankaradeva composed poems and plays in which language?
questions.explanation: Surdas was an ardent devotee of Krishna. His compositions, compiled in the Sursagara, Surasaravali, and Sahitya Lahari, express his devotion. Also contemporary was Shankaradeva of Assam (late fifteenth century) who emphasised devotion to Vishnu, and composed poems and plays in the Assamese.

questions.question: Mirabai became a disciple of ________, a saint from a caste considered untouchable.
questions.explanation: Mirabai was a Rajput princess married into the royal family of Mewar in the sixteenth century. Mirabai became a disciple of Ravidas, a saint from a caste considered “untouchable”. She was devoted to Krishna and composed innumerable bhajans expressing her intense devotion.

questions.question: Whose bhajan are preserved in the Guru Granth Sahib, Panch Vani, and Bijak?
questions.explanation: Kabir, who probably lived in the fifteenth-sixteenth centuries, was one of the most influential saints. We have little reliable information about his life. We get to know of his ideas from a vast collection of verses called Sakhis and pads said to have been composed by him and sung by wandering bhajan singers. Some of these were later collected and preserved in the Guru Granth Sahib, Panch Vani, and Bijak.

questions.question: The Sikh movement began to get politicized in the seventeenth century, a development which culminated in the institution of the Khalsa by:
questions.explanation: The Sikh movement began to get politicized in the seventeenth century, a development which culminated in the institution of the Khalsa by Guru Gobind Singh in 1699. The community of the Sikhs, called the Khalsa Panth, became a political entity.

questions.question:
Following is the composition of ________
O Allah-Ram present in all living beings
Have mercy on your servants, O Lord!
Why bump your head on the ground,
Why bathe your body in water?
You kill and you call yourself humble?
But your vices you conceal.
Twenty-four times the Brahmana keeps
the Ekadasi fast
While the Qazi observes the Ramzan
Tell me why does he set aside the eleven
months
To seek spiritual fruit in the twelfth?
Hari dwells in the East, they say
And Allah resides in the West,
Search for him in your heart, in the heart
of your heart;
There he dwells, Rahim-Ram
questions.explanation: 'In search of the True Lord', It is a composition of Kabir.

questions.question: Which of the following is an Alvar saint?
questions.explanation: There were 12 Alvars, who came from equally divergent backgrounds, the best known being Periyalvar, his daughter Andal, Tondaradippodi Alvar, and Nammalvar. Their songs were compiled in the Divya Prabandham.

questions.question: Supreme God who could deliver humans from such bondage if approached with devotion (or bhakti). This idea, advocated in the ________, grew in popularity in the early centuries of the Common Era.
questions.explanation: Supreme God who could deliver humans from such bondage if approached with devotion (or bhakti). This idea, advocated in the Bhagavadgita, grew in popularity in the early centuries of the Common Era.

questions.question: Pulaiyar and the Panars were the caste of ________.
questions.explanation: The seventh to ninth centuries saw the emergence of new religious movements, led by the Nayanars (saints devoted to Shiva) and Alvars (saints devoted to Vishnu) who came from all castes including those considered “untouchable” like the Pulaiyar and the Panars.

questions.question: Pulaiyar and the Panars were sharply critical of the ________.
questions.explanation: The seventh to ninth centuries saw the emergence of new religious movements, led by the Nayanars (saints devoted to Shiva) and Alvars (saints devoted to Vishnu) who came from all castes including those considered “untouchable” like the Pulaiyar and the Panars. They were sharply critical of the Buddhists and Jainas and preached ardent love of Shiva or Vishnu as the path to salvation.

questions.question: Identify the following, It is the Bronze image of
questions.explanation: It is an bronze image of Manikkavasagar.

questions.question: Between the ________ and ________ centuries the Chola and Pandya kings built elaborate temples.
questions.explanation: Between the tenth and twelfth centuries, the Chola and Pandya kings built elaborate temples around many of the shrines visited by the saint-poets, strengthening the links between the bhakti tradition and temple worship.

questions.question:
Following is the composition of ________
"Into my vile body of flesh
You came, as though it were a temple of gold,
And soothed me wholly and saved me,
O Lord of Grace, O Gem most Pure,
Sorrow and birth and death and illusion
You took from me and set me free.
O Bliss! O Light! I have taken refuge in You,
And never can I be parted from You"
questions.explanation: "The devotee and the Lord"- This is a composition of Manikkavasagar.

questions.question: Who propounded the doctrine of Vishishtadvaita?
questions.explanation: Ramanuja, born in Tamil Nadu in the eleventh century, was deeply influenced by the Alvars. According to him the best means of attaining salvation was through intense devotion to Vishnu. Vishnu in His grace helps the devotee to attain the bliss of union with Him. He propounded the doctrine of Vishishtadvaita or qualified oneness in that the soul even when united with the Supreme God remained distinct.

questions.question: Following has initiated the Virashaiva movement except:
questions.explanation: The Virashaiva movement began in Karnataka in the mid-twelfth century. It was initiated by Basavanna and his companions like Allama Prabhu and Akkamahadevi. This movement strongly argued for the equality of all human beings. It was against Brahmanical ideas about caste and the treatment of women. They were also against all forms of ritual and idol worship.

questions.question: Who rejected all forms of ritualism, outward display of piety, and social differences based on birth? In fact, they even rejected the idea of renunciation and preferred to live with their families, earning their livelihood like any other person.
questions.explanation: The most important among them were Janeshwar, Namdev, Eknath and Tukaram as well as women like Sakkubai and the family of Chokhamela, who belonged to the “untouchable” Mahar caste. In fact, they even rejected the idea of renunciation and preferred to live with their families, earning their livelihood like any other person, while humbly serving fellow human beings in need.

A slave

questions.question: Whose songs became popular with the masses in Rajasthan and Gujarat?
questions.explanation: Mirabai was a Rajput princess married into the royal family of Mewar in the sixteenth century. Mirabai became a disciple of Ravidas, a saint from a caste considered “untouchable”. She was devoted to Krishna and composed innumerable bhajans expressing her intense devotion. Her songs also openly challenged the norms of the “upper” castes and became popular with the masses in Rajasthan and Gujarat.

questions.question:
Match the following:

GOD Personalities
(a) Vishnu (i) Basavanna Sundarar, Periyalvar, his daughter Andal, Tondaradippodi Alvar and Nammalvar
(b) Shiva (ii) Shankaradeva, Appar, Sambandar, Sundarar and Manikkavasagar
(c) Krishna (iii) Surdas, Chaitanyadeva, Mirabai
questions.explanation:
1. Vishnu - Alvars saints devoted to Vishnu)-Periyalvar, his daughter Andal, Tondaradippodi Alvar, and Nammalvar.
  2. Shiva - Shankaradeva of Assam (late fifteenth century) emphasised devotion to Vishnu Appar, Sambandar, Sundarar, and Manikkavasagar.
  3. Krishna - Surdas was an ardent devotee of Krishna. Chaitanyadeva, a sixteenth-century bhakti saint from Bengal, preached selfless devotion to Krishna-Radha Mirabai became a disciple of Ravidas, a saint from a caste considered “untouchable”. She was devoted to Krishna and composed innumerable bhajans expressing her intense devotion.

questions.question: Which Mughal ordered the execution of Guru Arjan in 1606?
questions.explanation: The Mughal emperor Jahangir looked upon them as a potential threat and he ordered the execution of Guru Arjan in 1606. The Sikh movement began to get politicized in the seventeenth century, a development which culminated in the institution of the Khalsa by Guru Gobind Singh in 1699.

questions.question: Who was Chokhamela?

options.html: They were Muslim saints belong to Delhi
They belong to untouchable Mahar caste

questions.explanation: From the thirteenth to the seventeenth centuries Maharashtra saw a great number of saint-poets, whose songs in simple Marathi continue to inspire people. The most important among them were Janeshwar, Namdev, Eknath and Tukaram as well as women like Sakkubai and the family of Chokhamela, who belonged to the “untouchable” Mahar caste.

questions.question: The below mention figure is taken from the Bible written by the Martin Luther King in which language
questions.explanation: The above image is Title page of the German Bible translated by Martin Luther

questions.question:
Match the following:

(a) monotheism (i) submission to one God
(b) Vishishtadvaita (ii) qualified oneness in that the soul, even when united with the Supreme God remained distinct
(c) Advaita (iii) the doctrine of the oneness of the individual soul and the Supreme God which is the Ultimate Reality
questions.explanation:
1. Monotheism: submission to one God.
  2. Vishishtadvaita:  qualified oneness in that the soul, even when united with the Supreme God remained distinct. Ramanuja was an advocate of Vishishtaadvaita.
  3. Advaita:  the doctrine of the oneness of the individual soul and the Supreme God which is the Ultimate Reality. Shankara was an advocate of Advaita.

questions.question:
Match the following
Sufi Masters
(a) Qutbuddin Bakhtiar Kaki
(b) Baba Farid
(c) Bandanawaz Gisudaraz
Region
(1) Gulbarga
(2) Punjab
(3) Delhi
questions.explanation: The Chishti silsila was among the most influential orders. It had a long line of teachers like Khwaja Muinuddin Chishti of Ajmer, Qutbuddin Bakhtiar Kaki of Delhi, Baba Farid of Punjab, Khwaja Nizamuddin Auliya of Delhi and Bandanawaz Gisudaraz of Gulbarga

questions.question: Who rewrote the Gita in Marathi?
questions.explanation: Bhagavad Gita is part of Mahabharata written by Veda Vyasa Dnyaneshwar (1275-1296 CE) translated and commented on the Gita in Marathi, in his book Dnyaneshwari.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Dates Personalities
(a) 16th and 17th Century (i) Tulsidas
(b) 15th and 16th Century (ii) Namdev
(c) 13th and 14th Century (iii) Kabir
questions.explanation:
1. Tulsidas: Tulsidas, the author of Ramcharitmanas was born in Rajpur in the Banda district of Uttar Pradesh in the 16th century. 16th-17th Century writer.
  2. Namdev- Narsi Namdev is a village in Hingoli taluka of Hingoli district of the Indian state of Maharashtra. 13th-14th Century.
  3. Kabir-Kabir, who probably lived in the fifteenth-sixteenth centuries, was one of the most influential saints.

questions.question: Which of the following statement regarding Kabir is False?

options.html: Guru Nanak rejected the orthodox religions
Ramanujaaa was born in Tamil Nadu
Kabir was brought up in the family of farmers

questions.explanation: Kabir, who probably lived in the fifteenth-sixteenth centuries, was one of the most influential saints. He was brought up in a family of Muslim Julahas or weavers settled in or near the city of Benares (Varanasi).

questions.question:
Arrange the following statements in the order of formation of Guru Granth Sahib

  1. Guru Arjan compiled the compositions written by the successors of Guru Angad
  2. Guru Gobind Singh authenticated the compilation which came to be known as Guru Granth Sahib
  3. Guru Angad compiled the composition of Guru Nanak
  4. The writing of Shaik Farid, Sank Kabir, Bhagat Namdev, and Guru Teghbahadur Singh was added to Guru Arjan's compilation
questions.explanation: Before his death in 1539, Guru Nanak appointed one of his followers as his successor. His name was Lehna but he came to be known as Guru Angad, signifying that he was a part of Guru Nanak himself. Guru Angad compiled the compositions of Guru Nanak, to which he added his own in a new script known as Gurmukhi. The three successors of Guru Angad also wrote under the name of “Nanak” and all of their compositions were compiled by Guru Arjan in 1604. To this compilation were added the writings of other figures like Shaikh Farid, Sant Kabir, Bhagat Namdev, and Guru Tegh Bahadur. In 1706 this compilation was authenticated by his son and successor, Guru Gobind Singh. It is now known as Guru Granth Sahib, the holy scripture of the Sikhs.

questions.question: Shankara, one of the most influential philosophers of India, was born in Kerala in the ________.
questions.explanation: Shankara, one of the most influential philosophers of India, was born in Kerala in the eighth century. He was an advocate of Advaita or the doctrine of the oneness of the individual soul and the Supreme God which is the Ultimate Reality.

questions.question:
Which of the following included in the philosophy of Shankara

  1. He was an advocate of Advaita or the doctrine of the oneness of the individual soul
  2. He propounded the doctrine of Vishishtadvaita
  3. He considered the world around us to be an illusion or maya,
  4. The best means of attaining salvation was through intense devotion to Vishnu
questions.explanation: Shankara, one of the most influential philosophers of India, was born in Kerala in the eighth century. He was an advocate of Advaita or the doctrine of the oneness of the individual soul and the Supreme God which is the Ultimate Reality. He taught that Brahman, the only or Ultimate Reality, was formless and without any attributes. He considered the world around us to be an illusion or maya, and preached renunciation of the world and adoption of the path of knowledge to understand the true nature of Brahman and attain salvation.

questions.question: Nathpanthis, Siddhas and Yogis believe in the following ideas-

options.html: To them, the path to salvation lay in meditation on the formless Ultimate Reality and the realisation of oneness with
They advocated renunciation of the world
They advocated intense training of the mind and body through practices like yogasnas, breathing exercises, and meditation

questions.explanation: Nathpanthis, Siddhacharas and Yogis. They advocated renunciation of the world. To them, the path to salvation lay in meditation on the formless Ultimate Reality and the realisation of oneness with it. To achieve this they advocated intense training of the mind and body through practices like yogasanas, breathing exercises, and meditation. These groups became particularly popular among “low” castes. Their criticism of conventional religion created the ground for devotional religion to become a popular force in northern India.

questions.question: Jaipur is in which state?
questions.explanation: Jaipur is the capital of India’s Rajasthan state.

questions.question: The temple theatre of Kerala built by Chera borrowed stories from the ________.
questions.explanation: The Cheras drew upon Sanskritic traditions. The temple theatre of Kerala, which is traced to this period, borrowed stories from the Sanskrit epics.

questions.question: Lilatilakam deals with ________.
questions.explanation: A fourteenth-century text, the Lilatilakam, dealing with grammar and poetics, was composed in Manipravalam - literally, “diamonds and corals” referring to the two languages, Sanskrit and the regional language.

questions.question: Lilatilakam, was composed in ________.
questions.explanation: A fourteenth-century text, the Lilatilakam, dealing with grammar and poetics, was composed in Manipravalam.

questions.question: Jagannatha temple is in ________.
questions.explanation: The Shree Jagannath Temple of Puri is an important Hindu temple dedicated to Lord Jagannath, a form of Lord Vishnu, located on the eastern coast of India, at Puri in the state of Odisha.

questions.question: Viharas were related to ________.
questions.explanation: Viharas were a Buddhist temple or monastery.

questions.question: Attribution of living soul to plants, inanimate objects, and natural phenomena-
questions.explanation: Animism means the belief that all natural things, such as plants, animals, rocks, and thunder, have spirits and can influence human events.

questions.question: In 1230, Who dedicated his kingdom to the deity and proclaimed himself as the deputy of the god
questions.explanation: In 1230, King Anangabhima III dedicated his kingdom to the deity and proclaimed himself as the "deputy" of the god.

questions.question: Identify the temple situated at Puri
questions.explanation: The given image is of the Shree Jagannath Temple of Puri is an important Hindu temple dedicated to Lord Jagannath, a form of Lord Vishnu

questions.question: Which state was called Rajputana by the British?
questions.explanation: Rajputana was a princely state under the British rule now a Rajasthan state.

questions.question: Identify the following figure :-
questions.explanation: The given image is of Prince Raj Singh of Bikaner.

questions.question: Prithviraj was the ________ ruler.
questions.explanation: Prithviraj Chauhan was a Rajput king who ruled the kingdoms of Ajmer and Delhi in northern India in the 12th century.

questions.question:
Match the following:

State Place
(a) Rajasthan (i) Awadh
(b) West Bengal (ii) Bankura
(c) Lucknow (iii) Mewar
questions.explanation:
1. Mewar is a region of south-central Rajasthan state in western India
  2. Bankura district is an administrative unit in the Indian state of West Bengal
  3. Awadh known in British historical texts as Avadh is in Lucknow

questions.question: Kathak, now associated with several parts of ________.
questions.explanation: Kathak is the classical dance form that prevails in the North of India.

questions.question: Kathak was developed in two traditions or gharanas:

options.html: In the courts of Kerala and the other in Lucknow
In the courts of Rajasthan and the other in Lucknow
In the courts of Gujarat and the other in Orissa
In the courts of Rajasthan and the other in Kerala

questions.explanation: Under the Mughal emperors and their nobles, Kathak was performed in the court, where it acquired its present features and developed into a form of dance with a distinctive style. Subsequently, it developed in two traditions or gharanas: one in the courts of Rajasthan (Jaipur) and the other in Lucknow.

questions.question: Miniatures are:
questions.explanation: Miniatures (as their very name suggests) are small-sized paintings, generally done in water colour on cloth or paper.

questions.question: The Mughal emperors ________ patronised highly skilled painters who primarily illustrated manuscripts containing historical accounts and poetry.
questions.explanation: The Mughal emperors Akbar, Jahangir and Shah Jahan patronised highly skilled painters who primarily illustrated manuscripts containing historical accounts and poetry. These were generally painted in brilliant colours and portrayed court scenes, scenes of battle or hunting, and other aspects of social life.

questions.question: The earliest miniatures were on:
questions.explanation: The earliest miniatures were on palm leaves or wood. Some of the most beautiful of these, found in western India.

questions.question: Paintings portrayed the following except:
questions.explanation: Painters generally painted in brilliant colours and portrayed court scenes, scenes of battle or hunting, and other aspects of social life.

questions.question: Nadir Shah's invasion and the conquest of Delhi in ________.
questions.explanation: Nadir Shah’s invasion and the conquest of Delhi in 1739 resulted in the migration of Mughal artists to the hills to escape the uncertainties of the plains.

questions.question: Magadha is in ________.
questions.explanation: Magadha was an ancient kingdom located on the Indo-Gangetic plains in eastern India and spread over what is today the modern state of Bihar.

questions.question: Xuan Zang was a ________.
questions.explanation: Xuanzang was a Chinese Buddhist monk, scholar, traveller, and translator who travelled to India in the seventh century.

questions.question: The Brihaddharma Purana, a thirteenth-century Sanskrit text from Bengal, permitted the local Brahmanas to eat certain varieties of ________.
questions.explanation: Brahmanas were not allowed to eat non-vegetarian food, but the popularity of fish in the local diet made the Brahmanical authorities relax this prohibition for the Bengal Brahmanas. The Brihaddharma Purana, a thirteenth-century Sanskrit text from Bengal, permitted the local Brahmanas to eat certain varieties of fish.

questions.question: By the early nineteenth century, in Rumania school textbooks began to be written in ________ rather than in ________.
questions.explanation: By the early nineteenth century, in Rumania school textbooks began to be written in Rumanian rather than in Greek, and in Hungary, Hungarian was adopted as the official language instead of Latin.

questions.question: Bengali is derived from ________.
questions.explanation: Bengali is now recognised as a language derived from Sanskrit.

questions.question: Mangalakavyas is a ________.
questions.explanation: Early Bengali literature may be divided into two categories - one indebted to Sanskrit and the other independent of it. The first includes translations of the Sanskrit epics, the Mangalakavyas (literally auspicious poems, dealing with local deities).

questions.question: ________ is a popular regional deity, often worshipped in the form of a stone or a piece of wood.
questions.explanation: Stories concerning the worship of Dharma Thakur reveals that Dharma Thakur is a popular regional deity, often worshipped in the form of a stone or a piece of wood.

questions.question: A Persian word meaning a spiritual guide is ________.
questions.explanation: Pir or Peer is a title for a Sufi master or spiritual guide.

questions.question: In Bengal Officials and functionaries received land and often set up ________ that served as centres for religious transformation in these areas.
questions.explanation: Local communities of fisherfolk and shifting cultivators, often tribals, merged with the new communities of peasants. This coincided with the establishment of Mughal control over Bengal with their capital in the heart of the eastern delta at Dhaka. Officials and functionaries received land and often set up mosques that served as centres for religious transformation in these areas.

questions.question: Bengal also witnessed a temple-building spree from the late ____ century, which culminated in the _____ century
questions.explanation: Bengal also witnessed a temple-building spree from the late fifteenth century, which culminated in the nineteenth century.

it was devoted to God Jagannatha

In some temples, particularly in Vishnupur in the Bankura district of West Bengal.
The comparatively more complex three-roofed structure, three triangular roofs placed on the three walls move up to converge on a curved line or a point.
Temples were usually built on a square platform.

questions.question: The Chera kingdom of Mahodayapuram was established in the ninth century in the south-western part of the peninsula, part of present-day.
questions.explanation: The Chera kingdom of Mahodayapuram was established in the ninth century in the south-western part of the peninsula, part of present-day Kerala. It is likely that Malayalam was spoken in this area.

questions.question: The first literary works in Malayalam, dated to about the twelfth century, are directly indebted to ________.
questions.explanation: The first literary works in Malayalam, dated to about the twelfth century, are directly indebted to Sanskrit.

questions.question: Identify the icons in the following figure

questions.explanation: The Brihaddharma Purana, a thirteenth-century Sanskrit text from Bengal, permitted the local Brahmanas to eat certain varieties of fish.

questions.explanation: In the twelfth century, one of the most important rulers of the Ganga dynasty, Anantavarman, decided to erect a temple for Purushottama Jagannatha at Puri.

Dutch, the Rajput, and the English East India
Mughals, the Marathas and the Dutch
Rajput, the French and English East India

questions.question: The term Kathak is derived from Katha, a word used in _______.
questions.explanation: The term Kathak is derived from Katha, a word used in Sanskrit and other languages for story.

Story-tellers in temples of north India
Weavers in temples of north India
Dancers in temples of south India

questions.explanation: The kathaks were originally a caste of story-tellers in temples of north India, who embellished their performances with gestures and songs.

questions.question: Under ________, Kathak was performed in the court, where it acquired its present features and developed into a form of dance with a distinctive style.
questions.explanation: Under the Mughal emperors and their nobles, Kathak was performed in the court, where it acquired its present features and developed into a form of dance with a distinctive style. Subsequently, it developed in two traditions or gharanas.

questions.explanation: Under the patronage of Wajid Ali Shah, the last Nawab of Awadh, Kathak grew into a major art form.

questions.question: Identify the dance form on the following figure
questions.explanation: The given image is of the Kathak dancers, a court painting.

questions.question: Another region that attracted miniature paintings was the Himalayan foothills around the modern-day state of ________.
questions.explanation: By the late seventeenth century the region of Himalayan foothills had developed a bold and intense style of miniature around the modern-day state of Himachal.

questions.question: By the late seventeenth century Himalayas region had developed a bold and intense style of miniature painting called:
questions.explanation: The region that attracted miniature paintings was the Himalayan foothills. By the late seventeenth century this region had developed a bold and intense style of miniature painting called Basohli.

questions.question: By the mid ________ century the Kangra artists developed a style which breathed a new spirit into miniature painting.
questions.explanation: By the mid-eighteenth century the Kangra artists developed a style which breathed a new spirit into miniature painting. The source of inspiration was the Vaishnavite traditions.

questions.question: Which two colours were used in Kangra Paintings?
questions.explanation: Soft colours including cool blues and greens and lyrical treatment of themes distinguished Kangra painting.

questions.question: From the fourth-third centuries BCE, commercial ties began to develop between ________.
questions.explanation: From the fourth-third centuries BCE, commercial ties began to develop between Bengal and Magadha (South Bihar), which may have led to the growing influence of Sanskrit.

questions.question: During the fourth century, the ________ rulers established political control over north Bengal.
questions.explanation: During the fourth century, the Gupta rulers established political control over north Bengal and began to settle Brahmanas in this area.

questions.question: The Chinese traveller Xuan Zang came in which century?
questions.explanation: Xuanzang was a Chinese Buddhist monk, scholar, traveller, and translator who travelled to India in the seventh century.

questions.question: In ________, Akbar conquered Bengal.
questions.explanation: In 1586, when Akbar conquered Bengal, it formed the nucleus of the Bengal Suba.

questions.question: A wide range of non-Sanskrit words, derived from a variety of sources including ________, have become part of modern Bengali.
questions.explanation: Although Bengali is derived from Sanskrit, it passed through several stages of evolution. Also, a wide range of non-Sanskrit words, derived from a variety of sources including tribal languages, Persian, and European languages, have become part of modern Bengali.

questions.question: Nath literature include the following except:

fairy tales, folk tales and ballads
the songs of Maynamati and Gopichandra

questions.explanation: Nath literature includes the songs of Maynamati and Gopichandra, stories concerning the worship of Dharma Thakur, and fairy tales, folk tales and ballads.

A three-roofed temple with a tower

Lady is preparing food for the consumption

questions.explanation: Plaques were made using Terracotta.

questions.explanation: Dressed for domestic consumption, terracotta plaque is from the Vishalakshi temple, Arambagh.

questions.question: Between the fourteenth and sixteenth centuries, Bengal was ruled by:
questions.explanation: Between the fourteenth and sixteenth centuries, Bengal was ruled by Sultans who were independent of the rulers in Delhi.

questions.question: From the eighth century, ________ became the centre of a regional kingdom under the Palas.
questions.explanation: From the eighth century, Bengal became the centre of a regional kingdom under the Palas.

questions.question: During Fifteenth century ________ was the language of administration, ________ developed as regional language.
questions.explanation: While Persian was the language of administration, Bengali developed as a regional language.

questions.question: Which language was used in the Chera kingdom of Mahodayapuram?
questions.explanation: The Chera kingdom of Mahodayapuram was established in the ninth century in the south-western part of the peninsula, part of present-day Kerala. It is likely that Malayalam was spoken in this area.

questions.question: Identify the below figure in which language inscription is written
questions.explanation: The given image an early Kerala inscription, composed in Malayalam.

questions.question: Identify the following figure, what is seen in the figure
questions.explanation: The given image is of Krishna with gopis,terracotta plaque from the Shyamaraya temple, Vishnupur.

questions.question: Some of the most beautiful miniature paintings found in western India were used to illustrate ________ texts.
questions.explanation: The earliest miniatures were on palm leaves or wood. Some of the most beautiful of these, found in western India, were used to illustrate Jaina texts.

questions.question: Identify the below figure of dance class
questions.explanation: The below image is of Dance class, Lakshmana temple, Khajuraho.

questions.question:
Match the following:

State Place
(a) Tamil Nadu (i) Bharatanatyam
(b) Andhra Pradesh (ii) Kuchipudi
(c) Kerala (iii) Kathakali
questions.explanation:
Dance forms that are recognised as classical at present are:

  1. Bharatanatyam (Tamil Nadu)
  2. Kathakali (Kerala)
  3. Kuchipudi (Andhra Pradesh)

questions.question: Identify the below figure

options.html: A page from a palm-leaf manuscript of the earliest Sanskrit Ramayana
A page from a palm-leaf manuscript of the earliest Tamil Ramayana
A page from a palm-leaf manuscript of the earliest Bengali Ramayana
A page from a palm-leaf manuscript of the earliest Hindi Ramayana

questions.explanation: It is a A page from a palm-leaf manuscript of the earliest Bengali Ramayana.

questions.question: Identify what is seen in the below mention maintain

options.html: Place of living for the elephants

questions.explanation: The given is the Mughal miniature of  Akbar resting during a hunt.

questions.question: Identify the below miniature and what is seen in it

options.html: Maharana Ram Singh II playing holi

questions.explanation: The given miniature is the Rajput Miniature of Maharana Ram Singh II playing Holi, Kota.

questions.question:
Match the following:

State Place
(a) Orissa (1) Ananatavarman
(b) Kerala (2) Mangalkavya
(c) Bengal (3) Lilatilakam
questions.explanation:
1. Anantavarman, decided to erect a temple for Purushottama Jagannatha at Puri,Orissa
  2. The temple theatre of Kerala, which is traced to this period, borrowed stories from the Sanskrit epics. The first literary works in Malayalam, dated to about the twelfth century, are directly indebted to Sanskrit. Interestingly enough, a fourteenth-century text, the Lilatilakam, dealing with grammar and poetics, was composed in Manipravalam – literally, “diamonds and corals” referring to the two languages, Sanskrit and the regional language.
  3. Early Bengali literature may be divided into two categories – one indebted to Sanskrit and the other independent of it. The first includes translations of the Sanskrit epics, the Mangalakavyas.

questions.question: Which of the following is false-

options.html: From the eighth century, Bengal became the centre of a regional kingdom under the Palas.
Mughal control over Bengal with their capital in the heart of the eastern delta at Dhaka.
The Chera kingdom of Mahodayapuram was established in the ninth century in the south-western part of the peninsula.
In 1230, king Anangabhima II dedicated his kingdom to the deity and proclaimed himself as the deputy of the god.

questions.explanation: In 1230, King Anangabhima III dedicated his kingdom to the deity and proclaimed himself as the "deputy" of the god.

tests.name: Eighteenth Century Political Formations Test 01
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 07 Social Science Eighteenth Century Political Formations CBSE Online Test 01 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: When Aurangzeb died?
questions.explanation: He Died a Natural death in 1707.

questions.question: Why the Mughals empire was facing problems in closing years of the 17th century?

options.html: Mughals military resources were very powerful
Britishers were under the control of Mughals

questions.explanation: The Mughal Empire reached the height of its success and started facing a variety of crises towards the closing years of the seventeenth century. These were caused by a number of factors like the financial resources of his empire by fighting a long war in the Deccan.

questions.question: Mughals empire started declining its power under the reign of:
questions.explanation: Mughals empire started declining its power under the region of Aurangzeb. Aurangzeb had depleted the military and financial resources of his empire by fighting a long war in the Deccan.

questions.question: 9-10 percent of the land revenue paid to the head revenue collector in the Deccan called:
questions.explanation: Maratha Kings possessed the right to levy Sardeshmukhi in the entire region. Sardeshmukhi means 9-10 percent of the land revenue paid to the head revenue collector in the Deccan.

questions.question: It became difficult for Mughals emperors to keep a check on _________.
questions.explanation: Under his successors, the efficiency of the imperial administration broke down. It became increasingly difficult for the later Mughal emperors to keep a check on their powerful mansabdars.

questions.question: Murshid Quli Khan was Nawab of ________.
questions.explanation: Murshid Quli Khan, also known as Mohammad Hadi, was the first Nawab of Bengal.

questions.question: Who controlled the offices of revenue and military administration?
questions.explanation: Nobels appointed by governors (subadars) often controlled the offices of revenue and military administration (Diwani and Faujdari) as well.

questions.question: The Mughals emperors after ________ were unable to arrest the gradual shifting of political and economic authority into the hands of provincial governors, local chieftains, and other groups.
questions.explanation: Rebellion groups were able to seize the economic resources of the region to consolidate their positions. The Mughal emperors after Aurangzeb were unable to arrest the gradual shifting of political and economic authority into the hands of provincial governors, local chieftains, and other groups.

questions.question: Ahmad Shah Abdali was the ________ ruler.
questions.explanation: Ahmed Shah Abdali was the first of the Saddozai rulers of Afghanistan.

questions.question: Peacock throne was looted by:
questions.explanation: The wealth looted from the Mughal treasury by Nadir Shah included the peacock throne.

questions.question: Asaf Jah and Murshid Quli Khan held a Zat rank of:
questions.explanation: Sa‘adat Khan (Awadh) and Asaf Jah (Hyderabad) had occupied high Mansabdari positions and enjoyed the trust and confidence of the emperors. Both held a Zat rank of 7,000 each.

questions.question: Nizam-ul-Mulk Asaf Jah, the founder of ________ state.
questions.explanation: Nizam-ul-Mulk, a Mughal nobleman who founded the Hyderabad State in India.

questions.question: The state of Hyderabad was constantly engaged in a struggle against the ________ to the west.
questions.explanation: The state of Hyderabad was constantly engaged in a struggle against the Marathas to the west and with independent Telugu warrior chiefs (Nayakas) of the plateau.

questions.question: Telugu warrior chiefs were called ________.
questions.explanation: Independent Telugu warrior chiefs were called Nayakas.

questions.question: Burhan-ul-Mulk Saadat Khan was appointed subadar of:
questions.explanation: Burhan-ul-Mulk Sa‘adat Khan was appointed subadar of Awadh in 1722 and founded a state which was one of the most important to emerge out of the break-up of the Mughal Empire.

tests.name: Eighteenth Century Political Formations Test 02
tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 07 Social Science Eighteenth Century Political Formations CBSE Online Test 02 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.
  • The test will consist of only objective type multiple choice questions requiring students to mouse-click their correct choice of the options against the related question number.
  • For every question there will be only one correct option out of the four given options.
  • Each question carries one mark. There is no negative marking for incorrect choice.
  • For moving to the next question, click on "Next" at the bottom of the screen.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Burhan-ul-Mulk also held the combined offices of:
questions.explanation: Burhan-ul-Mulk also held the combined offices of Subadari, Diwani, and Faujdari. In other words, he was responsible for managing the political, financial, and military affairs of the province of Awadh.

questions.question: Revenue farmers were called ________.
questions.explanation: Ijaradars were the revenue farmers who have the right to collect taxes.

questions.question: ________ broke away from Mughal control under Murshid Quli Khan who was appointed as the naib, deputy to the governor of the province.
questions.explanation: Bengal gradually broke away from Mughal control under Murshid Quli Khan who was appointed as the naib, deputy to the governor of the province. Although never a formal subadar, Murshid Quli Khan very quickly seized all the power that went with that office.

questions.question: The organisation of the Sikhs into a political community during the seventeenth century helped in regional state-building in the ________.
questions.explanation: The organisation of the Sikhs into a political community during the seventeenth century helped in regional state-building in the Punjab. Several battles were fought by Guru Gobind Singh against the Rajput and Mughal rulers, both before and after the institution of the Khalsa in 1699.

questions.question: In an effort to reduce the Mughal influence in Bengal Murshid Quli Khan transferred all Mughal Jagirdars to:
questions.explanation: Murshid Quli Khan very quickly seized all the power that went with that office. Like the rulers of Hyderabad and Awadh, he also commanded the revenue administration of the state. In an effort to reduce the Mughal influence in Bengal he transferred all Mughal Jagirdars to Orissa and ordered a major reassessment of the revenues of Bengal.

questions.question: Under whose reign the banking house of Jagat Seth became extremely prosperous.
questions.explanation: The close connection between the state and bankers - noticeable in Hyderabad and Awadh as well – was evident in Bengal under the rule of Alivardi Khan. During his reign, the banking house of Jagat Seth became extremely prosperous.

questions.question: Many Rajput kings, had served under the Mughals with distinction particularly those belonging to:
questions.explanation: Many Rajput kings, particularly those belonging to Amber and Jodhpur, had served under the Mughals with distinction. In exchange, they were permitted to enjoy considerable autonomy in their Watan jagirs.

questions.question: Ajit Singh was the ruler of ________.
questions.explanation: Maharajah Ajit Singh was the ruler of Marwar, Jodhpur, Rajasthan.

questions.question: Sawai Raja Jai Singh founded his new capital at:
questions.explanation: Sawai Raja Jai Singh founded his new capital at Jaipur and was given the Subadari of Agra in 1722.

questions.question: Who was given the Subadari of Agra in 1722?
questions.explanation: Sawai Raja Jai Singh founded his new capital at Jaipur and was given the Subadari of Agra in 1722.

questions.question: Guru Gobind Singh died in-
questions.explanation: Guru Gobind Singh Ji left His physical body in 1708, at Abchal Nagar, Hazoor Sahib (Nanded, Maharashtra, India).

questions.question: In 1708, the Khalsa rose in revolt against the Mughal authority under whose leadership, declared their sovereign rule?
questions.explanation: After Guru Gobind Singh's death in 1708, the Khalsa rose in revolt against the Mughal authority under Banda Bahadur’s leadership, declared their sovereign rule by striking coins in the name of Guru Nanak and Guru Gobind Singh, and established their own administration between the Sutlej and the Jamuna.

questions.question: The Sikh territories in the late eighteenth century extended from the Indus to the Jamuna but they were divided under different rulers. One of them, Maharaja Ranjit Singh, reunited these groups and established his capital at:
questions.explanation: The Sikh territories in the late eighteenth century extended from the Indus to the Jamuna but they were divided under different rulers. One of them, Maharaja Ranjit Singh, reunited these groups and established his capital at Lahore in 1799.

questions.question: Chauth means:

options.html: 40 percent of the land revenue claimed by zamindars
44 percent of the land revenue claimed by zamindars
25 percent of the land revenue claimed by zamindars
4 percent of the land revenue claimed by zamindars

questions.explanation: Chauth means 25 percent of the land revenue claimed by zamindars. In the Deccan, this was collected by the Marathas.

questions.question: Give the timeline of The French Revolution:
questions.explanation: The French Revolution was a watershed event in modern European history that began in 1789 and ended in 1794.

questions.question: Jawahir Shah was the son of ________.
questions.explanation: Jawahir Shah was the son of Nadir Shah.

questions.question: By ________ the British had successfully grabbed major chunks of territory in eastern India.
questions.explanation: The boundaries of the Mughal Empire were reshaped by the emergence of a number of independent kingdoms. By 1765, another power, the British, had successfully grabbed major chunks of territory in eastern India.

questions.question: The third battle of Panipat was fought in:
questions.explanation: The Third Battle of Panipat took place on 14 January 1761 at Panipat, about 60 miles north of Delhi.

questions.question: Mughals empire started facing a variety of crises towards the closing years of the ________ century.
questions.explanation: The Mughal Empire reached the height of its success and started facing a variety of crises towards the closing years of the seventeenth century.

questions.question:
Match the following:

(a) Governors (1) Diwani
(b) Military administration (2) Faujdari
(c) Revenue administration (3) Subadars
questions.explanation: Nobels appointed as governors (subadars) often controlled the offices of revenue and military administration (Diwani and Faujdari) as well.

questions.question: Because of increase in taxes, Peasant and zamindari rebellions in many parts of _______ added to the problems.
questions.explanation: Peasant and zamindari rebellions in many parts of northern and western India added to the problems.These revolts were sometimes caused by the pressures of mounting taxes

questions.question: Identify whose portrait is shown
questions.explanation: The given picture is A 1779 portrait of Nadir Shah.

questions.question: Nadir Shah was the ruler of:
questions.explanation: Nader Shah Afshar (August 1688 - 19 June 1747) was one of the most powerful Iranian ruler.

questions.question: Nadir Shah invasion was followed by a series of plundering raids by:
questions.explanation: In the midst of this economic and political crisis, the ruler of Iran, Nadir Shah, sacked and plundered the city of Delhi in 1739 and took away immense amounts of wealth. This invasion was followed by a series of plundering raids by the Afghan ruler Ahmad Shah Abdali, who invaded north India five times between 1748 and 1761.

questions.question: How many times Ahmad Shah Abdali invaded India and from where?
questions.explanation: A series of plundering raids by the Afghan ruler Ahmad Shah Abdali, who invaded north India five times between 1748 and 1761.

questions.question: Ahmad Shah Abdali  invaded India in between ________.
questions.explanation: Ahmad Shah Abdali invaded India in between 1748 and 1761.

questions.question:
Read the below mention line and Identify about whose invasion these descriptions have been given:
(those) who had been masters were now in dire straits, and those who had been revered couldn't even (get water to quench their thirst. The recluses were pulled out of their corners. The wealthy were turned into beggars. Those who once set the style in clothes now went naked, and those who owned property were now homeless? The New City (Shahjahanabad) was turned into rubble. (Nadir Shah) then attacked the Old quarters of the city and destroyed a whole world that existed there.
questions.explanation: The given description is when Nadir Shah attacks Delhi. The account described is the invasion’s impact upon Delhi.

questions.question: Mughal Empire was further weakened by competition amongst different groups of nobles. They were divided into two major groups or factions-
questions.explanation: Under severe pressure from all sides, the empire was further weakened by competition amongst different groups of nobles. They were divided into two major groups or factions, the Iranis and Turanis (nobles of Turkish descent).

questions.question: Which two Mughal emperors were blinded by their nobles?

options.html: Ahmad Shah and Shah Alam II
Farrukh Siyar and Shah Alam II
Farrukh Shah II and Farrukh Siyar I

questions.explanation: The worst possible humiliation came when Ahmad Shah (1748-1754) and Shah Alam II (1759-1816) were blinded by their nobles.

questions.question: Which of the following states did not break their tie with Mughals?
questions.explanation: Awadh, Bengal, and Hyderabad were although extremely powerful and quite independent, the rulers of these states did not break their formal ties with the Mughal emperor.
