questions.question: States had enjoyed considerable independence under the Mughals as Watan jagirs. These included several:
questions.explanation: The states of the eighteenth century can be divided into several overlapping groups. One was where the States that enjoyed considerable independence under the Mughals as Watan jagirs. These included several Rajput principalities.

questions.question: States under the control of ___, ______, ________ were of differing sizes and had seized their independence from the Mughals after a long-drawn armed struggle.
questions.explanation: The last group included states under the control of Marathas, Sikhs and others like the Jats. These were of differing sizes and had seized their independence from the Mughals after a long-drawn armed struggle.

questions.question: Nizam-ul-Mulk Asaf Jah, the founder of Hyderabad state, was one of the most powerful members at the court of the Mughal Emperor?
questions.explanation: Nizam-ul-Mulk Asaf Jah, the founder of Hyderabad state, was one of the most powerful members at the court of the Mughal Emperor Farrukh Siyar. He was entrusted first with the governorship of Awadh, and later given charge of the Deccan.

questions.question: Asaf Jah brought skilled soldiers and administrators from ________ India who welcomed the new opportunities in the ________.
questions.explanation: Asaf Jah brought skilled soldiers and administrators from northern India who welcomed the new opportunities in the south. He appointed Mansabdars and granted jagirs.

questions.question: The ambitions of the Nizam to control the rich:

options.html: Textile-producing areas of the Coromandel coast
Wheat-producing areas of the Coromandel coast
Wheat-producing areas of the Malabar coast

questions.explanation: The ambitions of the Nizam to control the rich textile-producing areas of the Coromandel coast in the east were checked by the British who were becoming increasingly powerful in that region.

questions.question: Awadh was a prosperous region, controlling the rich alluvial Ganga plain and the main trade route between ________.
questions.explanation: Awadh was a prosperous region, controlling the rich alluvial Ganga plain and the main trade route between North India and Bengal.

questions.question: The close connection between the state and bankers noticeable in Hyderabad and Awadh as well was evident in Bengal under the rule of:
questions.explanation: The close connection between the state and bankers noticeable in Hyderabad and Awadh as well - was evident in Bengal under the rule of Alivardi Khan (1740-1756).

questions.question: Alivardi Khan ruled from:
questions.explanation: Aliwardi khan ruled from 1740 and his reign ended by 1756.

questions.question: Panipat and Ballabhgarh became important trading centres in the areas dominated by:
questions.explanation: The Jats were prosperous agriculturists, and towns like Panipat and Ballabhgarh became important trading centres in the areas dominated by them.

questions.question: Raja Ajit Singh of Jodhpur held the governorship of ________ and Sawai Raja Jai Singh of Amber was governor of ________.
questions.explanation: Influential Rajput families claimed the Subadari of the rich provinces of Gujarat and Malwa. Raja Ajit Singh of Jodhpur held the governorship of Gujarat and Sawai Raja Jai Singh of Amber was governor of Malwa.

questions.question: Who campaigns into Rajasthan from the 1740s put severe pressure on these principalities and checked their further expansion?
questions.explanation: Sawai Raja Jai Singh founded his new capital at Jaipur and was given the Subadari of Agra in 1722. Maratha campaigns into Rajasthan from the 1740s put severe pressure on these principalities and checked their further expansion.

questions.question: Several battles were fought by ________ against the Rajput and Mughal rulers, both before and after the institution of the Khalsa in 1699.
questions.explanation: Several battles were fought by Guru Gobind Singh against the Rajput and Mughal rulers, both before and after the institution of the Khalsa in 1699.

questions.question: Identify the figure of below mention Sikh Guru
questions.explanation: The given figure is of Guru Gobind Singh, the tenth guru

questions.question: Under a number of able leaders in the eighteenth century, the Sikhs organized themselves into a number of bands called ________ and later on ________.
questions.explanation: Under a number of able leaders in the eighteenth century, the Sikhs organized themselves into a number of bands called Jathas, and later on Misls. Their combined forces were known as the grand army (dal Khalsa).

questions.question: ________ carved out a stable kingdom with the support of powerful warrior families.
questions.explanation: Shivaji (1627-1680) carved out a stable kingdom with the support of powerful warrior families (Deshmukhs).

questions.question: After Shivaji's death, effective power in the Maratha state was wielded by a family of Chitpavan Brahmanas who served Shivaji's successors as:
questions.explanation: After Shivaji’s death, effective power in the Maratha state was wielded by a family of Chitpavan Brahmanas who served Shivaji’s successors as Peshwa (or principal minister).

questions.question: ________ became the capital of the Maratha kingdom.
questions.explanation: The Maratha kingdom was another powerful regional kingdom to arise out of sustained opposition to the Mughal rule. Poona became the capital of the Maratha kingdom.

questions.question: Between ________, the Maratha empire expanded.
questions.explanation: Between 1720 and 1761, the Maratha empire expanded. It gradually chipped away at the authority of the Mughal Empire.

questions.question: Nadir Shah, sacked and plundered the city of Delhi in ________.
questions.explanation: In the midst of this economic and political crisis, the ruler of Iran, Nadir Shah, sacked and plundered the city of Delhi in 1739 and took away immense amounts of wealth.

questions.question: Banda Bahadur was captured in ________ and executed in ________.
questions.explanation: After Guru Gobind Singh's death in 1708, the Khalsa rose in revolt against the Mughal authority under Banda Bahadur’s leadership. He was captured in 1715 and executed in 1716.

questions.question: Influential Rajput families claimed the Subadari of the rich provinces of Gujarat and Malwa. Raja Ajit Singh of Jodhpur held the governorship of Gujarat and Sawai Raja Jai Singh of Amber was governor of Malwa. These offices were renewed by:
questions.explanation: These influential Rajput families claimed the Subadari of the rich provinces of Gujarat and Malwa. Raja Ajit Singh of Jodhpur held the governorship of Gujarat and Sawai Raja Jai Singh of Amber was governor of Malwa. These offices were renewed by Emperor Jahandar Shah in 1713.

questions.question: Which of the following is false?

options.html: Nadir Shah was the ruler of Afghan
Ahmad Shah Abdali invaded north India
Saadat Khan founded in the state of Awadh
Sikh territories extended from Indus to Jammu

questions.explanation: Nadir shah was the ruler of Iran.

questions.question: Hampi was the capital of which empire?
questions.explanation: Hampi is located in the Krishna-Tungabhadra basin that formed the nucleus of the Vijayanagara Empire, founded in 1336.

questions.question: Identify the below mention picture

options.html: Farrukh Siyar receiving a noble in court
Ahmad Shah receiving a noble in court
Shah Alam II receiving a noble in court
Alamgir II receiving a noble in court

questions.explanation: The given picture is of Farrukh Siyar (1713-1719) receiving a noble in court.

questions.question: Nadir Shah looted ________ lakhs of rupees and some thousand ________ coins, nearly one crore worth of gold-ware, nearly ________ crores worth of jewels, most of them unrivalled in the world, and the above Included the Peacock throne.
questions.explanation:
The devastation of Delhi after Nadir Shah’s invasion was described by contemporary observers. One described the wealth looted from the Mughal treasury as follows:
Sixty lakhs of rupees and some thousand gold coins, nearly one crore worth of gold-ware, nearly fifty crores worth of jewels, most of them unrivalled in the world, and the above included the Peacock throne.

questions.question:
Read the below mention description and identify the king for whom these lines have been said
..was at the height of his power. He was the governor of Agra for 12 years and of Malwa for 5 or 6 years. He possessed a large army, artillery, and great wealth. His sway extended from Delhi to the banks of the Narmada.
questions.explanation: A description of Raja Jai Singh in a Persian account of 1732: Raja Jai Singh was at the height of his power. He was the governor of Agra for 12 years and of Malwa for 5 or 6 years. He possessed a large army, artillery, and great wealth. His sway extended from Delhi to the banks of the Narmada.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Mughal Ruler Year (Birth-Death)
(a) Alamgir II (i) 1754-1759
(b) Ahmad Shah (ii) 1748-1754
(c) Shah Alam II (iii) 1759-1816
questions.explanation: The worst possible humiliation came when two Mughal emperors, Farrukh Siyar (1713-1719) and Alamgir II (1754-1759) were assassinated, and two others Ahmad Shah (1748-1754) and Shah Alam II (1759-1816) were blinded by their nobles.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Mughal Nobility Provinces
(a) Saadat Khan (i) Awadh
(b) Murshid Quli Khan (ii) Hyderabad
(c) Asaf Jah (iii) Bengal
questions.explanation: Amongst the states that were carved out of the old Mughal provinces in the eighteenth century, three stand out very prominently. These were Awadh, Bengal, and Hyderabad. All three states were founded by members of the high Mughal nobility who had been governors of large provinces – Sa‘adat Khan (Awadh), Murshid Quli Khan (Bengal), and Asaf Jah (Hyderabad).

questions.question: Identify the following figure
questions.explanation: The following image is of Burhan-ul-Mulk Sa‘adat Khan who was appointed subadar of Awadh in 1722

questions.question: Identify the below mention figure
questions.explanation: The following image is of Mehrangarh Fort located in Jodhpur.

questions.question: Which of the following is false regarding the Environment?

options.html: It provides the air we breathe
The environment is not our basic life support system
It provides the land where we live
It provides the water we drink, the food we eat

questions.explanation: The environment is our basic life support system.

questions.question: Which of the following is not the human-made environment?
questions.explanation: The land is a naturally made by the environment.

questions.question: Which of the following is the human environment?
questions.explanation: Community is one of the Human Components of the Environment.

questions.question: What do you mean by Abiotic?

options.html: The world of non-living elements except land
The world of living elements except land

questions.explanation: Abiotic is the world of non-living elements. e.g. land.

questions.question: Which of the following is biotic?
questions.explanation: The world of living organisms is Biotic e.g. plants and animals.

questions.question: ________ is the solid crust or the hard top layer of the earth.
questions.explanation: Made up of rocks and minerals, Lithosphere is the solid crust or the hard top layer of the earth.

questions.question: ________ must learn to live and use their environment in a harmonious way.
questions.explanation: A perfect balance is necessary between the natural and human environment. Humans must learn to live and use their environment in a harmonious way.

questions.question: Lithosphere is made up of:
questions.explanation: The lithosphere is made up of rocks and minerals and covered by a thin layer of soil. It is an irregular surface with various landforms.

questions.question: Which domain provides us forests grasslands for grazing, land for agriculture and human settlements?
questions.explanation: The lithosphere is the domain that provides us with forests, grasslands for grazing, land for agriculture, and human settlements. It is also a source of mineral wealth.

questions.question: What does the atmosphere consist of?
questions.explanation: The atmosphere consists of a number of gases, dust and water vapour.

questions.question: The domain of water is referred to as:
questions.explanation: The domain of water is referred to as hydrosphere. It comprises various sources of water and different types of water bodies.

questions.question: Which of the following is not included in the water bodies?
questions.explanation: Plains are included in the Lithosphere.

questions.question: Atmosphere means:

options.html: It is the thick layer of air that surrounds the earth
It is the solid crust or the hard top layer of the earth
It is the liquid crust or the hard top layer of the earth
It is the thin layer of air that surrounds the earth

questions.explanation: The atmosphere is the thin layer of air that surrounds the earth. The gravitational force of the earth holds the atmosphere around it.

questions.question: A ________ is necessary between the natural and human environment.
questions.explanation: Humans must learn to live and use their environment in a harmonious way. A perfect balance is necessary between the natural and human environment.

questions.question:
Which of the following are the natural environment:

  1. Plants
  2. Roads
  3. Animals
  4. House
  5. Land
  6. Water
questions.explanation: Land, water, plants, and animals comprise the natural environment.

questions.question: Which is called the living world?
questions.explanation: Plant and animal kingdom together make the biosphere or the living world.

questions.question: Which of the following is not the major domain of the earth?
questions.explanation: Lithosphere, hydrosphere, atmosphere, and biosphere are the major domain of Earth.

questions.question: Which of the following is not the human environment?
questions.explanation: The building is a Human-made component of Environment.

questions.question: Which of the following is created by nature?
questions.explanation: Some objects are created by nature - for example, mountains, rivers, trees, animals.

questions.question: What are the components of the ecosystem?
questions.explanation: The natural environment or ecosystem refers to both biotic and abiotic conditions existing on the earth.

questions.question: A special day is celebrated on 5th June-
questions.explanation: On 5 June every year, World Environment Day is celebrated.

questions.question: Which of the following is not the feature of desert ecosystem?
questions.explanation: Kangaroo is part of the forest ecosystem.

questions.question: Human beings ________ with the environment and modify it according to their ________.
questions.explanation: Early humans adapted themselves to their natural surroundings. Human beings interact with the environment and modify it according to their need.

questions.question: Which layer protects us from the scorching heat of the sun?
questions.explanation: The atmosphere protects the environment and human beings from the harmful rays and scorching heat of the sun.

questions.question: ________ revolution made ________ speedy across the world.
questions.explanation: Humans learn new ways to use and change the environment. The information revolution made communication easier and speedy across the world.

questions.question: Juicy watermelon available in ________ and hot roasted peanuts in ________.
questions.explanation: Juicy Watermelon comes into season during the summer months and hot roasted peanuts come in winter.

questions.question: Identify the following figure
questions.explanation: The given image is of a desert ecosystem where a community of organisms that live together in an environment that seems to be deserted wasteland.eg. Camel.

questions.question: Family, health, education, and politics are:
questions.explanation: Human resources comprise of family, health, education, and politics.

questions.question:
Which of the following are abiotic:

  1. Soil
  2. Air
  3. Trees
  4. Water
  5. Animal
  6. Rocks
questions.explanation: Abiotic factors are the non-living parts of the environment that can often have a major influence on living organisms. Abiotic factors include water, sunlight, air, soil, and rocks.

questions.question: What makes the biosphere?
questions.explanation: Plant and animal kingdom together make the biosphere or the living world.

questions.question: Why we should protect the environment?

options.html: Many part of the environment are already secured by the excess use of resources
It is our basic life support system
Most of the human activities protect the environment like deforestation

questions.explanation: The environment is our basic life support system. It provides the air we breathe, the water we drink, the food we eat, and the land where we live.

questions.question: What holds the environment around the earth?
questions.explanation: The atmosphere is the thin layer of air that surrounds the earth. The gravitational force of the earth holds the atmosphere around it.

questions.question: Who interacts with the environment and modify it according to their needs?
questions.explanation: Human beings interact with the environment and modify it according to their need.

questions.question: Which is a human made environment?
questions.explanation: Human made environment is the artificial environment made by Humans for comforting their lives. Example :Bridge, buildings, roads, monuments.

questions.question: Which is the source of mineral wealth?
questions.explanation: The lithosphere is made up of rocks and minerals and covered by a thin layer of soil. It is the source of mineral wealth.

questions.question: Human beings modify the natural environment except:

options.html: The car fumes pollute the air and water is collected in a pot
The land is used to build factories
They plant more and more tree

questions.explanation: Human beings modify the natural environment like the car fumes pollute the air, water is collected in a pot, food is served in vessels, and the land is used to build factories.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Component of environment Types
(a) Natural (i) Living things, air, water
(b) Human (ii) Industries, Roads, Bridges
(c) Human-made (iii) Individual, Community, Education
questions.explanation:
1. Natural components include Living things, air, water
  2. Human components comprise of Individual, Community, Education
  3. Human-made includes Industries, Roads, Bridges

questions.question: The world of a living organism is known as ________.
questions.explanation: Biotics describe living or once-living components of a community; for example organisms, such as animals and plants.

questions.question: Place, people, things, and nature that surround any living organism is called:
questions.explanation: The place, people, things, and nature that surround any living organism is called an environment. It is a combination of natural and human-made phenomena.

questions.question: Which of the following is not abiotic?
questions.explanation: Animals are a biotic element.

questions.question: The lithosphere is an irregular surface with various landforms such as:
questions.explanation: It is an irregular surface with various landforms such as mountains, plateaus, plains, valleys, etc. Landforms are found over the continents and also on the ocean floors.

questions.question: Natural environment refers to both ________ and ________ conditions existing on the earth.
questions.explanation: The environment is a combination of natural and human-made phenomena. The natural environment refers to both and abiotic conditions existing on the earth.

questions.question: All plants, animals, and human beings depend on their immediate surroundings. Often they are also interdependent on each other. This relation between the living organisms, as well as the relation between the organisms and their surroundings, form an:
questions.explanation: An ecosystem is all the plants and animals that live in a particular area together with the complex relationship that exists between them and their environment. The biotic and abiotic components interact through nutrient cycles and energy flows.

questions.question: Environment word is taken from ________ word.
questions.explanation: The word Environment is derived from the French word “Environ” which means “surrounding”.

questions.question: Environ meaning:
questions.explanation: The word Environment is derived from the French word “Environ” which means “surrounding” or "neighbourhood".

options.html: activities, destruction, and interactions among human beings
activities, destruction, and independence among human beings
activities, creations and independence among human beings
activities, creations, and interactions among human beings

questions.explanation: While the natural environment refers to both biotic and abiotic conditions existing on the earth human environment reveals the activities, creations, and interactions among human beings.

questions.question:
Which of the following is false regarding the Lithosphere?

  1. It is a regular surface with various landforms such as mountains, plateaus, plains, valleys, etc.
  2. It is made up of rocks and minerals and covered by a thin layer of soil.
  3. Landforms are found over the continents
  4. Landforms are found also on the ocean floors
questions.explanation: It is an irregular surface with various landforms such as mountains, plateaus, plains, valleys, etc.

questions.question: Which domain protects us from the harmful rays and scorching heat of the sun?
questions.explanation: The atmosphere protects living things on Earth from the sun's harmful ultraviolet radiation and scorching heat of the sun.

questions.question: It is a narrow zone of the earth where land, water, and air interact with each other to support life:
questions.explanation: Plant and animal kingdom together make the biosphere or the living world. It is a narrow zone of the earth where land, water, and air interact with each other to support life.

questions.question: The changes in the atmosphere produce changes in the:
questions.explanation: Weather and Climate depend upon nature. The changes in the atmosphere produce changes in the weather and climate.

questions.question: ________ is a system formed by the interaction of all living organisms with each other and with the physical and chemical factors of the environment in which they live, all linked by the transfer of energy and material.
questions.explanation: An ecosystem includes all of the living things (plants, animals, and organisms) in a given area, interacting with each other, and also with their non-living environments (weather, earth, sun, soil, climate, atmosphere).

questions.question: Which day is celebrated as the World Environment Day?
questions.explanation: On 5 June every year World Environment Day is celebrated.

questions.question: It is a trade in which goods are exchanged without the use of money.
questions.explanation: Humans learn new ways to use and change the environment. There was an emergence of the barter system. It is a trade in which goods are exchanged without the use of money.

questions.question: Identify what is shown in the figure
questions.explanation: The given figure is of Pond eco system where Organisms live together in a pond

questions.question:
Which of the following statement is false?

  1. All plants, land and human beings do not depend on their immediate surroundings
  2. All plants, animals, and human are also interdependent on each other
  3. This relation between the living organisms, as well as the relation between the organisms and their surroundings, forms an ecosystem
  4. There could be an ecosystem of the large rain forest, grassland, desert, mountains, lake, river, ocean, and even a small pond
questions.explanation: All plants, animals, and human beings depend on their immediate surroundings.

questions.question: Identify what is shown in the figure given below

options.html: Exchange of goods in place of goods
Exchange of goods in place of money

questions.explanation: The given figure is of Barter System where goods are exchanged in place of goods.

questions.question: ________ provides the air we breathe, the water we drink, the food we eat, and the land where we live.
questions.explanation: The environment is our basic life support system. It provides the air we breathe, the water we drink, the food we eat, and the land where we live.

questions.question: Which of the following statements false regarding early human?

options.html: Humans learn new ways to use and change environment
They adapted themselves to the natural surroundings
With time needs reduced and became more varied

questions.explanation: With time needs grew and became more varied.

questions.question: Industrial revolution enabled:
questions.explanation: The emergence of the Industrial revolution enabled large scale production.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Domains of the Earth Meaning
(a) Atmosphere (i) a blanket of air which surrounds the earth
(b) Hydrosphere (ii) the solid crust or the hard top layer of the earth
(c) Lithosphere (iii) comprises various sources of water and different types of water bodies
questions.explanation:
1. The atmosphere is the thin layer of air that surrounds the earth.
  2. Hydrosphere comprises various sources of water and different types of water bodies like rivers, lakes, seas, oceans, etc.
  3. The lithosphere is the solid crust or the hard top layer of the earth. It is made up of rocks and minerals and covered by a thin layer of soil.

questions.question: Following activities threat to the environment except:
questions.explanation: Reforestation is a remedy to protect the environment.

questions.question: ________ beings modify the natural environment.
questions.explanation: The car fumes pollute the air, water is collected in a pot, food is served in vessels, and the land is used to build factories. Human beings make cars, mills, factories, and manufacture containers. This is how human beings modify the natural environment.

questions.question: Following points show the importance of biosphere except:

options.html: It provides natural resources like fuel, energy
It provides air, food, water, and land
It provides us the raw material for industries

questions.explanation: Roads and bridges are human-made components.

questions.question: Landforms are found over:
questions.explanation: Landforms such as mountains, plateaus, plains, valleys, etc. are found over the continents and also on the ocean floors.

questions.question: NCC stands for

questions.question: Identify the shaded portion in the picture, what does it show
questions.explanation: Land, water and air comprise of biosphere.

questions.question:
Arrange the following in the order they emerged:

  1. The wheel was invented
  2. Barter system emerged, trade started and commerce developed
  3. The information revolution made communication speedy across the world
  4. Transportation became faster
  5. Industrial revolution enabled large scale production
questions.explanation: With time needs grew and became more varied. Humans learn new ways to use and change the environment. They learn to grow crops, domesticate animals, and lead a settled life. The wheel was invented, surplus food was produced, the barter system emerged, trade started and commerce developed. The industrial revolution enabled large scale production. Transportation became faster. The information revolution made communication easier and speedy across the world.

questions.question: An ecosystem is a system formed by the interaction of all living organisms with each other and with the ________ and ________ factors of the environment in which they live, all linked by transfer of energy and material.

  2. The gravitational force of the earth holds the atmosphere around it. It protects us from the harmful rays and scorching heat of the sun
  3. It consists of a number of gases, dust and water vapour
  4. The changes in the atmosphere produce changes in the weather and climate

questions.question:
Match the following:

Terms Meaning
(a) Environment (i) The place, people, things and nature that surround any living organism
(b) Ecosystem (ii) It is a system formed by the interaction of all living organisms with each other and with the physical and chemical factors
(c) Biosphere (iii) A narrow zone of the earth where land, water, and air interact with each other to support life
questions.explanation:

  3. Plant and animal kingdom together make the biosphere or the living world. It is a narrow zone of the earth where land, water, and air interact with each other to support life.

questions.question: An ecosystem is a system formed by the interaction of all living organisms with each other and with the ________ factors of the environment in which they live, all linked by transfer of energy and material.

questions.question: What do you mean by Crust?

options.html: The lower layer over the earth's surface
The uppermost layer over the earth's surface
The middle layer over the earth's surface
It is a part of Mantle which is the layer over the earth's surface

questions.explanation: Crust is the uppermost layer of the earth's surface.

questions.question: The main mineral constituents of the continental mass are

questions.question: The innermost layer of the earth is
questions.explanation: The innermost layer of the Earth is Core.

questions.question: Sandstone is made from
questions.explanation: Sandstone is a sedimentary rock. It is a rock made of compacted sand. Sand is small grains of rocks and minerals (such as quartz and feldspar). Sandstone is made from grains of sand.

questions.question: The oceanic crust is mainly called ________.
questions.explanation: The oceanic crust is mainly called sima.

questions.question: Which layer is having a radius of about 3500 km?
questions.explanation: The core is having a radius of about 3500 km.

questions.question: Nife stands for:
questions.explanation: The innermost layer is mainly made up of nickel and iron and is called nife (ni – nickel and fe – ferrous i.e. iron).

questions.question: Ferrous is ________.
questions.explanation: Ferrous is the Latin name of iron.

questions.question: There are three major types of rocks except:
questions.explanation: There are 3 main types of rocks-Igneous, Sedimentary, and Metamorphic. But Mantle is the name of the layer of the Earth.

questions.question: The molten magma cools down deep inside the earth's crust. Solid rocks so formed are called ________.
questions.explanation: The molten magma cools down deep inside the earth's crust. Solid rocks so formed are called intrusive igneous rock.

questions.question: The remains of the dead plants and animals trapped in the layers of rocks are called ________.
questions.explanation: The remains of the dead plants and animals trapped in the layers of rocks are called fossils.

questions.question: Red fort is made up of
questions.explanation: Red Fort is made up of red sandstone.

questions.question: Rocks roll down, crack, and hit each other and are broken down into small fragments. These smaller particles are called ________.
questions.explanation: Sediment is a naturally occurring material that is broken down by processes of weathering and erosion, and is subsequently transported by the action of wind, water, or ice, and/or by the force of gravity acting on the particles.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Stone Name of the game
(a) Five stone (i) Gitti
(b) Seven Stone (ii) Pitthoo
(c) Hop Scotch (iii) Stapu
questions.explanation:
1. Gitti is five stone game.
  2. Pitthoo is played with seven stones.
  3. Stapu is the other name of hopscotch.

questions.question: The Taj Mahal is made of ________.
questions.explanation: Taj Mahal is made up of white stones i.e white marble.

questions.question: What is shown in the given figure
questions.explanation: The picture shows an erupting volcano with magma coming out.

questions.question: Identify the below mention figure
questions.explanation: The picture shown is of Red Fort.

questions.question: ________ are naturally occurring substances which have certain physical properties and definite chemical composition.
questions.explanation: Minerals are naturally occurring substances which have certain physical properties and definite chemical composition.

questions.question: What is Lithosphere?
questions.explanation: The lithosphere is the topmost layer of the Earth and is also called the crust.

questions.question: The rock which is made up of molten magma is
questions.explanation: Molten magma is converted into igneous rock.

questions.question: Following are the example of minerals except:
questions.explanation: Basalt is a common extrusive igneous rock formed from the rapid cooling of basaltic lava. Coal, gold, and petroleum are minerals.

questions.question: When magma from deep below forces its way up to earth's surface, it is called ________.
questions.explanation: When magma from deep below forces its way up to earth's surface, it is called a volcano.

questions.question: Limestone is changed into
questions.explanation: Marble is a metamorphic rock that forms when limestone is subjected to the heat and pressure of metamorphism.

questions.question: Silica and magnesium are the constituents of ________.
questions.explanation: Silica and magnesium are the constituents of crust.

questions.question: What happens when igneous and sedimentary rocks go under heat and pressure?
questions.explanation: When igneous and sedimentary rocks go under heat and pressure, it changes into metamorphic rocks.

questions.question: Which type of rocks is called primary rocks?
questions.explanation: Igneous rock is called primary rock.

questions.question:
Identify which of the followings is an example of sedimentary rocks:

  1. Coal
  2. Marble
  3. Limestone
  4. Graphite
  5. Slate
questions.explanation: Only coal and limestone are sedimentary rocks.

questions.question: How are rock sediments transported and deposited?
questions.explanation: Rock sediments are transported and deposited by water.

questions.question: ________ rocks are hard whereas ________ rocks are soft.
questions.explanation: Igneous rock, formed by the cooling of magma(molten rock) inside the Earth or on the surface. Sedimentary rocks, formed from the products of weathering by cementation or precipitation on the Earth's surface. So, igneous rocks are hard whereas sedimentary rocks are soft.

tests.instructionHtml:
This is Social Science class 07 Social Science Inside our Earth CBSE Online Test 03 . Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: It lies below the mantle and around the centre of the earth. What is it?
questions.explanation: The Earth's inner core is the Earth's innermost part. It is composed of an iron-nickel alloy and some other elements. Layers of earth are core, mantle and crust. So, core lies below mantle and is around the centre of the earth.

questions.question: Which of the following is false regarding Mantle?

options.html: It is made up of dense and heavy material
It extends up to a depth of 2900 km
The uppermost layer over the earth's surface
It lies between crust and core

questions.explanation: All other statements are correct. The mantle is the second layer, not the topmost layer.

questions.question: ________ rocks have a very fine-grained structure.
questions.explanation: Extrusive igneous rocks have a very fine-grained structure.

questions.question: The deepest mine in the world is in ________.
questions.explanation: Anglo Gold Ashanti's Mponeng gold mine, located south-west of Johannesburg in South Africa, is currently the deepest mine in the world. So, the deepest mine in the world is in South Africa.

questions.question: Why igneous rocks are called basic rocks?

options.html: These are found on the base of the earth
These are first to be formed in the rock cycle

questions.explanation: As igneous rocks are formed from magma and begin the rock cycle, they are called primary rocks of basic rocks. All other rocks are derived from them, which is why you will also find them referred to as parent rocks on occasion. So, igneous rocks are called basic rocks.

questions.question: Which of the following is true regarding the earth?

options.html: It is dynamic keeps on changing inside only
It is dynamic keeps on changing outside only
It is dynamic keeps on changing from inside and outside
It is dynamic do not change from inside

questions.explanation: Earth is dynamic and keeps changing from both outside and inside.

questions.question: The earth's crust is about ________ km on the continental masses and only ________ km on the ocean floors.
questions.explanation: The uppermost layer over the earth's surface is called the crust which is thinnest of all the layers. It is about 35 km on the continental masses and only 5 km on the ocean floors.

questions.question: The main mineral constituents of the continental mass called ________.
questions.explanation: The main mineral constituents of the continental mass are silica and alumina. It is thus called sial (si-silica and al-alumina).

questions.question: Sima stands for
questions.explanation: The material of the lower part of the earth's crust, underlying both the oceans and the continents, characterized as relatively heavy and rich in silica, and magnesia is called sima. Thus, sima has silica and magnesium.

questions.question: Just beneath the crust is the mantle which extends up to a depth of ________.
questions.explanation: Just beneath the crust is the mantle which extends up to a depth of 2900 km.

questions.question: The ____ forms only 0.5 percent of the volume of the earth, 16 percent consists of the ____ and 83 percent makes the ____.
questions.explanation: The crust forms only 0.5 per cent of the volume of the earth, 16 per cent consists of the Core and 83 per cent makes the Mantle.

questions.question: The Innermost layer of the earth is made up of ________.
questions.explanation: The innermost layer is the core with a radius of about 3500 km. It is mainly made up of nickel and iron and is called nife (ni - nickel and - fe - ferrous i.e. iron). The central core has a very high temperature and pressure. Thus, the innermost layer of the earth is made up of nife.

questions.question: Any natural mass of mineral matter that makes up the earth's crust is called ________.
questions.explanation: Any natural mass of mineral matter that makes up the earth's crust is called rock.

questions.question: Igneous word is taken from ________.
questions.explanation: Igneous rock derived from the Latin word "ignis" meaning fire.

questions.question: Which of the following is not the character of igneous rocks?

options.html: They have remains of animals and plants
These do not occur in strata
They do not allow water to percolate

questions.explanation:
Features of igneous rocks are:-

  1. These are hard and compact.
  2. They do not allow water to percolate.
  3. They do not occur in strata.
  4. They are formed of molten lava.

But they do not have remains of animals and plants.

questions.question: Greek word metamorphose means:
questions.explanation: Greek word metamorphose means change of form.

questions.question: ________ is actually fiery red molten ________ coming out from the interior of the earth on its surface.
questions.explanation: Lava is actually fiery red molten magma coming out from the interior of the earth on its surface.

questions.question: When the molten lava comes on the earth's surface, it rapidly cools down and becomes solid. Rocks formed in such a way on the crust are called:
questions.explanation: When the molten lava comes on the earth's surface, it rapidly cools down and becomes hard. Rocks formed in such a way on the crust are called extrusive igneous rocks.

questions.question: Deccan plateau is made up of ________ rocks.
questions.explanation: Deccan plateau is made up of basalt rocks.

questions.question: Grinding stones used to prepare paste/powder of spices and grains are made of ________.
questions.explanation: Grinding stones used to prepare paste/powder of spices and grains are made of granite or marble.

questions.question: Which of the following is false regarding sedimentary rocks?

options.html: These rocks may also contain fossils of plants, animals and other microorganisms that once lived on them
Sediments are transported and deposited by wind, water, etc. These loose sediments are compressed and hardened to form layers of rocks
Rocks roll down, crack, and hit each other and are broken down into small fragments. These smaller particles are called sediments
Molten magma cools down deep inside the earth's crust.

questions.explanation: Molten magma cools down deep inside the earth's crust. This forms igneous rock, not the sedimentary rock.

questions.question: ________ rocks can change into ________ rocks under great heat and pressure.
questions.explanation: Igneous and sedimentary rocks can change into metamorphic rock under great heat and pressure.

questions.question: One type of rock changes to another type under certain conditions in a cyclic manner. This process of transformation of the rock from one to another is known as the_______
questions.explanation: One type of rock changes to another type under certain conditions in a cyclic manner. This process of transformation of the rock from one to another is known as the rock cycle.

questions.question: Identify the type of rock on the following figure
questions.explanation:
The given figure shows sedimentary rocks.
Sedimentary rocks are deposited in layers as strata, forming a structure called bedding.

questions.question: Rocks which contains fossils are
questions.explanation: Sedimentary rocks can contain fossils because, unlike most igneous and metamorphic rocks, they form at temperatures and pressures that do not destroy fossil remains.

questions.question: Why we cannot go to the centre of the earth?

options.html: It has no land only contain water
It has a very high temperature and pressure
It is made up of silicon and ferrous

questions.explanation: We can't go to the centre of the earth because it has a very high temperature and pressure.

questions.question: How limestone is changed into marble?

options.html: Under great heat and pressure the limestone changes into marble
By putting the limestone in the fire it changes into marble
Underwater the limestone changes into marble
When limestone is broken by a hammer it changes into marble

questions.explanation: Marble is a metamorphic rock that forms when limestone is subjected to the heat and pressure of metamorphism. It is composed primarily of the mineral calcite (CaCO3) and usually contains other minerals, such as clay minerals, micas, quartz, pyrite, iron oxides, and graphite.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Terms Meaning
(a) Ignis (i) Change of form
(b) Sediments (ii) Fire
(c) Metamorphose (iii) Settle down
questions.explanation:
1. Ignis - fire
  2. Sediments - settle down
  3. Metamorphose - change of form

questions.question: Which of the following is true regarding Rocks?

options.html: It is an aggregate of minerals
It is innermost layer of the earth

questions.explanation: A rock is a solid, inorganic, naturally-formed substance without a particular atomic structure or chemical composition. It's probably easier to just remember that rocks are made up of two or more minerals.

questions.question: Igneous rocks have:
questions.explanation: Igneous rocks have pure minerals.

questions.question: Rocks used for building materials are
questions.explanation: Rocks used for building materials are granite, basalt, sandstone, slate, limestone.

questions.question: The Detailed study of the interior of the earth is called ________.
questions.explanation: The detailed study of the interior of the earth is called geology. Geology is an earth science concerned with the solid Earth, the rocks of which it is composed, and the processes by which they change over time.

questions.question: Metamorphic is a ________.
questions.explanation: Metamorphic comes from Greek words meaning "change" and "form".

questions.question:
Match the following:

Type of rocks Example
(a) Igneous (1) Slate
(b) Sedimentary (2) Sandstone
(c) Metamorphic (3) Basalt
questions.explanation:
1. Igneous - Basalt
  2. Sedimentary - Sandstone
  3. Metamorphic - Slate

questions.question: Identify what is shown in the given figure
questions.explanation:
The rock cycle is a model that describes the formation, breakdown, and reformation of a rock as a result of sedimentary, igneous, and metamorphic processes. All rocks are made up of minerals.
Thus, the picture shown here is of rock cycle.

questions.question: To reach to the centre of the earth you have to dig a hole ________ deep approximately.
questions.explanation: Earth radius is the approximate distance from Earth's center to its surface, about 6,371 km. So, to reach the earth centre we need to dig 6000 km approx.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Terms Meanings
(a) Minerals (i) Are naturally occurring substances which have certain physical properties and definite chemical composition
(b) Rocks (ii) The remains of the dead plants and animals trapped in the layers of rocks
(c) Fossils (iii) Any natural mass of mineral matter that makes up the earth's crust
questions.explanation:
1. Minerals - Are naturally occurring substances which have certain physical properties and definite chemical composition.
  2. Rocks - Any natural mass of mineral matter that makes up the earth's crust.
  3. Fossils - The remains of the dead plants and animals trapped in the layers of rocks.

questions.question: Identify what is shown in the following figure
questions.explanation: The figure shows layers of the earth.ie interior of earth.

questions.question: In the above figure Orange layer shows the
questions.explanation: The yellow layer shows Mantle.

questions.question: In the above figure which layer is thinnest and what it is called
questions.explanation: In the given picture the thinnest layer is Crust, which is shown in orange colour here.

questions.question:
Arrange the following statement in the formation of the rock cycle in order:

  1. Molten magma cools
  2. These igneous rocks are broken down into small particles
  3. It solidifies to become igneous rock
  4. Particle is transported and deposited to form sedimentary rocks
questions.explanation:
1. Molten magma cools
  2. It solidifies to become igneous rock.
  3. These igneous rocks are broken down into small particles
  4. Particle is transported and deposited to form sedimentary rocks

questions.question: Identify
questions.explanation: The given picture is of metamorphic rock.

questions.question: The radius of the earth is ________.
questions.explanation: Earth radius is the approximate distance from Earth's center to its surface, about 6,371 km.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Layer of the earth Composition
(a) Continental Crust (i) Nife
(b) Oceanic Crust (ii) Sima
(c) Core (iii) Sial
questions.explanation:
1. Continental Crust - Sial the main mineral constituents of the continental mass are silica and alumina. It is thus called sial 
  2. Oceanic Crust -Sima the oceanic crust mainly consists of silica and magnesium; it is therefore called sima (si-silica and ma-magnesium)
  3. Core - Nife the innermost layer is the core with a radius of about 3500 km. It is mainly made up of nickel and iron and is called nife (ni - nickel and - fe - ferrous i.e. iron).

questions.question: The deepest mine in the world, is in South Africa. It is about ________ km. deep. In a search for oil, engineers have dug a hole about ________ km deep.
questions.explanation: The deepest mine in the world is in South Africa. It is about 4 km deep. In the search for oil, engineers have dug a hole about 6 km deep.

questions.question: The lithosphere is broken into a number of plates known as the _______.
questions.explanation: Lithospheric plates are regions of Earth's crust and upper mantle that are fractured into plates that move across a deeper plasticine mantle. Earth's crust is fractured into 13 major and approximately 20 total lithospheric plates. These plates move around very slowly - just a few millimetres each year. This is because of the movement of the molten magma inside the earth. The molten magma inside the earth moves in a circular manner.

questions.question: The molten magma inside the earth moves in a ________ manner.
questions.explanation: These plates move around very slowly - just a few millimetres each year. This is because of the movement of the molten magma inside the earth. The molten magma inside the earth moves in a circular manner.

questions.question: The earth movements are divided on the basis of the ________ which causes them to move.
questions.explanation: The earth movements are divided on the basis of the forces which cause them. The forces which act in the interior of the earth are called as Endogenic forces and the forces that work on the surface of the earth are called as Exogenic forces.

questions.question: Two types of forces which cause earth movement are-
questions.explanation: The earth movements are divided on the basis of the forces which cause them. The forces which act in the interior of the earth are called Endogenic forces and the forces that work on the surface of the earth are called Exogenic forces.

questions.question: The forces which act in the interior of the earth are called ________.
questions.explanation: The earth movements are divided on the basis of the forces which cause them. The forces which act in the interior of the earth are called endogenic forces and the forces that work on the surface of the earth are called exogenic forces.

questions.question: Which of the following is not exogenic force?
questions.explanation:
The forces that work on the surface of the earth are called Exogenic forces. Following are external forces

  1. River
  2. Wind
  3. Sea-waves
  4. Glacier
  5. Erosional and Depositional

Thus, the volcano is not an external or exogenic force.

questions.question: ________ movements like earthquakes and volcanoes cause mass destruction over the surface of the earth.
questions.explanation: Endogenic forces sometimes produce sudden movements and at the other times produce slow movements. Sudden movements like earthquakes and volcanoes cause mass destruction over the surface of the earth.

questions.question: It is a vent (opening) in the earth's crust through which molten material erupts suddenly.
questions.explanation:
A volcano is a vent (opening) in the earth’s crust through which molten material erupts suddenly.
​​​​​​

questions.question: When the Lithospheric plates move, the surface of the earth vibrates. The vibrations can travel all around the earth. These vibrations are called ________.
questions.explanation: An earthquake is the shaking of the surface of the Earth, resulting from the sudden release of energy in the Earth's lithosphere that creates seismic waves. When the Lithospheric plates move, the surface of the earth vibrates. The vibrations can travel all around the earth. These vibrations are called Earthquake.

questions.question: The place in the crust where the movement starts at the time of earthquake is called the ________.
questions.explanation: The point on the Earth's surface located directly above the focus of an earthquake. Focus - The location where the earthquake begins. Thus, the place in the crust where the movement starts at the time of earthquake is called the focus.

questions.question: The place on the surface above the focus is called the ________.
questions.explanation: The epicenter is the point on the earth's surface vertically above the hypocenter (or focus), point in the crust where a seismic rupture begins.

questions.question: An earthquake is measured with a machine called a ________.
questions.explanation: An earthquake is measured with a machine called a seismograph. The magnitude of the earthquake is measured on the Richter scale.

questions.question: Bhuj is in ________.
questions.explanation: The 2001 Gujarat earthquake, also known as the Bhuj earthquake, occurred on 26 January, India's 51st Republic Day, at 08:46 AM IST, and lasted for over 2 minutes. The intraplate earthquake reached 6.9 on the Richter scale. Thus, Bhuj is in Gujarat.

questions.question: A massive earthquake hit Bhuj Town on:
questions.explanation: The 2001 Gujarat earthquake, also known as the Bhuj earthquake, occurred on 26 January, India's 51st Republic Day, at 08:46 AM IST, and lasted for over 2 minutes.

questions.question: Following points to be considered for the earthquake preparedness except:

options.html: Stay away from fireplaces, areas around chimneys
Stay near windows that shatter including mirrors and picture frames
Take shelter under a kitchen counter, table or desk, against an inside corner or wall
Spread awareness amongst your friends and family members and face any disaster confidently

questions.explanation:
Earthquake Preparedness

  1. Where to take shelter during an earthquake - Safe Spot – Under a kitchen counter, table, or desk, against an inside corner or wall.
  2. Stay Away from - Fireplaces, areas around chimneys, windows that shatter including mirrors and picture frames.
  3. Be Prepared - Spread awareness amongst your friends and family members and face any disaster confidently.

So, all other statements are true except this statement.

questions.question: Which of the following is not associated with earthquake?
questions.explanation: The vent is associated with the volcano. Focus, epicenter, and wave are associated with an earthquake.

questions.question: ________ is classified as a major earthquake.
questions.explanation: An earthquake of 2.0 or less can be felt only a little. An earthquake over 5.0 can cause damage from things falling. A 6.0 or higher magnitude is considered very strong and 7.0 is classified as a major earthquake.

questions.question: Where are Niagara falls located?
questions.explanation: Niagara falls located on the border between Canada and the USA in North America.

questions.question: The highest waterfall in the world is ________.
questions.explanation: The highest waterfall is Angel Falls of Venezuela in South America.

questions.question: It is a circular opening through which hot molten material erupt suddenly. This opening is called ________.
questions.explanation: A volcano is a vent (opening) in the earth’s crust through which molten material erupts suddenly.

questions.question: When the river tumbles at a steep angle over very hard rocks or down a steep valley side it forms a ________.
questions.explanation: The running water in the river erodes the landscape. When the river tumbles at a steep angle over very hard rocks or down a steep valley side it forms a waterfall.

questions.question: As the river enters the plain it twists and turns forming large bends known as:
questions.explanation: As the river enters the plain it twists and turns to form large bends known as meanders. Due to continuous erosion and deposition along the sides of the meander, the ends of the meander loop come closer and closer.

questions.question: The steep rocky coast rising almost vertically above seawater is called ________.
questions.explanation: The steep rocky coast rising almost vertically above seawater is called sea cliff. The sea waves deposit sediments along the shores forming beaches.

questions.question: An active agent of erosion and deposition in the deserts is ________.
questions.explanation: An active agent of erosion and deposition in the deserts is wind. In deserts, you can see rocks in the shape of a mushroom, commonly called mushroom rocks.

questions.question: The natural cavity of weak rocks formed by the action of waves ________.
questions.explanation: Seawaves continuously strike at the rocks. Cracks develop. Over time they become larger and wider. Thus, hollow like caves are formed on the rocks. They are called sea caves.

questions.question: In deserts you can see rocks in the shape of a mushroom, commonly called ________.
questions.explanation: An active agent of erosion and deposition in the deserts is wind. In deserts, you can see rocks in the shape of a mushroom, commonly called mushroom rocks. pe of a mushroom, commonly called mushroom rocks. Winds erode the lower section of the rock more than the upper part. Therefore, such rocks have a narrower base and a wider top.

questions.question: When the wind blows, it lifts and transports sand from one place to another. When it stops blowing the sand falls and gets deposited in low hill like structures. These are called ________.
questions.explanation: When the wind blows, it lifts and transports sand from one place to another. When it stops blowing the sand falls and gets deposited in low hill - like structures. These are called sand dunes. When the grains of sand is very fine and light, the wind can carry it over very long distances.

questions.question: Large deposits of loess is found in
questions.explanation: When the grains of sand are very fine and light, the wind can carry it over very long distances. When such sand is deposited in large areas, it is called loess. Large deposits of loess is found in China. Can be understood with the picture given below:

questions.question: Which of the following is not the coastal landforms?
questions.explanation: When the grains of sand is very fine and light, the wind can carry it over very long distances. When such sand is deposited in large areas, it is called loess. Thus, loess is a desert landform and all others are coastal landforms.

questions.question: Which of the following river form delta?
questions.explanation: All the given rivers, Amazon, Nile, and Zaire form delta. Delta is an area of low, flat land, sometimes shaped like a triangle, where a river divides into several smaller rivers before flowing into the sea.

questions.question: Where are the Victoria Falls located?
questions.explanation: Victoria's waterfalls are located on the borders of Zambia and Zimbabwe in Africa.

questions.question: Lithospheric plates move around very slowly, just a few ________ each year
questions.explanation: Lithospheric plates move around very slowly, just a few millimeters each year.

questions.question: Which of the following is false regarding Lithospheric plates?

options.html: The Hydrosphere is broken into a number of plates known as the Lithospheric plates.
The molten magma inside the earth moves in a circular manner.
Lithospheric plates move because of the movement of the molten magma inside the earth.
These plates move around very slowly, just a few millimetres each year.

questions.explanation: The Lithosphere is broken into a number of plates known as the Lithospheric plates, not hydrosphere.

questions.question: What are the Exogenic forces?

options.html: The forces that work on the interior of the earth only
The forces that work on the atmosphere of the earth only
The forces that work on the surface of the earth
The forces that work inside the earth

questions.explanation: The movement of lithospheric plates causes changes on the surface of the earth. The earth movements are divided on the basis of the forces which cause them. The forces which act in the interior of the earth are called Endogenic forces and the forces that work on the surface of the earth are called Exogenic forces.

questions.question: Building mountain is a ________ force.
questions.explanation: Building a mountain is diastrophic force that comes under endogenic forces.

questions.question: The earth's crust consists of several large and some small, rigid, irregularly shaped plates (slabs) which carry continents and the ocean floor. What are these plates called?
questions.explanation: The earth's crust consists of several large and some small, rigid, irregularly shaped plates (slabs) which carry continents and the ocean floor. These plates are called Lithospheric plates. Each lithospheric plate is composed of a layer of the oceanic crust or continental crust superficial to an outer layer of the mantle.

questions.question: There are three types of earthquake waves:

options.html: P wave, S wave, L wave
P wave, S wave, T wave
P wave, Q wave, R wave
P wave, M wave, L wave

questions.explanation:
There are three types of earthquake waves:

  1. P waves or longitudinal waves
  2. S waves or transverse waves
  3. L waves or surface waves

questions.question: Which forces sometimes produce sudden movements and at the other times produce slow movements?
questions.explanation: Endogenic forces sometimes produce sudden movements and at the other times produce slow movements. Sudden movements like earthquakes and volcanoes cause mass destruction over the surface of the earth.

questions.question: In the word Endogenic, "genic" means?
questions.explanation: The word origin can be explained as: Endo (inside) + genic (origin) = Endogenic

questions.question: Greatest damage during an earthquake is usually closest to the ________ and the strength of the earthquake decreases away from the centre.
questions.explanation: The greatest damage during an earthquake is usually closest to the epicenter and the strength of the earthquake decreases away from the centre.

questions.question: A massive earthquake measuring ________ on the Richter scale hit Bhuj Town.
questions.explanation: A massive earthquake measuring 6.9 on the Richter scale hit Bhuj Town on 26th January 2001. The President declared a state of emergency in the quake zone.

questions.question: The magnitude of the earthquake is measured on the ________.
questions.explanation: The magnitude of the earthquake is measured on the Richter scale. An earthquake of 2.0 or less can be felt only a little. An earthquake over 5.0 can cause damage from things falling. A 6.0 or higher magnitude is considered very strong and 7.0 is classified as a major earthquake.

questions.question: An earthquake of ________ can be felt only a little. An earthquake over 5.0 can cause damage from things falling. A ________ magnitude is considered very strong.
questions.explanation: An earthquake of 2.0 or less can be felt only a little. An earthquake over 5.0 can cause damage from things falling. A 6.0 or higher magnitude is considered very strong and 7.0 is classified as a major earthquake.

questions.question: What is Weathering?

options.html: It is the wearing away of the landscape by wind
It is the wearing away of the landscape water
It is the breaking up of the rocks on the earth's surface
It is the wearing away of the landscape by ice

questions.explanation: The landscape is being continuously worn away by two processes - weathering and erosion. Weathering is the breaking up of the rocks on the earth’s surface.

questions.question: The process of erosion and deposition create-

options.html: Different landforms interior of the earth
Same landforms interior of the earth
Different landforms on the surface of the earth
Same landforms on the surface of the earth

questions.explanation: The landscape is being continuously worn away by two processes - weathering and erosion. Weathering is the breaking up of the rocks on the earth’s surface. Erosion is the wearing away of the landscape by different agents like water, wind and ice. The eroded material is carried away or transported by water, wind, etc. and eventually deposited. This process of erosion and deposition create different landforms on the surface of the earth.

questions.question: The highest waterfall is ________.
questions.explanation: The highest waterfall is Angel Falls of Venezuela in South America.

questions.question: In due course of time the meander loop cuts off from the river and forms a cut-off lake, also called ________.
questions.explanation: Due to continuous erosion and deposition along the sides of the meander, the ends of the meander loop come closer and closer. In due course of time the meander loop cuts off from the river and forms a cut-off lake, also called an ox-bow lake.

questions.question: As the river approaches the sea, the speed of the flowing water decreases and the river begins to break up into a number of streams called ________.
questions.explanation: As the river approaches the sea, the speed of the flowing water decreases and the river begins to break up into a number of streams called distributaries. A distributary is a stream that branches off and flows away from a mainstream channel. They are a common feature of river deltas. The phenomenon is known as river bifurcation. The opposite of a distributary is a tributary.

questions.question: The erosion and deposition of the sea waves give rise to:
questions.explanation: The erosion and deposition of the sea waves gives rise to coastal landforms. Seawaves continuously strike at the rocks. Cracks develop. Over time they become larger and wider. Thus, hollow like caves are formed on the rocks.

questions.question: Glaciers are "rivers" of ________.
questions.explanation: Glaciers are “rivers of ice” which to erode the landscape by bulldozing soil and stones to expose the solid rock below. A glacier is a persistent body of dense ice that is constantly moving under its own weight; it forms where the accumulation of snow exceeds its ablation (melting and sublimation) over many years, often centuries.

questions.question: The material carried by the glacier such as rocks big and small, sand and silt gets deposited. These deposits form ________.
questions.explanation: Glaciers carve out deep hollows there. As the ice melts they get filled up with water and become beautiful lakes in the mountains. The material carried by the glacier such as rocks big and small, sand and silt gets deposited. These deposits form glacial moraines.

questions.question: Volcano derived its name from ________.
questions.explanation: The word volcano is derived from the name of Vulcano, a volcanic island in the Aeolian Islands of Italy whose name, in turn, comes from Vulcan, the god of fire in Roman mythology.

questions.question: Which of the following is the highest waterfall in India?
questions.explanation: The Jog waterfalls created by the Sharavathi river in Sharavathi valley of Shimoga district in Karnataka. Its breathtaking spectacle when Sharavathi river falling from a height of 829 ft.

questions.question: In deserts, you can see rocks in the shape of a mushroom. Explain why?

options.html: Because winds erode the lower section of the rock more than the upper part
Because winds erode the lower section of the rock equally with the upper part
Because winds erode the lower section of the rock less than the upper part
Because water erode the lower section of the rock more than the upper part

questions.explanation: An active agent of erosion and deposition in the deserts is wind. In deserts, you can see rocks in the shape of a mushroom, commonly called mushroom rocks. Winds erode the lower section of the rock more than the upper part. Therefore, such rocks have a narrower base and wider top which look like mushrooms.

questions.question: When the grains of sand is very fine and light, the wind can carry it over very long distances. When such sand is deposited in large areas, it is called ________.
questions.explanation: Loess is a geologically recent deposit of silt or material which is usually yellowish or brown in color and consisting of tiny mineral particles brought by wind to the places where they now lie. When the grains of sand is very fine and light, the wind can carry it over very long distances. When such sand is deposited in large areas, it is called Loess.

questions.question: Meanders found in ________.
questions.explanation: As the river enters the plain it twists and turns forming large bends known as meanders. Due to continuous erosion and deposition along the sides of the meander, the ends of the meander loop come closer and closer.

questions.question: Which waterfall of the following is situated in North America?
questions.explanation: The highest waterfall is Angel Falls of Venezuela in South America. The other waterfalls are Niagara falls located on the border between Canada and the USA in North America.

questions.question: Lithospheric plates move because

options.html: movement of the solid magma outside the earth
movement of the molten magma outside the earth
movement of the molten magma inside the earth
movement of the solid magma inside the earth

questions.explanation: These plates move around very slowly - just a few millimetres each year. This is because of the movement of the molten magma inside the earth. The molten magma inside the earth moves in a circular manner.

questions.question:
Match the following:

(a) Glaciers (i) A Crescent-shaped lake formed by the river meanders
(b) Sand dune (ii) Moving mass of ice
(c) Oxbow lake (iii) Small hills of sand
questions.explanation:
1. Glaciers - Moving mass of ice
  2. Sand dune - Small hills of sand
  3. Oxbow lake - Crescent-shaped lake formed by the river meanders

questions.explanation: The given figure shows a volcano with magma chamber at the bottom.

questions.question:

Type of Forces Example

1. Earthquake - Endogenic forces sometimes produce sudden movements and at the other times produce slow movements.like earthquake and volcano

(b) Beaches (ii) Rise and fall of water caused by friction of the wind on water surfaces

  2. Beaches - Landform alongside a body of water

questions.question: Our earth is surrounded by a huge blanket of ________.
questions.explanation: Our earth is surrounded by a huge blanket of air called atmosphere. All living beings on this earth depend on the atmosphere for their survival. It provides us the air we breathe and protects us from the harmful effects of the sun’s rays. Without this blanket of protection, we would be baked alive by the heat of the sun during the day and get frozen during the night. So it is this mass of air that has made the temperature on the earth liveable.

questions.question: Which two gases make up the bulk of the atmosphere?
questions.explanation: Our atmosphere is made up of around 78% of nitrogen and around 21% of oxygen.

questions.question: Cold air is ________ than hot air.
questions.explanation: When air is heated, it expands, becomes lighter, and goes up. Cold air is denser and heavy. That is why it tends to sink down.

questions.question: Which is the second most plentiful gas in the air?
questions.explanation: Around 21% of the atmosphere is made up of oxygen. Green plants produce oxygen during photosynthesis and humans and animals take oxygen from the air as they breathe.

questions.question: Green plants use ________ to make their food and release ________.
questions.explanation: Green plants use carbon dioxide from the atmosphere to make their food and they release oxygen during photosynthesis.

questions.question: Which of the following is not the layer of the atmosphere?
questions.explanation: Hydrosphere refers to all the waters on the earth's surface, such as lakes and seas, and sometimes including water near the earth's surface, such as clouds. It is not a layer of the atmosphere.

questions.question: All the weather phenomena like rainfall, fog and hailstorm occur in which layer?
questions.explanation: The troposphere is the nearest layer to the earth's surface. Its height is 13 km. Hence, all weather phenomena like fog, hailstorm, and rainfall occur in this layer.

questions.question: Which layer is almost free from clouds?
questions.explanation: Above the troposphere, lies the stratosphere. Its height is 50 km. This layer is free from clouds and weather phenomena. It is ideal for flying aeroplanes.

questions.question: The air we breathe exists in ________.
questions.explanation: As Troposphere is nearest to the earth's surface and its height is 13 km, it contains the air we breathe.

questions.question: Jet planes flying in the sky leave a ________ trail behind them.
questions.explanation: Jet planes flying in the sky leave a white trail behind them. The moisture from their engines condenses. We see trails of this condensed moisture for some time when there is no air movement, hence their trails appear to be white.

questions.question: Meteorites burn up in this layer on entering from the space-
questions.explanation: This is caused by the meteorite rubbing against molecules in the mesosphere. When the friction is great enough, the meteorite burns up.

questions.question: ________ is this hour-to-hour, day to day condition of the atmosphere.
questions.explanation: The state of the atmosphere at a particular time and place is the weather. Weather is described in terms of variable conditions such as temperature, humidity, wind velocity, precipitation, and barometric pressure.

questions.question: Which of the following instruments is used to measure rainfall?
questions.explanation: A rain gauge (also known as an udometer, pluviometer, or an ombrometer) is an instrument used by meteorologists and hydrologists to gather and measure the amount of liquid precipitation over a set period of time. Most standard rain gauges have a wide funnel leading into the cylinder and are calibrated so that one-tenth of an inch of rain measures one inch when it collects inside. The funnel is 10 times the cross-sectional area of the tube. Rainfall as low as .01 inches can be measured with this instrument.

questions.question: The degree of hotness and coldness of the air is known as ________.
questions.explanation: Air temperature is a measure of how hot or cold the air is. It is the most commonly measured weather parameter. More specifically, temperature describes the kinetic energy, or energy of motion, of the gases that make up air. Air temperature is usually measured in Celsius (C) or Fahrenheit (F).

questions.question: Composition of air:
questions.explanation: Nitrogen accounts for 78% of the atmosphere, oxygen 21%, and argon 0.9%. Gases like carbon dioxide, nitrous oxides, methane, and ozone are trace gases that account for about a tenth of one percent of the atmosphere.
