questions.question:
Who said these lines
"Man is mortal. Everyone has to die some day or the other. But one must resolve to lay down one's life in enriching the noble ideals of self-respect and in bettering one's human life... Nothing is more disgraceful for a brave man than to live life devoid of self-respect."?
questions.explanation: B.R. Ambedkar said that “It is disgraceful to live at the cost of one's self-respect. Self-respect is the most vital factor in life. Without it, man is a cipher. To live worthily with self-respect, one has to overcome difficulties. It is out of hard and ceaseless struggle alone that one derives strength, confidence and recognition. “Man is mortal. Everyone has to die some day or the other. But one must resolve to lay down one's life in enriching the noble ideals of self-respect and in bettering one's human life.Nothing is more disgraceful for a brave man than to live life devoid of self-respect.”

questions.question: What is shown in the below figure.
questions.explanation: The Sansad Bhawan (Parliament Building) is the house of the Parliament of India, located in New Delhi. Originally called the House of Parliament, it was designed by the British architect Edwin Lutyens and Herbert Baker in 1912-1913 and construction began in 1921 and ended in 1927. The opening ceremony of the Parliament House, then called the Central Legislative Assembly, was performed on 18 January 1927 by Lord Irwin, the Viceroy of India.

questions.question: Identify the person who played major role in framing constitution.
questions.explanation: Bhimrao Ramji Ambedkar (14 April 1891 – 6 December 1956), popularly known as Baba Saheb, was an Indian jurist, economist, politician and social reformer who inspired the Dalit Buddhist Movement and campaigned against social discrimination against Untouchables (Dalits), while also supporting the rights of women and labour. He was Independent India's first law minister, the principal architect of the Constitution of India and a founding father of the Republic of India

questions.question:
Match the following
Column I
(a) Dignity
(b) Equality
(c) Constitution
Column II
(1) Act of treating each person in same manner
(2) Thinking of one self and other person as worthy of respect
(3) Documents lay down basic rules and regulation for the government of the country
questions.explanation:
1. The dignity and self-respect of each person and their community can only be realised if they have adequate resources to support and nurture their families and if they are not discriminated against.
  2. The Indian Constitution recognises the equality of all persons. Movements and struggles for equality in India continuously refer to the Indian Constitution to make their point about equality and justice for all.
  3. The foundation of all movements for justice and the inspiration for all the poetry and songs on equality is the recognition that all people are equal. As you know, the Indian Constitution recognises the equality of all persons. Movements and struggles for equality in India continuously refer to the Indian Constitution to make their point about equality and justice for all

questions.question:
Match the following
Tawa Movement events
(a) Rights to private contractors
(b) Dam build
(c) Celebrated new year
Year of events
(1) 1994
(2) 1978
(3) 1997
questions.explanation:
1. In 1994, the government gave the rights for fishing in the Tawa reservoir to private contractors. These contractors drove the local people away and got cheap labour from outside.
  2. The Tawa dam began to be built in 1958 and was completed in 1978. It submerged large areas of forest and agricultural land.
  3. On January 2, 1997, people from 33 villages of Tawa started the new year with the first catch.

questions.question: Identify the following figure in Madhya Pradesh.
questions.explanation: Originating in the Mahadeo hills of Chindwara district, the Tawa flows through Betul, before joining the Narmada in Hoshangabad. The Tawa dam began to be built in 1958 and was completed in 1978. It submerged large areas of forest and agricultural land. The forest dwellers were left with nothing. Some of the displaced people settled around the reservoir and apart from their meagre farms found a livelihood in fishing.

questions.question: Identify the figure given below.
questions.explanation: The newly formed Tawa Matsya Sangh (TMS) organised rallies and a chakka jam (road blockade), demanding their right to continue fishing for their livelihood. In response to their protests, the government created a committee to assess the issue. The committee recommended that fishing rights be granted to the villagers for their livelihood. In 1996, the Madhya Pradesh government decided to give to the people displaced by the Tawa dam the fishing rights for the reservoir.

questions.question: Why the members of TMS were protesting?

options.html: Demand for right to continues fishing for their livelihood
Demand for money from the government
Demand for right to form new government
Demand for right to give contract to private contactors

questions.explanation: The newly formed Tawa Matsya Sangh (TMS) organised rallies and a chakka jam (road blockade), demanding their right to continue fishing for their livelihood. questions.explanation:
1. Originating in the Mahadeo hills of Chindwara district, the Tawa river flows through Betul, before joining the Narmada in Hoshangabad. The Tawa dam began to be built in 1958 and was completed in 1978.
  2. The Mahadeo Hillsare a range of hills in Madhya Pradesh state of central India. The hills are situated in the northern section of the Satpura Range.
  3. According to the 2001 Census data women form 48 per cent of the population, Muslims form 13 per cent of the population, Dalits form 16 per cent and Adivasis 8 per cent.

questions.question:
Match the following
People face discrimination
(a) Omprakash Valmiki
(b) African-Americans
(c) Ansari
Discriminated against
(1) Caste
(2) Religion
(3) Race, caste
questions.explanation:
1. Omprakash Valmiki is a famous Dalit writer. In his autobiography, Joothan, he writes, about the discrimination that he faced as being a dalit boy,(due to his caste) “I had to sit away from the others in the class, and that too on the floor. The mat ran out before reaching the spot I sat on. Sometimes I would have to sit way behind everybody, right near the door.
  2. In the United States of America, the African–Americans continue to describe their lives today as largely unequal. This, despite the fact that there was a movement in the late 1950s to push for equal rights for African– Americans. Prior to this, African–Americans were treated extremely unequally in the United States and denied equality through law.
  3. Ansari's faced a huge discrimination as they ate meat, Their dignity was also hurt when persons refused to lease their apartments to them. However, when the property dealer suggested that they change their name, it was their dignity or self-respect that made them refuse this suggestion.

questions.question: Which was a very important activity on the Samoan islands?
questions.explanation: The Samoan Islands are part of a large group of small islands in the southern part of the Pacific Ocean. In the 1920s, according to research reports on Samoan society, children did not go to school. They learnt many things, such as how to take care of children or do household work from older children and from adults. Fishing was a very important activity on the islands. Young people, therefore, learnt to undertake long fishing expeditions.

questions.question: What is the sense of self-awareness?
questions.explanation: Being a boy or a girl is an important part of one’s identity. The society we grow up in teaches us what kind of behaviour is acceptable for girls and boys, what boys and girls can or cannot do. Identity is a sense of self-awareness of who one is. Typically, a person can have several identities. For example, a person can be a girl, a sister and a musician.

questions.question: What is the term that is commonly used to describe the women's work and situation?
questions.explanation: Double-burden Literally means a double load. This term is commonly used to describe the women’s work situation. It has emerged from a recognition that women typically labour both inside the home (housework) and outside. In most societies, including our own, the roles men and women play or the work they do, are not valued equally. Men and women do not have the same status. If we add up the housework and the work, women do outside the home, we find that women spend much more time working than men and have much less time for leisure.

questions.question: Besides physical tasks, Women also involve a strong emotional aspect that is-
questions.explanation: Across the world, the main responsibility of a women is  housework and care-giving tasks, like looking after the family, especially children, the elderly and sick members. Yet, as we have seen, the work that women do within the home is not recognised as work. It is also assumed that this is something that comes naturally to women. It, therefore, does not have to be paid for. And society devalues this work.

questions.question: When someone is not given due recognition for a task or job they have done, they can feel _____
questions.explanation: Societies assign different values to the roles men and women play and the work they do, which becomes a basis for inequality and discrimination. Work Done primarily by women as housework is often not considered ‘work’ and, therefore they feel invisible and self devalued.

questions.question: Were Harmeet and Shonali correct in saying that their mother do not work?

options.html: No, as household work is devalued
No, Harmeet mothers is elder than him
Yes, as household work is revalued
Yes, Her mother not do any work

questions.explanation: Harmeet’s family did not think that the work Jaspreet did within the house was real work. This feeling is not unique to their families. Across the world, the main responsibility for housework and care-giving tasks, like looking after the family, especially children, the elderly and sick members, lies with women. Yet, as we have seen, the work that women do within the home is not recognised as work. It is also assumed that this is something that comes naturally to women. It, therefore, does not have to be paid for. And society devalues this work.

questions.question: It is a social construct and cannot be determined by biological differences-
questions.explanation: Gender is a term that you may often have heard. It is a term, however, that is not easily understood. It determines, for example, who we are and what we will become, where we can go and where not, the life choices available to us and those we eventually make. Our understanding of gender is often based on the family and society that we live in. This leads us to think that the roles we see men and women around us play are fixed and natural. In fact, these roles differ across communities around the world. By gender, then, we mean the many social values and stereotypes our cultures attach to the biological distinction ‘male’ and ‘female’. It is a term that helps us to understand many of the inequalities and power relations between men and women in society.

questions.question: At what age did the boys of Samoan join older boys for learning outdoor jobs?
questions.explanation: The Samoan Islands are part of a large group of small islands in the southern part of the Pacific Ocean. In the 1920s, according to research reports on Samoan society, children did not go to school. They learnt many things, such as how to take care of children or do household work from older children and from adults. Fishing was a very important activity on the islands. Both boys and girls looked after their younger siblings. But, by the time a boy was about 9 years old, he joined the older boys in learning outdoor jobs like fishing and planting coconuts.

questions.question: After the age of ____ or so, girls also went on fishing trips, worked in the plantations and learnt how to weave baskets.
questions.explanation: The Samoan Islands are part of a large group of small islands in the southern part of the Pacific Ocean. In the 1920s, according to research reports on Samoan society, children did not go to school. They learnt many things, such as how to take care of children or do household work from older children and from adults. Girls had to continue looking after small children or do errands for adults till they were teenagers. But, once they became teenagers they had much more freedom. After the age of 14 or so, girls also went on fishing trips, worked in the plantations, learnt how to weave baskets.

questions.question: From which class did the girls and boys go to separate school in a town in Madhya Pradesh.
questions.explanation: On an account of experiences of being in a small town in Madhya Pradesh in the 1960s, From Class VI onwards, boys and girls went to separate schools. The girls’ school was designed very differently from the boys’ school. They had a central courtyard where they played in total seclusion and safety from the outside world. The boys’ school had no such courtyard and our playground was just a big space attached to the school.

questions.question: Who was Melani?
questions.explanation: Melani was a domestic worker who narrate about her experience of working in Delhi as– A domestic worker’s day can begin as early as five in the morning and end as late as twelve at night.Despite the hard work they do, their employers often do not show them much respect. This is what “My first job was with a rich family that lived in a three-storeyed house. The memsahib was very strange as she would shout to get any work done. My work was in the kitchen. There were two other girls who did the cleaning. Our day would begin at 5 o’clock.

questions.question: The government has passed laws that make it mandatory for organisations that have more than ____ employees to provide creche facilities.
questions.explanation: The government has set up anganwadis or child-care centres in several villages in the country. The government has passed laws that make it mandatory for organisations that have more than 30 women employees to provide crèche facilities. The provision of crèches helps many women to take up employment outside the home. It also makes it possible for more girls to attend schools.

questions.question: In ____ areas women and girls carry heavy loads of firewood.
questions.explanation: Housework actually involves many different tasks. A number of these tasks require heavy physical work. In both rural and urban areas women and girls have to fetch water. In rural areas women and girls carry heavy headloads of firewood. Tasks like washing clothes, cleaning, sweeping and picking up loads require bending, lifting and carrying. Many chores, like cooking, involve standing for long hours in front of hot stoves.The work women do is strenuous and physically demanding.

questions.question: How many women that are working in India are engaged in agricultural work?
questions.explanation: 83.6 per cent of working women in India are engaged in agricultural work. Their work includes planting, weeding, harvesting and threshing. Yet, when we think of a farmer we only think of a man.

questions.question: Many people believe that women are better nurses because_________.

options.html: They are more patient and gentle
They are more aware about the problem of patient
Men do not prefer to do the job of nurses

questions.explanation: A lot of the children in Rosie Ma’am’s class drew women as nurses and men as army officers. The reason they did this is because they feel that outside the home too, women are good at only certain jobs. For example, many people believe that women make better nurses because they are more patient and gentle. This is linked to women’s roles within the family. Similarly, it is believed that science requires a technical mind and girls and women are not capable of dealing with technical things.

questions.question: In the ___ century, many new ideas about education and learning emerged.
questions.explanation: Ramabai (1858–1922), championed the cause of women’s education. She never went to school but learnt to read and write from her parents. In the nineteenth century, many new ideas about education and learning emerged. Schools became more common and communities that had never learnt reading and writing started sending their children to school. But there was a lot of opposition to educating girls even then. Yet many women and men made efforts to open schools for girls. Women struggled to learn to read and write.

questions.question: Rashsundari Devi was born in which state?
questions.explanation: Rashsundari Devi (1800–1890), who was born in West Bengal, some 200 years ago. At the age of 60, she wrote her autobiography in Bangla. Her book titled Amar Jiban is the first known autobiography written by an Indian woman. She taught herself how to read and write in secret, well after her marriage.

questions.question: The Book Amar Jiban is written in which language?
questions.explanation: Rashsundari Devi (1800–1890), who was born in West Bengal, some 200 years ago. At the age of 60, she wrote her autobiography Amar Jiban in Bangla. Her book is the first known autobiography written by an Indian woman.

questions.question: Rokeya Sakhawat Hossain was born in-
questions.explanation: Rokeya Sakhawat Hossain was born into a rich family who owned a lot of land. Though she knew Urdu, she was stopped from learning Bangla and English. Rokeya learnt to read and write Bangla and English with the support of her elder brother. She went on to become a writer. She wrote a remarkable story titled Sultana’s Dream in 1905 to practise her English skills when she was merely 25 years old. This story imagined a woman called Sultana who reaches a place called Ladyland.

questions.question: Rokeya Sakhawat was stopped from learning_____and__________.
questions.explanation: Rokeya Sakhawat Hossain was born into a rich family who owned a lot of land. Though she knew how to read and write Urdu, she was stopped from learning Bangla and English. In those days, English was seen as a language that would expose girls to new ideas, which people thought were not correct for them. Rokeya learnt to read and write Bangla and English with the support of her elder brother.She wrote a remarkable story titled Sultana’s Dream in 1905 to practise her English skills. This story imagined a woman called Sultana who reaches a place called Ladyland.

questions.question: Rokeya wrote a remarkable story titled Sultana's Dream in ______.
questions.explanation: Rokeya Sakhawat Hossain was born in a rich family who owned a lot of land. Though she knew how to read and write Urdu, she was stopped from learning Bangla and English.Rokeya learnt to read and write Bangla and English with the support of her elder brother. She wrote a remarkable story titled Sultana’s Dream in 1905 to practise her English skills when she was merely 25 years old. This story imagined a woman called Sultana who reaches a place called Ladyland.

questions.question: Chaitanya Bhagabat is about the_____________.
questions.explanation: After learning the alphabet, Rashsundari Devi was able to read the Chaitanya Bhagabat, (the life of a saint).

questions.question: The word Adivasi, official term is-
questions.explanation: Scheduled Caste (SC) is the official term for Dalit, and Scheduled Tribe (ST) is the official term for Adivasi.

questions.question: International Women's Day is celebrated on-
questions.explanation: International Women's Day is celebrated on March 8 every year. It commemorates the movement for women's rights.While the first observance of a Women's Day was held on February 28, 1909 in New York, March 8 was suggested by the 1910 International Woman's Conference to become an "International Woman's Day." The day was then predominantly celebrated by the socialist movement and communist countries until it was adopted in 1975 by the United Nations.

questions.question: Which method was adopted by the women's movement to fight for the women's right?
questions.explanation: Campaigns to fight discrimination and violence against women are an important part of the women’s movement. Campaigns have also led to new laws being passed. A law was passed in 2006 to give women who face physical and mental violence within their homes, also called domestic violence, some legal protection.

questions.question: Every year, on 14 August, several people gather at Wagah on the border of _____ and______.
questions.explanation: Wagah is a village situated in Lahore District of Pakistan and serves as a goods transit terminal and a railway station between Pakistan and India, and lies on the old Grand Trunk Road between Lahore, Punjab, Pakistan and Amritsar India.Every year, on 14 August, several thousand people gather at Wagah on the border of India and Pakistan and hold a cultural programme to demonstrate the solidarity between the people of India and Pakistan.

questions.question: Rokeya learnt to read and write Bangla and English with the support of her______________.
questions.explanation: Rokeya Sakhawat Hossain was born into a rich family who owned a lot of land. Though she knew how to read and write Urdu, she was stopped from learning Bangla and English. In those days, English was seen as a language that would expose girls to new ideas, which people thought were not correct for them. Therefore, it was mostly boys who were taught English. Rokeya learnt to read and write Bangla and English with the support of her elder brother and an elder sister

questions.question: Our belief  that people belonging to particular groups based on religion, wealth, language, we create a____.
questions.explanation: When we believe that people belonging to particular groups based on religion, wealth, language are bound to have certain fixed characteristics or can only do a certain type of work, we create a stereotype. For example, we saw how boys and girls are made to take certain subjects not because he or she has an aptitude for it, but because they are either boys or girls. Stereotypes prevent us from looking at people as unique individuals.

questions.question: When we do not treat people equally we create _________.
questions.explanation: When we do not treat people equally or with respect we are indulging in discrimination. It happens when people or organisations act on their prejudices. Discrimination usually takes place when we treat some one differently or make a distinction.

questions.question: When someone forcefully breaks the law or a rule or openly shows disrespect, we can say that he or she has committed a
questions.explanation: The women’s movement raises its voice when violations violations against women take place or for example, when a law or policy acts against their interests. Public rallies and demonstrations are a very powerful way of drawing attention to injustices.When someone forcefully breaks the law or a rule or openly shows disrespect, we can say that he or she has committed a violation

questions.explanation: Sexual harassment refers to physical or verbal behaviour that is of a sexual nature and against the wishes of a woman.Efforts made by the women’s movement led the Supreme Court to formulate guidelines in 1997 to protect women against sexual harassment at the workplace and within educational institutions.

questions.question: Indian woman who wrote first autobiography?
questions.explanation: Rashsundari Devi (1800–1890), who was born in West Bengal, some 200 years ago. At the age of 60, she wrote her autobiography in Bangla. Her book titled Amar Jiban is the first known autobiography written by an Indian woman. Rashsundari Devi was a housewife from a rich landlord’s family. At that time, it was believed that if a woman learnt to read and write, she would bring bad luck to her husband and become a widow! Despite this, she taught herself how to read and write in secret, well after her marriage.

questions.question: 1800-1890 birth year and death year of________.

At that time, it was believed that if a woman learnt to read and write, she would bring bad luck to her husband and become a widow! Despite this, she taught herself how to read and write in secret, well after her marriage.

questions.question: In the _______ , according to research reports on Samoan society, children did not go to school. They learnt many things, such as how to take care of children or do household work from older children and from adults

questions.question: If a boy has put in a lot of effort into making a special birthday gift for his friend and this friend does not say anything about this, then the boy may feel______________.
questions.explanation: When someone is not given due recognition for a task or job they have done, they can feel de-valued. For example, if a boy has put in a lot of effort into making a special birthday gift for his friend and this friend does not say anything about this, then the boy may feel de-valued.

questions.question: A person can be a girl, a sister and a musician is an example of_________.
questions.explanation: Identity is a sense of self-awareness of who one is. Typically, a person can have several identities. For example, a person can be a girl, a sister and a musician.

questions.question: Which of the following statement is False

options.html: The work that women do is less valued than that of men
Woman who stay at home do not work
Our society girls are discriminated in day to day life
They way children grow in Madhya Pradesh and in Samoa is not same

questions.explanation: Women stay at home performs many tasks.These task includes washing clothes, cleaning, sweeping and picking up loads require bending, lifting and carrying. Many chores, like cooking, involve standing for long hours in front of hot stoves

questions.question: Following points show the preference is given to boys as compared to girls except

options.html: The boys are considered to be bread earnings
The birth of a girl is considered more important and is regarded as auspicious
Boys are given more opportunities as given to boys
The female child is considered liability of the family

questions.explanation: The birth of a boy is considered more important and is regarded as auspicious

questions.question: What did girls learn after the age of 14 years?
questions.explanation: Girls had to continue looking after small children or do errands for adults till they were teenagers. But, once they became teenagers they had much more freedom. After the age of fourteen or so, girls also went on fishing trips, worked in the plantations, learnt how to weave baskets. Cooking was done in special cooking-houses, where boys were supposed to do most of the work while girls helped with the preparations.

questions.question: What can you say about the work of men and women?

options.html: The work of women should be valued more than that of men
Both of their works should not be valued at all
Men's work should be valued more than women's

questions.explanation: In most societies, including our own, the roles men and women play or the work they do, are not valued equally. Men and women do not have the same status. The roles women play and the work they do are usually valued less than the roles men play and the work they do. This should not be encouraged. Both their work should be equally valued.

questions.question: The provision of ___ helps many women to take up employment outside the home.
questions.explanation: The government has passed laws that make it mandatory for organisations that have more than 30 women employees to provide crèche facilities. The provision of crèches helps many women to take up employment outside the home. It also makes it possible for more girls to attend schools.

questions.question: Women work in agricultural sector includes________________.
questions.explanation: 83.6 per cent of working women in India are engaged in agricultural work. Their work includes planting, weeding, harvesting and threshing. Yet, when we think of a farmer we only think of a man.

questions.question: Ramabai went on to set up a Mission in Khedgaon near Pune in ____, where widows and poor women were encouraged not only to become literate but to be independent.
questions.explanation: Ramabai (1858–1922), championed the cause of women’s education. She never went to school but learnt to read and write from her parents. She was given the title ‘Pandita’ because she could read and write Sanskrit, a remarkable achievement as women then were not allowed such knowledge. She went on to set up a Mission in Khedgaon near Pune in 1898, where widows and poor women were encouraged not only to become literate but to be independent. They were taught a variety of skills from carpentry to running a printing press, skills that are not usually taught to girls even today.

questions.question: Ramabai was given the title "Pandita" because she could read and write _____ , a remarkable achievement as women then were not allowed such knowledge.
questions.explanation: Ramabai (1858–1922), championed the cause of women’s education. She never went to school but learnt to read and write from her parents. She was given the title ‘Pandita’ because she could read and write Sanskrit, a remarkable achievement as women then were not allowed such knowledge. She went on to set up a Mission in Khedgaon near Pune in 1898, where widows and poor women were encouraged not only to become literate but to be independent.

questions.question: Book titled Amar Jiban is written by________________.
questions.explanation: Rashsundari Devi (1800–1890), who was born in West Bengal, some 200 years ago. At the age of 60, she wrote her autobiography in Bangla. Her book titled Amar Jiban is the first known autobiography written by an Indian woman.

questions.question: Earlier it was believed that if a woman learnt to read and write ________.

options.html: she would bring bad luck to her children
she would bring bad luck to her family
she would bring bad luck to her society
she would bring bad luck to her husband and become a widow

questions.explanation: Rashsundari Devi (1800–1890), who was born in West Bengal, some 200 years ago. At the age of 60, she wrote her autobiography in Bangla. Her book titled Amar Jiban is the first known autobiography written by an Indian woman. Rashsundari Devi was a housewife from a rich landlord’s family. At that time, it was believed that if a woman learnt to read and write, she would bring bad luck to her husband and become a widow! Despite this, she taught herself how to read and write in secret, well after her marriage.

questions.question: According to Rokeya Sakhawat lady land is a place where ________.

options.html: Lady have freedom to study and work

questions.explanation: Rokeya Sakhawat Hossain was born into a rich family. Rokeya learnt to read and write Bangla and English with the support of her elder brother and an elder sister. She wrote a remarkable story titled Sultana’s Dream in 1905 to practise her English skills when she was merely 25 years old. This story imagined a woman called Sultana who reaches a place called Ladyland. Ladyland is a place where women had the freedom to study, work, and create inventions like controlling rain from the clouds and flying air cars. In this Ladyland, the men had been sent into seclusion – their aggressive guns and other weapons of war defeated by the brain-power of women.

questions.question: According to the 1961 census, about ___per cent of all boys and men and __ per cent of all girls and women.
questions.explanation: According to the 1961 census, about 40 per cent of all boys and men(7 years old and above) were literate (that is, they could at least write their names) compared to just 15 per cent of all girls and women. In the most recent census of 2001, these figures have grown to 76 per cent for boys and men, and 54 per cent for girls and women

questions.question: Census of__ literacy rate have grown to 76 per cent for boys and men, and 54 per cent for girls.
questions.explanation: According to the 1961 census, about 40 per cent of all boys and men(7 years old and above) were literate (that is, they could at least write their names) compared to just 15 per cent of all girls and women. In the most recent census of 2001, these figures have grown to 76 per cent for boys and men, and 54 per cent for girls and women. This means that the proportion of both men and women who are now able to read and have at least some amount of schooling has increased.

questions.question: The 2001 census also found that ____girls are less likely, than ____ girls, to complete primary school
questions.explanation: Girls who are from Dalit and Adivasi backgrounds are less likely to remain in school. The 2001 census also found that Muslim girls are less likely, than Dalit and Adivasi girls, to complete primary school. While a Muslim girl is likely to stay in school for around three years, girls from other communities spend around four years in school.

questions.question: A law was passed in ____ to give women who face physical and mental violence within their homes, also called domestic violence, some legal protection.
questions.explanation: Campaigns to fight discrimination and violence against women are an important part of the women’s movement. Campaigns have also led to new laws being passed. A law was passed in 2006 to give women who face physical and mental violence within their homes, also called domestic violence, some legal protection.

questions.question: An efforts made by the women's movement led the Supreme Court to formulate guidelines in 1997 to protect women against______________.
questions.explanation: Sexual harassment refers to physical or verbal behaviour that is of a sexual nature and against the wishes of a woman.Efforts made by the women’s movement led the Supreme Court to formulate guidelines in 1997 to protect women against sexual harassment harassment harassment at the workplace and within educational institutions.

questions.question: We saw how boys and girls are made to take certain subjects not because he or she has an aptitude for it, but because they are either boys or girls. It is an example of__________.
questions.explanation: When we believe that people belonging to particular groups based on religion, wealth, language are bound to have certain fixed characteristics or can only do a certain type of work, we create a stereotype. For example, in this chapter, we saw how boys and girls are made to take certain subjects not because he or she has an aptitude for it, but because they are either boys or girls. Stereotypes prevent us from looking at people as unique individuals.

questions.question: Ramabai birth year and death year______________.
questions.explanation: Ramabai (1858–1922), championed the cause of women’s education. She never went to school but learnt to read and write from her parents. She was given the title ‘Pandita’ because she could read and write Sanskrit, a remarkable achievement as women then were not allowed such knowledge. She went on to set up a Mission in Khedgaon near Pune in 1898, where widows and poor women were encouraged not only to become literate but to be independent.

questions.question: Look at the figure shown below, why girls like to go to school together in a group

options.html: They also carried fears of being teased or attacked.
Going in a group consider good
They have group activity to perform
They like going in a group

questions.explanation: Every evening, once school was over, the boys watched as hundreds of school girls crowded the narrow streets. As these girls walked on the streets, they looked so purposeful. This was unlike the boys who used the streets as a place to stand around idling, to play, to try out tricks with their bicycles. For the girls, the street was simply a place to get straight home. The girls always went in groups, perhaps because they also carried fears of being teased or attacked.

questions.question: The Samoan Islands are part of a large group of small islands in the southern part of the ________.
questions.explanation: Samoa lies south of the equator, about halfway between Hawaii and New Zealand, in the Polynesian region of the Pacific Ocean.

questions.question:
Match the following
Column I
(a) Care-giving
(b) Double burden
(c) De-valued
Column II
(1) women typically labour both inside the home
(2) When someone is not given due recognition for a task
(3) tasks related to looking after and nurturing
questions.explanation:
1. Care-giving refers to a range of tasks related to looking after and nurturing. Besides physical tasks, they also involve a strong emotional aspect.
  2. Double-burden, Literally means a double load. This term is commonly used to describe the women’s work situation. It has emerged from a recognition that women typically labour both inside the home (housework) and outside.
  3. When someone is not given due recognition for a task or job they have done, they can feel de-valued. For example, if a boy has put in a lot of effort into making a special birthday gift for his friend and this friend does not say anything about this, then the boy may feel de-valued.

questions.question:
Match the following
Column I
(a) Identity
(b) Gender
(c) Sex
Column II
(1) Sense of self-awareness of who one is.
(2) Biological difference
(3) Social difference
questions.explanation:
1. Identity is a sense of self-awareness of who one is. Typically, a person can have several identities. For example, a person can be a girl, a sister and a musician.
  2. By gender, then, we mean the many social values and stereotypes our cultures attach to the biological distinction ‘male’ and ‘female’.It is a term that helps us to understand many of the inequalities and power relations between men and women in society.
  3. The Constitution says that being male or female should not become a reason for discrimination. In reality, inequality between the sexes exists. The government is, therefore, committed to understanding the reasons for this and taking positive steps to remedy the situation

questions.question: Identify the lady :-
questions.explanation: Laxmi Lakra is the first woman engine driver for Northern Railways. Engine drivers are men. But 27-year-old Laxmi Lakra, from a poor tribal family in Jharkhand has begun to change things. Laxmi studied in a government school. Even in school, Laxmi helped with the housework and did odd jobs. She studied hard and did well and then went on to get a diploma in electronics. She then took the railway board exam and passed it on her first attempt.

questions.question:
Match the following
Term
(a) Discrimination
(b) Stereotype
(c) Violation
Meaning
(1) usually takes place when we treat someone differently or make a distinction
(2) prevent us from looking at people as unique individuals
(3) When someone forcefully breaks the law or a rule or openly shows disrespect
questions.explanation:
1. Discrimination: When we do not treat people equally or with respect we are indulging in discrimination. It happens when people or organisations act on their prejudices. Discrimination usually takes place when we treat some one differently or make a distinction.
  2. Stereotype: When we believe that people belonging to particular groups based on religion, wealth, language are bound to have certain fixed characteristics or can only do a certain type of work, we create a stereotype.
  3. Violation: When someone forcefully breaks the law or a rule or openly shows disrespect, we can say that he or she has committed a violation.

questions.question: One word that is often used to collectively refer to the radio, TV, newspapers, the Internet, and several other forms of communication. This word is:
questions.explanation: One word that is often used to collectively refer to the radio, TV, newspapers, the Internet, and several other forms of communication. This word is ‘media’.

questions.question: Media is the plural form of the word:
questions.explanation: Media is the plural form of the word ‘medium’ and it describes the various ways through which we communicate in society.

questions.question: ________ can reach millions of people because they use certain technologies.
questions.explanation: TV, radio, and newspapers are a form of media that reaches millions of people, or the masses, across the country and the world and, thus, they are called mass media.

questions.question: When the government prevents either a news item, or scenes from a movie, or the lyrics of a song from being shared with the larger public, this is referred to as:
questions.explanation: When the government prevents either a news item, or scenes from a movie, or the lyrics of a song from being shared with the larger public, this is referred to as censorship. There have been periods in Indian history when the government censored the media. The worst of these was the Emergency between 1975-1977.

questions.question: ________ refers to news reports, articles, interviews, stories, etc., that are printed in newspapers, magazines, and books for a wide audience to read.
questions.explanation: Publish prepare and issue (a book, journal, or piece of music) for public sale in the newspaper, magazines, or journals.

questions.question: This word is used to refer to a TV or radio programme that is widely transmitted.
questions.explanation: To broadcast a programme means to send it out by radio waves so that it can be heard on the radio or seen on television.

questions.question: When a large number of people come together and openly state their opposition to some issue. Organising a rally, starting a signature campaign, blocking roads, etc. are some of the ways in which this is done:
questions.explanation: A public protest is a means for people to complain in a public way about something they think is wrong and build support to correct it by organising rallies, demonstrations, signature campaigns, blocking roads, etc.

questions.question: Media decides which subject to highlight and hence:
questions.explanation: The media also plays an important role in deciding what stories to focus on, and therefore, decides on what is newsworthy. Due to the significant influence, it plays in our lives and in shaping our thoughts, it is commonly said that the media ‘sets the agenda’.

questions.question:
What is the local media?

  1. Media run by local groups intended to cover local issues of people in remote areas.
  2. Media run by national groups intended to cover local issues of people in remote areas.
  3. Media run by state groups intended to cover local issues of people in remote areas.
  4. Media run by international groups intended to cover local issues of people in remote areas.
questions.explanation: Recognizing that the media will not be interested in covering ‘small’ issues that involve ordinary people and their daily lives, several local groups have come forward to start their own media. This is the local media.

questions.question: Which of the following is not the example of digital media?
questions.explanation: The book is an example of print media.

questions.question: Lighthouse of democracy is ________.
questions.explanation: The media is the lighthouse of democracy because it is the media that aware people about their rights.

questions.question: Objective of media are as follows:

options.html: Highlight the visits of a famous political party
Highlight one side of the aspects

questions.explanation: Business houses control the media. At times, it is in the interest of these businesses to focus on only one side of the story.

questions.question: ________ is a report which discusses all point of view of a particular story.
questions.explanation: A balanced report is one that discusses all points of view of a particular story and then leaves it to the readers to make up their minds. Writing a balanced report, however, depends on the media being independent.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of print media?
questions.explanation: Newspapers, Magazines, and other materials that are physically printed on paper are examples of print media.

questions.question: ________ is all about building brands.
questions.explanation: Advertising brand is all about building brands.This is done in order to differentiate it from other products in the market.

questions.question: ________ means stamping a product with a particular name or sign.
questions.explanation: Advertising brand is all about building brands. At a very basic level, ‘branding’ means stamping a product with a particular name or sign.

questions.question: A person who collects and writes new stories for print or electronic media?
questions.explanation: A journalist is a person who writes for newspapers, magazines, or news websites or prepares news to be broadcast.

questions.question: Branding actually came from ________.
questions.explanation: Branding actually came from cattle grazing. Cattle of different owners grazed together in ranches and they often got mixed up. The owners thought of a solution. They started marking their cattle with the owner’s sign by using a heated iron. This was called ‘branding’.

questions.question: A popular model may charge ________ lakh or more per advertisement.
questions.explanation: Celebrities promote the products and charge a huge amount. A popular model may charge ₹ 5 lakh or more per advertisement.

questions.question: ________ refers to advertisements made by the State or private agencies that have a larger message for society.
questions.explanation: Social advertisements refer to advertisements made by the government or private agencies that have a larger message for society.

questions.question: ________ is the principal source of political information and public debate.
questions.explanation: Media is the principal source of political information and public debate.

questions.question: ________ refers to a thing or service that has been made for being sold in the market.
questions.explanation: Product: This refers to a thing or service that has been made for being sold in the market.

questions.question: Various social agencies, social services institutions take the responsibility of ________.
questions.explanation: Social advertisements refer to advertisements made by the government or private agencies that have a larger message for society.

questions.question: ________ refers to the person for whom the goods or products have been made and who pays money to buy and use them.
questions.explanation: A consumer is a person who purchases goods and services for personal use.

questions.question: ________ refers to a special identification or name that is associated with a product. Such identification is created through the process of advertising.
questions.explanation: At a very basic level, ‘branding’ means stamping a product with a particular name or sign. This is done in order to differentiate it from other products in the market

questions.question: The promotion of the saleable goods, services, and ideas by the reputed sponsor is termed as:
questions.explanation: Advertising is a means of communication with the users of a product or service. The promotion of the saleable goods, services, and ideas by the reputed sponsor is termed as an advertisement.

questions.question: This refers to the power to change what someone believes or does.
questions.explanation: To influence: This refers to the power to change what someone believes or does.

questions.question: ________ refers to people's lives being identified by the products they own, the clothes they wear, the places they eat in, etc.
questions.explanation: Lifestyle: In this chapter, this word refers to people’s lives being identified by the products they own, the clothes they wear, the places they eat in, etc.

questions.question: Advertisement draw our attention towards:
questions.explanation: Advertisements draw our attention to various products and describe them positively so that we become interested in buying them. Also Advertising brand is all about building brand and its value.

questions.question: Which of the following is not an example of social advertisement?

options.html: Use of Sunsilk for your hair

questions.explanation: It is a commercial advertisement.

tests.instructionHtml:

questions.question: ________ draws our attention to various product and describe them positively so that we become interested in buying them.
questions.explanation: Advertisements draw our attention to various products and describe them positively so that we become interested in buying them.

questions.question: What is the telecast rate for a 30 seconds advertisement on a major TV channel?
questions.explanation: The telecast rate for a 30 second advertisement on a major TV channel is ₹ 1.65 lakh.

questions.question: How brand values are conveyed to consumers?

options.html: Use of visual and words give an overall image
Use of visual and picture give an overall image
Use of signs and symbols give an overall image
Use of audio and words give an overall image

questions.explanation: Values such as treating our guests well and making sure our children get nutritious food are used by brands to create brand values. These brand values are conveyed through the use of visuals and words to give us an overall image that appeals to us.

questions.question: Who keeps a watch on the print media in India?
questions.explanation: The Press Council of India is a statutory body in India that governs the conduct of the print media.

questions.question: With electronic typewriters, journalism underwent a sea-change in the ________.
questions.explanation: With electronic typewriters, journalism underwent a sea-change in the 1940s.

questions.question: Most of the cartoons that you see on television are mostly from:
questions.explanation: Television images travel huge distances through satellites and cables. This allows us to view news and entertainment channels from other parts of the world. Most of the cartoons that you see on television are mostly from Japan or the United States.

questions.question: Coast of Florida is in the ________.
questions.explanation: The Coast of Florida is in the United States.

questions.question: Who invented television?
questions.explanation: John L. Baird sat in front of the apparatus with which he demonstrated to the Royal Institute, his invention, the ‘televisor’, an early television.

questions.question: The cost to advertise on a news channel varies from ________ per 10 seconds depending on the popularity of the channel.
questions.explanation: The cost to advertise on a news channel varies from ₹ 500 to ₹ 8,000 per 10 seconds depending on the popularity of the channel.

questions.question: There have been periods in Indian history when the government censored the media. The worst of these was the Emergency between ________.
questions.explanation: When the government prevents either a news item, or scenes from a movie, or the lyrics of a song from being shared with the larger public, this is referred to as censorship. There have been periods in Indian history when the government censored the media. The worst of these was the Emergency between 1975-1977.

questions.question: In the following ways, media play an important role in democracy except:

options.html: The public does not become aware of the fraudulent practices
Media helps in forming an opinion of the masses
Medias information citizen can learn how the government work
Media also announces the opinion of the public about certain issues, problem

questions.explanation: The public becomes aware of fraudulent practices.

questions.question: ________ is a letter or petition signed by the many people to show support for a cause or an action.
questions.explanation: A signature campaign is a process of gathering enough signatures in a letter or petition in order to support a cause or an action.

questions.question: Government body responding for developing, regulating, and sending out information:
questions.explanation: The Ministry of Information and Broadcasting is a branch of the Government of India which is an apex body for formulation and administration of the rules and regulations and laws relating to information, broadcasting, the press, and films in India.

questions.question: Why are most newspapers not able to provide a balanced story before readers?

options.html: Most media are controlled by big business houses
Most media are controlled by writers
Most media want to cover only a short story
Most media want to save time

questions.explanation: At times, it is in the interest of these businesses to focus on only one side of the story. Media’s continual need for money and its links to advertising means that it becomes difficult for media to be reporting against people who give them advertisements. Media is, thus, no longer considered independent because of its close links to business.

questions.question: The credibility of the media can be maintained in the following ways except:

options.html: It does not make money from string operation
Media should not pronounce people guilty or innocence

questions.explanation: The media must resort to ethical means.

questions.question: How do media decide upon which events need to be published?

options.html: Events which are against the politician
Events which take less time to cover

questions.explanation: The media tends to focus on a particular aspect of a story because they believe this makes the story interesting.

questions.question: A journalist should be able to operate with

options.html: freedom to act with one's consciousness and professional judgment

questions.explanation:
A journalist must have the following skillsets:

  1. He must act in a fair and just manner and must have the freedom to act.
  2. He must use his professional judgment and consciousness.

questions.question: This is done in order to differentiate one product from other products in the market.
questions.explanation: Branding means stamping a product with a particular name or sign. This is done in order to differentiate it from other products in the market.

questions.question: Cattle of different owners grazed together in ranches and they often got mixed up. The owners thought of a solution. They started marking their cattle with the owner's sign by using a heated iron. This was called ________.
questions.explanation: Branding actually came from cattle grazing. Cattle of different owners grazed together in ranches and they often got mixed up. The owners thought of a solution. They started marking their cattle with the owner’s sign by using a heated iron. This was called ‘branding’.

questions.question: When a company takes masoor ki daal and puts it into a packet they decide on a name like Top Taste Daal. It appeals to

options.html: our concern for our children's education
our modern tradition of not treating guests well
our concern for our children's health
our social tradition of treating guests extremely well

questions.explanation: ‘Top Taste Daal’ is appealing to our social tradition of treating guests extremely well. ‘Best Taste Daal’ is appealing to our concern for our children’s health and that they eat things that are good for them.

questions.question: When was the Right to Information Act passed?
questions.explanation: The RTI Bill was passed by the Parliament of India on 15 June 2005 and came into force on 12 October 2005.

questions.question: Branded daals cost much more than daals that are sold loose because they include the costs of
questions.explanation: Branded daals cost much more than daals that are sold loose because they include the costs of packaging and advertising. So, many people cannot afford them.

questions.question: In reality, there is little difference between daals that are sold loose and those sold in a packet. We are just made to imagine the difference because of the ________.
questions.explanation: People will come to believe that only branded daals are good and will want to buy the daal that comes in a sealed packet rather than that which is sold loose. But, in reality, there is little difference between daals that are sold loose and those sold in a packet. We are just made to imagine the difference because of the advertisement.

questions.question: Celebrities promoting products. It was recently reported that a top cricketer signed a three-year contract to do various advertisements for ________ crores.
questions.explanation: Celebrities promoting products. It was recently reported that a top cricketer signed a three-year contract to do various advertisements for ₹ 180 crores. A popular model may charge ₹ 5 lakh or more per advertisement.

questions.question: The telecast rate for a ________ advertisement on a major TV channel is 1.65 lakh.
questions.explanation: The telecast rate for a 30-second advertisement on a major TV channel is ₹ 1.65 lakh.

questions.question: In a democracy in which all people are equal and should be able to lead a life of dignity, advertising tends to promote a certain lack of respect for the ________.
questions.explanation: In a democracy in which all people are equal and should be able to lead a life of dignity, advertising tends to promote a certain lack of respect for the poor. They are not the faces we most often see in advertisements and so we tend to dismiss their lives as worthless.

questions.question: Who is confused because it really cannot be differentiated between top taste items and best taste items?
questions.explanation: The consumer is consumer confused because they really cannot tell the difference between ‘Top Products’ and ‘Best Best Product'. The manufacturer has to give the consumer a reason to prefer a particular brand of product.

questions.question: Branded things are ________ than the non-branded things.
questions.explanation: Non branded things are comparatively cheaper than Branded things. Branded products are costly.

questions.question: The aim of the advertisements is to

options.html: increase the quantity of the product
increase the quality of the product
increase the cost of the product
persuade a customer to buy the product

questions.explanation: The purpose of advertising is to inform the consumers about their product and convince customers that a company's services or products are the best, enhance the image of the company.

questions.question: When did the advertisement see the most scientific research?
questions.explanation: Advertising and marketing have experienced some major milestones. Advertisements have seen scientific research in 1960.

questions.question: Which things are employed in advertising?
questions.explanation: Every business spends a lot of money on advertising its products. Many types of techniques and media are used in advertising.

questions.question: ________ advertising is intended to promote the sale of an advertised product by appealing directly to the buyer.
questions.explanation: Commercial advertising usually involves selling a product or service. Usually, the goal in mind here is to make more money by appealing buyers.

questions.question: Following are the basic requirements of good advertising except:

options.html: It should not be against Religion
Information about the product should be provided accurately
It should be well designed in conformity with the law

questions.explanation: It should not be against morality. It must be made in compliance with ethics.

questions.question: Identify the following figure
questions.explanation: The given figure is of the typical typewriter.

questions.question: Following points show the relationship between media and big business houses except:

options.html: Media raise voice against businessman fraudulent activities
Media helps in an advertisement for products of big business houses
Media helps business houses to know about the detailed programmes of government
Media gets business through big business houses

questions.explanation: Media is, thus, no longer considered independent because of its close links to business. Media doesn't raise voice against businessman fraudulent activities.

questions.question: Look at the collage identify what kind of media is seen
questions.explanation: The given figure is of the press conference where the an interview  is given to journalists by a prominent person in order to make an announcement or answer questions.

questions.question: When a company takes red lentil and puts it into a packet they decide on a name like Top Taste Daal. This naming of the product is called:
questions.explanation: Daals or pulses are usually sold loose in the market. We usually know daals by their different types like masoor ki daal, urad ki daal, etc. These names are not brand names. When a company takes masoor ki daal and puts it into a packet, it will need to give the daal a special name. It needs to do this so that we don’t confuse the daal in that particular packet with the daal that is sold loose. They decide on a name like ‘Top Taste Daal’. This naming of the product is called ‘branding’.

questions.question: When a company takes masoor ki daal and puts it into a packet they decide on a name like Best Taste Daal is appealing

options.html: our social tradition of treating guests extremely well
our modern tradition of not treating guests well
our concern for our children's education
our concern for our children's health

questions.explanation: ‘Best Taste Daal’ is appealing to our concern for our children’s health and that they eat things that are good for them.

questions.question: The cost of bringing out a quarter-page color advertisement in a leading newspaper.
questions.explanation: Producing and showing advertisements in the media is very expensive. The cost of bringing out a quarter-page colour advertisement in a leading newspaper is ₹ 8.36 lakh.

questions.question: Identify which type of advertisement is shown below
questions.explanation: This is a social advertisement regarding the crossing of unmanned railway level crossing. Social advertisements refer to advertisements made by the government or private agencies that have a larger message for society.

questions.question: Changing technology, or machines, and making technology more modern, helps ________ to reach more people.
questions.explanation: Changing technology, or machines, and making technology more modern, helps media to reach more people. It also improves the quality of sound and the images.

questions.question:
Arrange the following points in series how the advertisement get made

  1. A company decides to attract the attention of the customer
  2. A campaign is visualised that appeal to specific customers
  3. A consumer profile is determined
  4. A brand identity has created that appeal to the target audience for the job
  5. Market professional is appointed for the job
  6. Last market strategy and various media are selected through which advertisement will be done
questions.explanation:
In the following series, the advertisement gets made

  1. A company first decides to attract the attention of the customer
  2. For that a consumer profile is determined
  3. After the determination of a consumer profile, a campaign has visualized that appeal to specific customers
  4. Market professional is appointed for the job to reach the customers
  5. A brand identity is created that appeals to the target audience for the job
  6. Last market strategy and various media are selected through which advertisement will be done

questions.question: Identify the type of advertisement below
questions.explanation: The given is the Social advertisement regarding educational rights of disabled children

questions.question: Why were the textiles of Surat so famous?
questions.explanation: The textiles of Surat were famous for their gold lace borders (zari) and had a market in West Asia, Africa and Europe.

questions.question:
Match the following:

Company Name Advertisement Tag -line
(a) Saffola (i) Because the city never sleeps
(b) Garnier (ii) Take care
(c) Citi Bank (iii) Abhi to Main Jawan Hoon
questions.explanation:
Companies use different attractive and creative taglines for their product in order to appeal to the buyers. Eg :

  1. Saffola use Abhi to Main Jawan Hoon
  2. Garnier use Take Care
  3. Citi Bank use Because the city never sleeps

questions.question: A weekly market is so called because it is held on a specific
questions.explanation: A weekly market is so called because it is held on a specific day of the week. Weekly markets do not have permanent shops.

questions.question: Who finally sell goods to consumer
questions.explanation: The people in between the producer and the final consumer are the traders. The wholesale trader first buys goods in large quantities. For example, the vegetable wholesale trader will not buy a few kilos of vegetables, but will buy in large lots of 25 to 100 kilos. These will then be sold to other traders. In these markets, buying and selling takes place between traders. It is through these links of traders that goods reach faraway places. The trader who finally sells this to the consumer, is the retailer.

questions.question: Which type of markets do not have permanent shops?
questions.explanation: Weekly markets do not have permanent shops. Traders set up shops for the day and then close them up in the evening. Then they may set up at a different place the next day.

questions.question: Which of the following things will not find in a weekly market
questions.explanation: Branded clothes are sold in shopping Malls.

questions.question: Collecting ____ item has become status symbol
questions.explanation: Branded goods are expensive, often promoted by advertising and claims of better quality and are used as status symbol.

questions.question: Who among the following sell goods at higher price than other in the chain market
questions.explanation: The trader who buys good from the wholesaler and sells it to the consumer at a higher price and earns profit.

questions.question: Who are seller in the weekly market
questions.explanation: Weekly markets do not have permanent shops. Small traders set up shops for the day and then close them up in the evening. Then they may set up at a different place the next day

questions.question: Who is Swapna
questions.explanation: Swapna is a small farmer in Kurnool (Andhra Pradesh) grows cotton on her small piece of land

questions.question: Kurnool is in
questions.explanation: Kurnool is a beautiful city located in Indian state of Andhra Pradesh.

questions.question: Ginning mill buys the
questions.explanation: Trader sells cotton to the Ginning Mill

questions.question: ______  spins the cotton into yarn
questions.explanation: Spinning mill buys the cotton,spins it and sells yarn to the yarn dealer.

questions.question: Who buys the bales
questions.explanation:
Ginning mill removes the seeds and presses
the cotton into bales.Spinning mill buys the bales.

questions.question: ________ is a system where the merchants distribute work among the weavers according to the orders they have received.
questions.explanation: Putting-out-system

questions.question: Erode market is situated in
questions.explanation: Erode is  bi-weekly cloth market in Tamil Nadu is one of the largest cloth markets in the world

questions.question: Ginning Mill

options.html: removes the seeds and presses the cotton into bales
removes the seeds and presses the jute into bales
removes the seeds and presses the silk into bales
removes the seeds and presses the nylon into bales

questions.explanation:
Ginning mill buys the cotton.It removes
the seeds and presses the cotton into bales.

questions.question: Following are the types of market except
questions.explanation: There are many kinds of markets: these can include shops, hawker’s stalls in our neighbourhood, a weekly market, a large shopping complex or a mall

questions.question: How many workers work in Impex  garment factory
questions.explanation: The Impex garment factory has 70 workers. Most of them are women

questions.question: Shops in the ____ are of a permanent nature and offer a wide variety of goods and services and product of daily usage
questions.explanation: Neighbourhood shops sells goods and services in our neighbourhood.They sell all kind of items and services of daily purposes, like dairy,  groceries, stationery, medicines, hair saloon etc.They can be permanent shops and some are roadside stalls like vegetable  hawkers, fruit vendor, the mechanic etc.

questions.question: The final product reaches the buyers through a
questions.explanation: A chain of markets is formed by the series of exchange of goods from the producer to different traders till they reach the final consumers.

questions.question: On looms yarn is woven into
questions.explanation: Weavers invest all their savings or borrow money at high interest rates to buy looms.On looms yarn is woven into clothes.

questions.question: If the weavers were to buy yarn on their own and sell cloth, they would probably earn
questions.explanation: It is possible to earn three times more money when weavers buy yarn on their own because if weavers buy yarn in bulk their buying cost would be less and when they sell cloth they can earn profit at double price or more,because they do all the work on their own.

questions.question: There exist a chain of ______ at each level from the production of goods till final sale
questions.explanation: A chain of markets links the producer of cotton to the buyer at the supermarket. Buying and selling takes place at every step in the chain

questions.question: The arrangement between the merchant and the weavers is an example of
questions.explanation: In putting out arrangment ,The merchant distributes work among the weavers based on the orders he has received for cloth. The weavers get the yarn from the merchant and supply him the cloth.

questions.question: In a______, people with common interests come together and work for their mutual benefit.
questions.explanation: In a cooperative, people with common interests come together and work for their mutual benefit. In a weaver’s cooperative, the weavers form a group and take up certain activities collectively.

questions.question: ______ Government runs a Free School Uniform programme in the state. The government procures the cloth for this programme from the powerloom weaver?s cooperatives.
questions.explanation: The Tamil Nadu government runs a Free School Uniform programme in the state. The government procures the cloth for this programme from the powerloom weaver’s cooperatives. Similarly, the government buys cloth from the handloom weaver’s cooperatives and sells it through stores known as Co-optex.

questions.question: Who gain by exploiting the craftsmen and pay lower prices for their goods and services
questions.explanation: Intermediary a person who acts as a link between people in order to try and bring about an agreement.Intermediaries  gain by exploiting the craftsmen and pay lower prices for their goods and services

questions.question: The story of shirt begins with ____ and ends with _____

options.html: Production of cotton, Sale of shirt
Production of cotton, Sale of bale
Production of yarn, Sale of shirt
Production of bale, Sale of cotton

questions.explanation: The story of a shirt begins with the production of cotton and ends with the sale of the shirt.

questions.question: Seller of weekly market earn ___ than the seller of the mall
questions.explanation: The weekly market trader earns little compared to the profit of a regular shop owner in a shopping complex.

questions.question: Exporters are those

options.html: Who purchase goods within domestic territory
Who sell goods within domestic territory

questions.explanation: Exporter means a person, country, or company that sends goods or services to another country for sale.

questions.question: Order of Chain of marketing
questions.explanation:
A series of markets that are connected like links in a chain because products pass from one market to another.

PRODUCERS

Producer is a person, company, or country that makes, grows, or supplies goods or commodities for sale

WHOLESALE TRADER
A person who buys products in bulk from factory and may store them in godown, and sell it to other traders.
AGENTS:
The people in between the producer and the final consumer are the traders.
RETAILER
The trader who buys good from the wholesaler and sells it to the consumer.All neighbourhood shops are retailers.
